1→# Roko Implementation Status
     2→
     3→> **Last updated**: 2026-04-12
     4→>
     5→> Single source of truth for what's implemented vs. specified across the Roko system.
     6→> For naming conventions, see [`00-architecture/01-naming-and-glossary.md`](00-architecture/01-naming-and-glossary.md).
     7→> For the crate map, see [`00-architecture/15-crate-map.md`](00-architecture/15-crate-map.md).
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Status Tiers
    12→
    13→| Tier | Meaning |
    14→|------|---------|
    15→| **Shipping** | End-to-end wired, tested, used in self-hosting workflow. CLI-accessible. |
    16→| **Built** | Code exists, compiles, has tests — but not yet called from the runtime or CLI. |
    17→| **Scaffold** | Struct/trait stubs exist. No meaningful implementation. |
    18→| **Specified** | Described in PRD docs only. No code. |
    19→| **Deferred** | Intentionally postponed (Phase 2+, chain-dependent, or research-only). |
    20→
    21→---
    22→
    23→## Master Status Matrix
    24→
    25→| # | Section | Tier | Primary Crate(s) | Status Doc |
    26→|---|---------|------|-------------------|------------|
    27→| 00 | [Architecture](00-architecture/INDEX.md) | **Shipping** | `roko-core` (376 tests) | — |
    28→| 01 | [Orchestration](01-orchestration/INDEX.md) | **Shipping** | `roko-orchestrator` (158 tests), `roko-cli` | — |
    29→| 02 | [Agents](02-agents/INDEX.md) | **Shipping** | `roko-agent` (346 tests) | [15-status-gaps.md](02-agents/15-status-gaps.md) |
    30→| 03 | [Composition](03-composition/INDEX.md) | **Shipping** | `roko-compose` (23 tests) | [13-current-status-and-gaps.md](03-composition/13-current-status-and-gaps.md) |
    31→| 04 | [Verification](04-verification/INDEX.md) | **Shipping** | `roko-gate` (200 tests), `roko-fs` (37 tests) | — |
    32→| 05 | [Learning](05-learning/INDEX.md) | **Shipping** | `roko-learn` (101 tests) | — |
    33→| 06 | [Neuro](06-neuro/INDEX.md) | **Built** | `roko-neuro` | [16-current-status-and-gaps.md](06-neuro/16-current-status-and-gaps.md) |
    34→| 07 | [Conductor](07-conductor/INDEX.md) | **Built** | `roko-conductor` | — |
    35→| 08 | [Chain](08-chain/INDEX.md) | **Built** | `roko-chain` (52 tests) | — |
    36→| 09 | [Daimon](09-daimon/INDEX.md) | **Built** | `roko-daimon` | [13-current-status-and-gaps.md](09-daimon/13-current-status-and-gaps.md) |
    37→| 10 | [Dreams](10-dreams/INDEX.md) | **Scaffold** | `roko-dreams` | [16-implementation-status.md](10-dreams/16-implementation-status.md) |
    38→| 11 | [Safety](11-safety/INDEX.md) | **Shipping** (core) / **Specified** (advanced) | `roko-agent` (safety layer) | — |
    39→| 12 | [Interfaces](12-interfaces/INDEX.md) | **Scaffold** | `roko-cli` (text dashboard) | — |
    40→| 13 | [Coordination](13-coordination/INDEX.md) | **Specified** | — | [12-current-status-and-gaps.md](13-coordination/12-current-status-and-gaps.md) |
    41→| 14 | [Identity & Economy](14-identity-economy/INDEX.md) | **Deferred** | — | — |
    42→| 15 | [Code Intelligence](15-code-intelligence/INDEX.md) | **Built** | `roko-index`, `roko-lang-*` | [10-current-status-and-gaps.md](15-code-intelligence/10-current-status-and-gaps.md) |
    43→| 16 | [Heartbeat](16-heartbeat/INDEX.md) | **Specified** | — | — |
    44→| 17 | [Lifecycle](17-lifecycle/INDEX.md) | **Specified** | — | — |
    45→| 18 | [Tools](18-tools/INDEX.md) | **Shipping** (builtins) / **Scaffold** (MCP servers) | `roko-std` (96 tests) | — |
    46→| 19 | [Deployment](19-deployment/INDEX.md) | **Specified** | — | — |
    47→| 20 | [Technical Analysis](20-technical-analysis/INDEX.md) | **Specified** | — | — |
    48→| 21 | [References](21-references/INDEX.md) | N/A (bibliography) | — | — |
    49→
    50→---
    51→
    52→## Detailed Breakdown
    53→
    54→### Shipping (end-to-end wired, CLI-accessible)
    55→
    56→These components form the working self-hosting loop: `roko prd` → `roko plan run` → gate → persist → resume.
    57→
    58→| Component | Crate | Tests | CLI Command |
    59→|-----------|-------|-------|-------------|
    60→| Signal/Engram type + 6 Synapse traits | `roko-core` | 376 | — (kernel) |
    61→| Plan DAG executor + parallel scheduling | `roko-orchestrator` | 158 | `roko plan run` |
    62→| 5 LLM backends + CascadeRouter + MCP | `roko-agent` | 346 | `roko run` |
    63→| 6-layer SystemPromptBuilder + 9 templates | `roko-compose` | 23 | — (used by orchestrator) |
    64→| 11 gates + 6-rung pipeline + adaptive thresholds | `roko-gate` | 200 | — (used by orchestrator) |
    65→| JSONL FileSubstrate + GC | `roko-fs` | 37 | — (storage layer) |
    66→| Episodes + playbooks + bandits + experiments | `roko-learn` | 101 | — (feedback loops) |
    67→| 19 built-in tools (file, shell, search, MCP) | `roko-std` | 96 | — (tool dispatch) |
    68→| ProcessSupervisor + event bus + cancellation | `roko-runtime` | — | — (infra) |
    69→| Safety layer (role auth + pre/post checks) | `roko-agent` | — | — (integrated) |
    70→| PRD lifecycle (idea/draft/plan) | `roko-cli` | 38 | `roko prd` |
    71→| Research agent (topic/enhance) | `roko-cli` | — | `roko research` |
    72→| Session persistence + resume | `roko-cli` | — | `roko plan run --resume` |
    73→| Efficiency events + cascade router persist | `roko-learn` | — | — (auto) |
    74→| Configuration management | `roko-cli` | — | `roko config` |
    75→
    76→### Built (compiles, has code, not fully wired to runtime)
    77→
    78→| Component | Crate | Tests | Gap |
    79→|-----------|-------|-------|-----|
    80→| HDC vectors (10,240-bit) + fingerprinting | `roko-primitives` | — | Used by roko-index, not yet by runtime |
    81→| Knowledge store (6 types × 4 tiers) | `roko-neuro` | — | Struct exists; not wired to orchestrator knowledge injection |
    82→| PAD vector + 6 behavioral states + somatic markers | `roko-daimon` | — | Struct exists; not wired to tier routing |
    83→| 10 reactive watchers + circuit breaker | `roko-conductor` | — | Built but not called from orchestrate.rs |
    84→| Chain client + wallet + witness | `roko-chain` | 52 | Needs Korai chain deployment |
    85→| Tree-sitter parsing + symbol graph + PageRank | `roko-index` | — | Built; MCP server not wired |
    86→| Rust/TypeScript/Go language support | `roko-lang-*` | — | Built; used by roko-index |
    87→| EVM simulator | `mirage-rs` | 141 | Chain-domain testing tool; works standalone |
    88→
    89→### Scaffold (stubs only)
    90→
    91→| Component | Crate | Gap |
    92→|-----------|-------|-----|
    93→| Dream engine (NREM/REM/integration) | `roko-dreams` | Runner + cycle facades exist; core algorithms unimplemented |
    94→| MCP servers (GitHub, Slack, Scripts, Stdio) | `roko-mcp-*` | Crate stubs, no implementation |
    95→| HTTP server + REST API | `roko-serve` | Crate exists, no routes |
    96→| Text dashboard (TUI) | `roko-cli` | Renders text pages, no interactive terminal UI |
    97→
    98→### Specified (PRD docs only, no code)
    99→
   100→| Component | Section | Key Docs |
   101→|-----------|---------|----------|
   102→| Heartbeat cognitive loop (Gamma/Theta/Delta) | 16 | 9-step pipeline, dual process, attention auction |
   103→| Agent mesh + pheromone gossip | 13 | Stigmergy, pheromone kinds, mesh sync |
   104→| Morphogenetic specialization | 13 | Reaction-diffusion agent differentiation |
   105→| Technical analysis oracles (7 frontier methods) | 20 | HDC-TA, spectral manifolds, causal discovery, TDA |
   106→| Temporal logic safety monitors | 11 | Büchi automata, LTL/CTL verification |
   107→| Witness DAG + ZK proofs | 11 | Content-addressed audit DAG, plonky2 |
   108→| Formal verification pipeline | 11 | Heimdall, Slither, Echidna, HEVM |
   109→| Cognitive kernel safety | 11 | Namespace isolation, signal delivery |
   110→| Active inference compute allocation | 16 | EFE estimation, LinUCB bandits |
   111→| Plugin SDK + event sources | 18 | Domain plugin automation |
   112→| Agent lifecycle (birth → retirement) | 17 | Full lifecycle state machine |
   113→| Deployment (cloud, bare-metal, hybrid) | 19 | Infrastructure patterns |
   114→
   115→### Deferred (Phase 2+)
   116→
   117→| Component | Section | Why Deferred |
   118→|-----------|---------|--------------|
   119→| Identity & economy layer | 14 | Requires Korai chain launch |
   120→| ERC-8004 registries | 08 | Chain-dependent |
   121→| x402 micropayments | 08 | Chain-dependent |
   122→| Reputation system (7-domain) | 08 | Chain-dependent |
   123→| Sonification / audio interface | 12 | Research/experimental |
   124→| Generational evolution (agent lineages) | 17 | Requires stable mesh + economy |
   125→
   126→---
   127→
   128→## Test Coverage Summary
   129→
   130→| Crate | Tests | Layer |
   131→|-------|-------|-------|
   132→| `roko-core` | 376 | Kernel |
   133→| `roko-agent` | 346 | L1 Framework |
   134→| `roko-gate` | 200 | L3 Harness |
   135→| `roko-orchestrator` | 158 | L4 Orchestration |
   136→| `mirage-rs` | 141 | L1 Framework (chain testing) |
   137→| `roko-learn` | 101 | Cross-cut |
   138→| `roko-std` | 96 | L1 Framework |
   139→| `roko-chain` | 52 | L1 Framework |
   140→| `roko-cli` | 38 | L4 Application |
   141→| `roko-fs` | 37 | L3 Harness |
   142→| `roko-compose` | 23 | L2 Scaffold |
   143→| **Total** | **1,568** | |
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## Critical Path to Full Self-Hosting
   148→
   149→The self-hosting loop works today (`prd` → `plan run` → gate → persist → resume`). Three capabilities would close the remaining gaps:
   150→
   151→1. **Interactive TUI** (Section 12) — Wire `ratatui` into the text dashboard scaffold. Currently `roko dashboard` outputs plain text.
   152→
   153→2. **Automatic plan generation** (Section 01) — Trigger `prd plan` automatically when a PRD is published, removing the manual step.
   154→
   155→3. **Feedback loop** (Section 05) — Failed task gates feed back into the plan generator for automatic re-planning, closing the learn-from-failure cycle.
   156→
   157→After these three, Roko can fully self-host: read its own PRDs, generate plans, execute them, validate results, learn from failures, and iterate — without human intervention beyond initial PRD creation.
   158→
   159→---
   160→
   161→## How to Read This Document
   162→
   163→- **If you're new**: Start with [QUICKSTART.md](QUICKSTART.md), then return here for orientation.
   164→- **If you're implementing**: Find your target section in the matrix above. Check the tier. If "Shipping," you're extending working code. If "Built," you're wiring existing code into the runtime. If "Specified," you're building from the PRD spec.
   165→- **If you're debugging**: The test count column tells you where coverage exists. Crates with `—` for tests are the riskiest to modify.
   166→- **If you're reviewing**: Cross-reference with section-specific status docs (linked in the matrix) for detailed gap analysis.
   167→

content.content:
1→# 04 — Verification (L3 Harness)
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-gate` (`crates/roko-gate/src/`)
     5→> **Generated**: 2026-04-11
     6→> **Source**: Prompt 04-verification
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## What This Section Covers
    11→
    12→Layer 3 (Harness) is Roko's verification layer. It establishes ground truth about
    13→agent-produced artifacts by running gates — deterministic external tools (compilers,
    14→test runners, linters, static analyzers) that produce `Verdict`s. Gate verdicts flow
    15→back into the system as signals, feeding the conductor, the router, the learning loops,
    16→and the agents themselves.
    17→
    18→The key design principle: **Gate failure is a verdict, not an error.** The `Gate` trait
    19→returns `Verdict` directly — not `Result<Verdict>`. This means every downstream consumer
    20→receives a definitive answer without error-handling code.
    21→
    22→---
    23→
    24→## Sub-Documents
    25→
    26→| # | File | Topic | Lines |
    27→|---|---|---|---|
    28→| 00 | [00-gate-trait.md](./00-gate-trait.md) | The Gate trait: signature, `-> Verdict` design, `name()`, position in universal loop | 210 |
    29→| 01 | [01-gate-implementations.md](./01-gate-implementations.md) | 11 concrete gates: ShellGate, CompileGate, ClippyGate, TestGate, SymbolGate, DiffGate, and scaffolds | 247 |
    30→| 02 | [02-6-rung-selector.md](./02-6-rung-selector.md) | 7-rung selector: Compile→Lint→Test→Symbol→GeneratedTest→PropertyTest→Integration, PlanComplexity, escalation | 230 |
    31→| 03 | [03-gate-pipeline.md](./03-gate-pipeline.md) | GatePipeline: sequential composition, short-circuit, verdict aggregation, test count merging | 218 |
    32→| 04 | [04-artifact-store.md](./04-artifact-store.md) | ArtifactStore: BLAKE3 content-addressed, append-only, deduplicating artifact storage | 200 |
    33→| 05 | [05-ratcheting.md](./05-ratcheting.md) | GateRatchet: monotonic rung tracking, regression prevention, convergence thrashing protection | 225 |
    34→| 06 | [06-adaptive-thresholds.md](./06-adaptive-thresholds.md) | AdaptiveThresholds: per-rung EMA pass rates, retry budget, skip advisory, persistence | 210 |
    35→| 07 | [07-process-reward-models.md](./07-process-reward-models.md) | Process rewards: Promise + Progress scoring, per-turn intervention, multi-timescale feedback | 220 |
    36→| 08 | [08-agent-feedback-from-gates.md](./08-agent-feedback-from-gates.md) | GateFeedback: line classification, noise filtering, severity buckets, token economy | 225 |
    37→| 09 | [09-evaluation-lifecycle.md](./09-evaluation-lifecycle.md) | 14 feedback loops across 5 speed tiers, four-phase lifecycle, Karpathy property, Gauntlet | 215 |
    38→| 10 | [10-autonomous-eval-generation.md](./10-autonomous-eval-generation.md) | Autonomous test generation: verification pipeline, separation of concerns, cheap-model convergence | 220 |
    39→| 11 | [11-evoskills.md](./11-evoskills.md) | EvoSkills: three-tier learning hierarchy, adversarial surrogate verification, cross-model transfer | 220 |
    40→| 12 | [12-forensic-ai-causal-replay.md](./12-forensic-ai-causal-replay.md) | Forensic AI: content-addressed causal chains, regulatory compliance, gap analysis | 225 |
    41→
    42→**Total**: ~2,865 lines across 13 sub-documents plus this index.
    43→
    44→---
    45→
    46→## Architecture Summary
    47→
    48→```
    49→                    RungSelector
    50→                   (complexity + caps + failures)
    51→                         │
    52→                         ▼
    53→                    GatePipeline
    54→                   (sequential, short-circuit)
    55→                         │
    56→              ┌──────────┼──────────┐
    57→              ▼          ▼          ▼
    58→         CompileGate  ClippyGate  TestGate  ...  IntegrationGate
    59→          (Rung 0)    (Rung 1)   (Rung 2)       (Rung 6)
    60→              │          │          │                │
    61→              └──────────┼──────────┘                │
    62→                         ▼                           │
    63→                    Aggregated Verdict ◄─────────────┘
    64→                         │
    65→         ┌───────────────┼───────────────────┐
    66→         ▼               ▼                   ▼
    67→    GateRatchet    AdaptiveThresholds   GateFeedback
    68→   (regression)   (retry budget, skip)  (agent retry)
    69→         │               │                   │
    70→         ▼               ▼                   ▼
    71→    ArtifactStore   EfficencyEvents     ProcessRewards
    72→   (BLAKE3 hash)   (per-turn data)   (Promise+Progress)
    73→         │               │                   │
    74→         └───────────────┼───────────────────┘
    75→                         ▼
    76→                  Evaluation Lifecycle
    77→                (14 loops × 5 speed tiers)
    78→                         │
    79→                    ┌────┼────┐
    80→                    ▼         ▼
    81→              EvoSkills   ForensicReplay
    82→            (skill lib)  (causal chains)
    83→```
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→## Key Design Decisions
    88→
    89→### 1. `Gate::verify()` returns `Verdict`, not `Result<Verdict>`
    90→
    91→Gate failure is a verdict, not an error. Infrastructure failures (spawn errors,
    92→timeouts, malformed input) are encoded as `Verdict::fail()`. This eliminates error
    93→propagation in the pipeline, ratchet, and all downstream consumers.
    94→
    95→**Source**: `crates/roko-core/src/traits.rs:102–108`
    96→
    97→### 2. Sequential gate execution with short-circuit
    98→
    99→Gates run cheapest-first. The first failure stops the pipeline. This is the primary
   100→optimization that makes the 7-rung system efficient: a 3-second compile failure prevents
   101→a 15-minute test run.
   102→
   103→**Source**: `crates/roko-gate/src/gate_pipeline.rs`
   104→
   105→### 3. Monotonic ratchet
   106→
   107→Once a plan passes rung N, it cannot regress below N. This prevents convergence
   108→thrashing where the agent oscillates between fixing different rungs.
   109→
   110→**Source**: `crates/roko-gate/src/ratchet.rs`
   111→
   112→### 4. EMA-based adaptive thresholds
   113→
   114→Per-rung pass rates tracked via EMA (α=0.1) inform retry budgets and skip advisories.
   115→Persistent to disk for cross-session continuity.
   116→
   117→**Source**: `crates/roko-gate/src/adaptive_threshold.rs`
   118→
   119→### 5. Separation of test generation from implementation
   120→
   121→Different agents generate tests and implement code. This adversarial setup prevents the
   122→implementation agent from generating easy-to-pass tests.
   123→
   124→**Source**: Verification-first architecture (bardo-backup reference)
   125→
   126→---
   127→
   128→## Cross-References
   129→
   130→| Topic | See Also |
   131→|---|---|
   132→| Gate trait in the Synapse Architecture | `docs/01-architecture/` (Synapse traits) |
   133→| Gate feedback → agent prompts | `docs/03-scaffold/` (prompt assembly) |
   134→| Gate verdicts → model routing | `docs/05-learning/` (CascadeRouter) |
   135→| Gate verdicts → conductor | `docs/06-conductor/` (circuit breaker, watchers) |
   136→| Orchestrator wiring | `crates/roko-cli/src/orchestrate.rs` |
   137→| Agent dispatch | `crates/roko-agent/src/dispatcher/mod.rs` |
   138→| Episode logging | `.roko/episodes.jsonl` |
   139→| Efficiency events | `.roko/learn/efficiency.jsonl` |
   140→| Gate thresholds | `.roko/learn/gate-thresholds.json` |
   141→
   142→---
   143→
   144→## Source Material
   145→
   146→### Canonical Sources (refactoring-prd/)
   147→
   148→| File | Sections Used |
   149→|---|---|
   150→| `01-synapse-architecture.md` | Gate trait signature, cybernetic feedback loops |
   151→| `02-five-layers.md` | Layer 3 Harness definition, process reward models |
   152→| `07-implementation-priorities.md` | Tier 2J prediction tracking |

   155→

   157→
   158→| File | What It Provided |
   159→|---|---|
   160→| `prd/16-testing/01-gauntlet.md` | Gauntlet benchmark suite |
   161→| `prd/16-testing/07-fast-feedback-loops.md` | 5 fast evaluation loops |
   162→| `prd/16-testing/08-slow-feedback-loops.md` | 3 slow evaluation loops |

1→# 00 — The Gate Trait
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-core` (`crates/roko-core/src/traits.rs`)
     5→> **Status**: Stable, implemented
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→The `Gate` trait is one of six foundational traits in Roko's Synapse Architecture. It
    15→occupies a unique position: while Substrate stores, Scorer evaluates, Router selects,
    16→Composer assembles, and Policy governs — **Gate verifies**. It is the system's sole
    17→mechanism for establishing ground truth about agent-produced artifacts.
    18→
    19→Gates answer one question: *did the agent's output meet the verifiable criteria for
    20→this verification step?* The answer is always a `Verdict` — never an error, never a
    21→maybe, never a "could not determine." This design is deliberate and load-bearing.
    22→
    23→> **Citation**: Synapse Architecture (refactoring-prd/01-synapse-architecture.md) — "1
    24→> noun (Signal) + 6 verb traits" composing the universal loop.
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## 2. The Trait Signature
    29→
    30→```rust
    31→// crates/roko-core/src/traits.rs, lines 102–108
    32→
    33→pub trait Gate: Send + Sync {
    34→    /// Verify the signal and return a verdict.
    35→    async fn verify(&self, signal: &Signal, ctx: &Context) -> Verdict;
    36→
    37→    /// Human-readable name (appears in verdicts).
    38→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
    39→}
    40→```
    41→
    42→Two methods. That's the entire surface area.
    43→
    44→### 2.1 `verify()` Returns `Verdict`, Not `Result<Verdict>`
    45→
    46→This is the single most important design decision in the Gate trait and it requires
    47→emphasis because it diverges from the idiomatic Rust pattern of returning `Result` from
    48→fallible operations.
    49→
    50→**Gate failure is not an error — it is a verdict.**
    51→
    52→When `cargo check` reports a compilation error, that is not an infrastructure failure.
    53→It is meaningful information: the code does not compile. The gate's job is to encode
    54→that outcome into a `Verdict::fail()` with a reason string and optional error digest.
    55→The caller never has to handle two failure paths (infrastructure error vs. verification
    56→failure); there is only one path: the verdict.
    57→
    58→This means:
    59→- A gate that cannot spawn its subprocess returns `Verdict::fail("spawn failed: ...")`
    60→- A gate that times out returns `Verdict::fail("timed out after N ms")`
    61→- A gate whose input signal has malformed JSON returns `Verdict::fail("signal body is not a GatePayload: ...")`
    62→- A gate whose test suite has 3 failures returns `Verdict::fail("test foo::bar ... FAILED; ...")`
    63→
    64→All four are verdicts. The pipeline, ratchet, adaptive thresholds, and feedback systems
    65→never need to pattern-match on `Result::Err`. They receive a `Verdict` and act on it.
    66→
    67→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/01-synapse-architecture.md — Gate trait signature:
    68→> `async fn verify(&self, engram: &Engram, ctx: &Context) -> Verdict`. The canonical
    69→> source explicitly uses `-> Verdict`, not `-> Result<Verdict>`.
    70→
    71→> **Citation**: crates/roko-core/src/traits.rs:102–108 — Active implementation
    72→> confirms the `-> Verdict` return type.
    73→
    74→### 2.2 Why `async`
    75→
    76→Gates shell out to external tools: compilers, test runners, linters, static analyzers.
    77→These are I/O-bound operations measured in seconds to minutes. Making `verify()` async
    78→allows the gate pipeline to:
    79→
    80→1. Run multiple independent gates concurrently (future: parallel gate groups)
    81→2. Apply timeouts via `tokio::time::timeout` without blocking the executor
    82→3. Compose with the rest of Roko's async runtime (agent dispatch, signal persistence)
    83→
    84→Every concrete gate implementation uses `#[async_trait]` from the `async-trait` crate
    85→to satisfy the trait's async method.
    86→
    87→### 2.3 Why `Send + Sync`
    88→
    89→Gates are composed into pipelines (`GatePipeline`) that may be shared across tasks in
    90→the plan executor. The `Send + Sync` bounds ensure gates can be stored in
    91→`Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>` and passed between threads safely.
    92→
    93→---
    94→
    95→## 3. The `name()` Method
    96→
    97→```rust
    98→fn name(&self) -> &str;
    99→```
   100→
   101→Every verdict carries the name of the gate that produced it. This serves three purposes:
   102→
   103→1. **Traceability**: When a verdict flows into the feedback system, the agent (and the
   104→   human operator) can see *which* gate failed. "compile:cargo" vs. "test:cargo" vs.
   105→   "clippy:cargo" vs. "diff" vs. "symbol" — these are distinct verification steps with
   106→   distinct remediation paths.
   107→
   108→2. **Ratcheting**: The `GateRatchet` tracks the highest rung passed per plan. Gate
   109→   names associate verdicts with rungs.
   110→
   111→3. **Adaptive thresholds**: The `AdaptiveThresholds` system tracks per-rung pass rates.
   112→   Gate names allow the threshold system to correlate verdicts with rungs for EMA
   113→   updates.
   114→
   115→Naming convention in the codebase follows `category:tool` format:
   116→- `compile:cargo`, `compile:npm`, `compile:go`
   117→- `test:cargo`, `test:npm`, `test:go`
   118→- `clippy:cargo` (lint gates)
   119→- `shell:true`, `shell:custom_script`
   120→- `diff`, `symbol` (standalone names)
   121→
   122→---
   123→
   124→## 4. The `Verdict` Type
   125→
   126→While `Verdict` is defined in `roko-core` (not in the gate crate), understanding it is
   127→essential to understanding gates. A `Verdict` carries:
   128→
   129→| Field | Type | Purpose |
   130→|---|---|---|
   131→| `passed` | `bool` | Did the gate pass? |
   132→| `gate` | `String` | Name of the gate that produced this verdict |
   133→| `reason` | `String` | Human-readable explanation (especially on failure) |
   134→| `detail` | `Option<String>` | Full output (stdout + stderr) for debugging |
   135→| `error_digest` | `Option<String>` | Machine-parseable error summary |
   136→| `duration_ms` | `u64` | Wall-clock time the gate took |
   137→| `test_count` | `Option<TestCount>` | Parsed test counts (passed/failed/ignored) |
   138→
   139→Key construction patterns used across all gate implementations:
   140→
   141→```rust
   142→// Passing verdict
   143→Verdict::pass(&self.name)
   144→    .with_detail(combined_output)
   145→    .with_duration(elapsed_ms)
   146→
   147→// Failing verdict with reason
   148→Verdict::fail(&self.name, reason_string)
   149→    .with_detail(combined_output)
   150→    .with_duration(elapsed_ms)
   151→
   152→// Failing verdict with machine-parseable digest
   153→Verdict::fail(&self.name, "3 symbol expectations unmet")
   154→    .with_error_digest(digest)
   155→    .with_detail(digest.clone())
   156→    .with_duration(elapsed_ms)
   157→
   158→// Test gate attaches parsed counts
   159→verdict.with_test_count(TestCount::new(passed, failed, ignored))
   160→```
   161→
   162→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/compile.rs:113–122 — CompileGate demonstrates
   163→> the standard pass/fail verdict construction pattern.
   164→
   165→---
   166→
   167→## 5. The Gate Contract
   168→
   169→Every gate implementation must satisfy these invariants:
   170→
   171→### 5.1 Total Function
   172→
   173→`verify()` must always return a `Verdict`. It must not panic, it must not return `Err`,
   174→and it must not hang indefinitely. Every gate in the codebase enforces a timeout
   175→(typically via `tokio::time::timeout`) and converts timeout expiration into
   176→`Verdict::fail()`.
   177→
   178→Timeouts by gate:
   179→- `CompileGate`: 10 minutes (600,000 ms)
   180→- `TestGate`: 15 minutes (900,000 ms)
   181→- `ClippyGate`: 5 minutes (300,000 ms)
   182→- `ShellGate`: 5 minutes (300,000 ms)
   183→
   184→### 5.2 Deterministic on Identical Inputs
   185→
   186→Given the same signal body and filesystem state, a gate should produce the same verdict.
   187→Gates do not use randomness. They do not consult LLMs (except the future
   188→`LlmJudgeGate`, which has its own reproducibility constraints).
   189→
   190→### 5.3 Side-Effect Free (on the source)
   191→
   192→Gates read the filesystem and run subprocesses, but they must not modify the source code
   193→being verified. A gate that rewrites the code to make it pass would defeat its purpose.
   194→Gates may produce artifacts (build caches, test output files), but these go into
   195→`CARGO_TARGET_DIR` or the `ArtifactStore`, not into the source tree.
   196→
   197→### 5.4 Duration Tracking
   198→
   199→Every verdict must carry `duration_ms`. This feeds the adaptive threshold system's retry
   200→budget calculations and the efficiency event logger's per-gate timing data.
   201→
   202→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/shell.rs:57–118 — ShellGate's `verify()`
   203→> demonstrates all contract properties: timeout handling, duration tracking, infallible
   204→> return.
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## 6. How Gates Differ from Scorers
   209→
   210→Both Gates and Scorers evaluate signals, but they serve different purposes:
   211→
   212→| Dimension | Gate | Scorer |
   213→|---|---|---|
   214→| Output | `Verdict` (pass/fail + metadata) | `Score` (numeric, 0.0–1.0) |
   215→| Truth source | External tool (compiler, test runner) | Internal heuristic or model |
   216→| Determinism | Deterministic (same code → same result) | May be probabilistic |
   217→| Cost | High (subprocess spawn, minutes) | Low (computation, milliseconds) |
   218→| Role in loop | Verification (L3 Harness) | Evaluation (L2 Engine) |
   219→
   220→A Scorer might estimate "this code looks 80% correct." A Gate runs the compiler and
   221→says definitively "this code compiles" or "this code does not compile." The Scorer's
   222→estimate is useful for routing decisions; the Gate's verdict is ground truth.
   223→
   224→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/02-five-layers.md — Layer 2 (Engine) vs. Layer 3
   225→> (Harness): scoring vs. verification distinction.
   226→
   227→---
   228→
   229→## 7. Position in the Universal Loop
   230→
   231→The universal loop in Roko is: **query → score → route → compose → act → verify →
   232→write → react**. Gates occupy the **verify** step.
   233→
   234→```
   235→Agent produces output (act)
   236→    ↓
   237→Gate pipeline verifies output (verify)
   238→    ↓
   239→Verdict flows to:
   240→    → Substrate (write: persist verdict as signal)
   241→    → Scorer (react: update scoring model)
   242→    → Router (react: update bandit arms)
   243→    → Composer (react: adjust prompt sections)
   244→    → Conductor (react: circuit breaker, watchers)
   245→    → Ratchet (react: track highest rung passed)
   246→    → Adaptive thresholds (react: update per-rung EMA)
   247→    → Agent feedback (react: filter output for agent context)
   248→```
   249→
   250→The gate verdict is the system's primary feedback signal. It drives six different
   251→learning and adaptation mechanisms. This is why the gate returning `Verdict` directly
   252→(not `Result<Verdict>`) matters so much — every downstream consumer expects a definitive
   253→answer, and wrapping it in `Result` would mean every consumer needs error-handling code
   254→for a case that should never arise.
   255→
   256→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/01-synapse-architecture.md — Universal loop:
   257→> query→score→route→compose→act→verify→write→react. Cybernetic feedback loops from
   258→> Gate to Scorer, Router, Composer.
   259→
   260→---
   261→
   262→## 8. Relationship to the GVU Framework
   263→
   264→The Generation-Verification-Update (GVU) framework from Song et al. (ICLR 2025) proves
   265→a critical theorem: **self-improvement succeeds when the verifier is strong, not when
   266→the generator is strong**. Specifically, the Variance Inequality shows that an oracle
   267→verifier (verification noise σ_V ≈ 0) enables improvement despite arbitrarily high
   268→generation noise.
   269→
   270→Roko's gate pipeline is, in GVU terms, the verifier. Compilers and test suites are
   271→oracle verifiers for their respective properties — they have zero false positive rate
   272→for the properties they check. This is why Roko invests in a rich gate ecosystem (11
   273→gates, 7 rungs) rather than relying solely on better prompts: **the returns to stronger
   274→verification compound, while the returns to stronger generation plateau**.
   275→
   276→> **Citation**: Song et al. "Self-Improving Diffusion Models with Synthetic Data" (ICLR
   277→> 2025) — Generation-Verification-Update framework, Variance Inequality theorem.
   278→
   279→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md — Innovation VIII: Process Reward
   280→> Models informed by GVU framework's verification-first insight.
   281→
   282→---
   283→
   284→## 9. Future: The Gate Trait in Multi-Language Contexts
   285→
   286→The current Gate trait signature accepts `Signal` (which the canonical architecture
   287→names `Engram`). The signal's body carries a `GatePayload` with `BuildSystem` (Cargo,
   288→Npm, Go, Make). This means the Gate trait itself is language-agnostic — only the
   289→concrete implementations (`CompileGate`, `TestGate`, `ClippyGate`) know about specific
   290→build systems.
   291→
   292→Adding support for a new language requires:
   293→1. Adding a `BuildSystem` variant (e.g., `BuildSystem::Gradle`)
   294→2. Implementing `check_args()`, `test_args()`, `lint_args()` for the new variant
   295→3. Optionally: a new `parse_*_test_counts()` function for the test runner's output format
   296→
   297→No changes to the Gate trait are needed.
   298→
   299→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/test_gate.rs:171–179 — `parse_test_counts()`
   300→> dispatches by `BuildSystem`, demonstrating language-agnostic gate design.
   301→
   302→---
   303→
   304→## 10. Summary
   305→
   306→The Gate trait is deliberately minimal: two methods, no error return type, no generic
   307→parameters. This simplicity is a feature. It means any verification step — from a 5ms
   308→regex check to a 15-minute integration test suite — can be expressed as a Gate, composed
   309→into a pipeline, tracked by a ratchet, measured by adaptive thresholds, and fed back to
   310→agents and learning systems through a uniform interface.
   311→
   312→The single most important thing to remember about the Gate trait:
   313→
   314→**`verify()` returns `Verdict`, not `Result<Verdict>`. Gate failure is a verdict, not an error.**
   315→

1→# 01 — Gate Implementations
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-gate` (`crates/roko-gate/src/`)
     5→> **Status**: 11 gate implementations, 7 wired into the rung selector
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping

    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→Roko ships 11 concrete gate implementations. Each implements the `Gate` trait (see
    15→[00-gate-trait.md](./00-gate-trait.md)) and returns `Verdict` directly — not
    16→`Result<Verdict>`. Every gate handles its own errors internally, converting
    17→infrastructure failures (spawn errors, timeouts, malformed input) into
    18→`Verdict::fail()`.
    19→
    20→The gates are organized by verification layer, from cheapest/fastest to most
    21→expensive/thorough:
    22→
    23→| Gate | Module | Rung | Cost | What it verifies |
    24→|---|---|---|---|---|
    25→| `CompileGate` | `compile.rs` | 0 (Compile) | Low | Code compiles |
    26→| `ClippyGate` | `clippy_gate.rs` | 1 (Lint) | Low | No lint violations |
    27→| `TestGate` | `test_gate.rs` | 2 (Test) | Medium | Tests pass |
    28→| `SymbolGate` | `symbol_gate.rs` | 3 (Symbol) | Zero (no subprocess) | Required symbols exist |
    29→| `DiffGate` | `diff_gate.rs` | N/A (pre-pipeline) | Zero | Non-vacuous changes |
    30→| `ShellGate` | `shell.rs` | Any | Varies | Arbitrary shell command |
    31→| `GeneratedTestGate` | `generated_test_gate.rs` | 4 (GeneratedTest) | High | Auto-generated tests pass |
    32→| `PropertyTestGate` | `property_test_gate.rs` | 5 (PropertyTest) | High | Property-based tests pass |
    33→| `IntegrationGate` | `integration_gate.rs` | 6 (Integration) | Highest | Integration tests pass |
    34→| `LlmJudgeGate` | `llm_judge_gate.rs` | N/A (auxiliary) | Medium | LLM-based quality judgment |
    35→| `VerifyChainGate` | `verify_chain_gate.rs` | N/A (auxiliary) | Low | Chain verification |
    36→
    37→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/lib.rs — Module declarations for all 11 gates.
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## 2. ShellGate — The Foundation
    42→
    43→**File**: `crates/roko-gate/src/shell.rs` (186 lines)
    44→
    45→`ShellGate` is the simplest gate and the building block for others. It runs an arbitrary
    46→shell command and passes if the exit code is 0.
    47→
    48→```rust
    49→pub struct ShellGate {
    50→    program: String,
    51→    args: Vec<String>,
    52→    timeout_ms: u64,     // default: 5 minutes (300,000 ms)
    53→    name: String,
    54→}
    55→```
    56→
    57→### Construction
    58→
    59→```rust
    60→ShellGate::new("cargo", vec!["fmt".into(), "--check".into()])
    61→    .with_timeout_ms(60_000)
    62→    .with_name("format_check")
    63→```
    64→
    65→### Verdict Construction
    66→
    67→The `ShellGate::verify()` method demonstrates the canonical pattern used by all
    68→shell-spawning gates:
    69→
    70→1. **Read payload**: Extract `GatePayload` from signal body for `working_dir` and
    71→   `extra_env`. If absent, use process defaults.
    72→2. **Build command**: Set program, args, cwd, env vars, `kill_on_drop(true)`.
    73→3. **Run with timeout**: `tokio::time::timeout(duration, cmd.output()).await`
    74→4. **Handle three outcomes**:
    75→   - `Err(_)` → timeout → `Verdict::fail("timed out after N ms")`
    76→   - `Ok(Err(io_err))` → spawn failure → `Verdict::fail("spawn failed: ...")`
    77→   - `Ok(Ok(output))` → check exit code → `Verdict::pass()` or `Verdict::fail()`
    78→5. **Always attach**: `.with_detail(combined_output).with_duration(elapsed_ms)`
    79→
    80→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/shell.rs:57–118 — Full `ShellGate::verify()`
    81→> implementation.
    82→
    83→---
    84→
    85→## 3. CompileGate — Rung 0
    86→
    87→**File**: `crates/roko-gate/src/compile.rs` (195 lines)
    88→
    89→`CompileGate` wraps `ShellGate`'s pattern with build-system awareness. It reads
    90→`BuildSystem` from the `GatePayload` and runs the appropriate check command.
    91→
    92→```rust
    93→pub struct CompileGate {
    94→    build_system: BuildSystem,
    95→    extra_args: Vec<String>,
    96→    timeout_ms: u64,     // default: 10 minutes (600,000 ms)
    97→    name: String,        // e.g., "compile:cargo"
    98→}
    99→```
   100→
   101→### Build System Dispatch
   102→
   103→The `BuildSystem` enum (defined in `payload.rs`) supports:
   104→- **Cargo**: `cargo check --workspace`
   105→- **Npm**: `npm run build`
   106→- **Go**: `go build ./...`
   107→- **Make**: `make`
   108→
   109→Each variant implements `program()`, `check_args()`, `test_args()`, and `lint_args()`.
   110→
   111→### Error Summarization
   112→
   113→On failure, `CompileGate` extracts up to 3 error-level diagnostics from stderr via
   114→`summarize_errors()`. This keeps the verdict's `reason` field concise while the full
   115→output lives in `detail`.
   116→
   117→```rust
   118→fn summarize_errors(stderr: &str, max: usize) -> String {
   119→    let errors: Vec<&str> = stderr.lines()
   120→        .filter(|l| l.trim_start().starts_with("error:") || l.trim_start().starts_with("error["))
   121→        .take(max)
   122→        .collect();
   123→    if !errors.is_empty() { errors.join("; ") }
   124→    else { stderr.lines().find(|l| !l.trim().is_empty()).unwrap_or("compilation failed").to_string() }
   125→}
   126→```
   127→
   128→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/compile.rs:134–151 — `summarize_errors()`
   129→> function.
   130→
   131→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/tmp/mori-agents/20-verification-first-architecture.md —
   132→> Rung 0 is compilation: "cheapest check that proves the code at least compiles."
   133→
   134→---
   135→
   136→## 4. ClippyGate — Rung 1
   137→
   138→**File**: `crates/roko-gate/src/clippy_gate.rs` (212 lines)
   139→
   140→`ClippyGate` runs the language-appropriate linter. For Rust, this is `cargo clippy --
   141→-D warnings`; for Go, `go vet`; for Node, `npm run lint`.
   142→
   143→```rust
   144→pub struct ClippyGate {
   145→    build_system: BuildSystem,
   146→    extra_args: Vec<String>,
   147→    timeout_ms: u64,     // default: 5 minutes (300,000 ms)
   148→    name: String,        // e.g., "clippy:cargo"
   149→}
   150→```
   151→
   152→### Argument Splicing
   153→
   154→ClippyGate handles the Cargo-specific `--` sentinel carefully: extra args are inserted
   155→*before* the `--` separator so they apply to the Cargo invocation (e.g., `--features ci`)
   156→rather than to Clippy's own flags.
   157→
   158→```rust
   159→let dash_idx = base.iter().position(|a| *a == "--");
   160→if let Some(idx) = dash_idx {
   161→    // Insert extra_args before the "--"
   162→    for arg in &base[..idx] { cmd.arg(arg); }
   163→    for arg in &self.extra_args { cmd.arg(arg); }
   164→    for arg in &base[idx..] { cmd.arg(arg); }
   165→}
   166→```
   167→
   168→### Design: Lint Before Test
   169→
   170→ClippyGate is designed to run before `TestGate` in a short-circuit pipeline. Lint checks
   171→are fast (seconds) and catch many issues that would cause expensive test failures
   172→(minutes). Running lint first saves time by failing fast.
   173→
   174→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/clippy_gate.rs:70–93 — Argument splicing logic.
   175→
   176→---
   177→
   178→## 5. TestGate — Rung 2
   179→
   180→**File**: `crates/roko-gate/src/test_gate.rs` (384 lines)
   181→
   182→`TestGate` runs the project's test suite and parses passed/failed/ignored counts from
   183→the output.
   184→
   185→```rust
   186→pub struct TestGate {
   187→    build_system: BuildSystem,
   188→    selector: TestSelector,
   189→    extra_args: Vec<String>,
   190→    timeout_ms: u64,     // default: 15 minutes (900,000 ms)
   191→    name: String,        // e.g., "test:cargo"
   192→}
   193→```
   194→
   195→### Test Selectors
   196→
   197→The `TestSelector` enum controls which tests run:
   198→
   199→```rust
   200→pub enum TestSelector {
   201→    All,                    // Run everything
   202→    Quick,                  // --lib (unit tests only)
   203→    Patterns(Vec<String>),  // Specific test patterns
   204→}
   205→```
   206→
   207→For Cargo, `Quick` adds `--lib`. For Go, `Patterns(["TestFoo", "TestBar"])` becomes
   208→`-run TestFoo|TestBar`.
   209→
   210→### Test Count Parsing
   211→
   212→`parse_test_counts()` dispatches by build system:
   213→- **Cargo**: Parses `test result: ok. N passed; M failed; K ignored` lines, aggregating
   214→  across multiple test targets.
   215→- **Go**: Counts `--- PASS:`, `--- FAIL:`, `--- SKIP:` markers.
   216→
   217→Parsed counts are attached to the verdict via `.with_test_count(tc)`. This enables
   218→downstream policies to classify "mostly passing" runs (e.g., ≥90% pass rate with ≥20
   219→tests and ≥1 failure) differently from total failures.
   220→
   221→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/test_gate.rs:166–241 — `parse_test_counts()` and
   222→> per-build-system parsers.
   223→
   224→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/tmp/mori-agents/20-verification-first-architecture.md —
   225→> Rung 1 (test execution), verification-first architecture.
   226→
   227→---
   228→
   229→## 6. SymbolGate — Rung 3
   230→
   231→**File**: `crates/roko-gate/src/symbol_gate.rs` (1001 lines)
   232→
   233→`SymbolGate` is unique among the gates: it spawns no subprocesses and makes no LLM
   234→calls. It parses Rust source files directly and verifies that every symbol in a
   235→`SymbolManifest` exists with the correct kind, visibility, and module path.
   236→
   237→```rust
   238→pub struct SymbolGate {
   239→    source_roots: Vec<PathBuf>,
   240→    name: String,
   241→}
   242→```
   243→
   244→### What It Catches
   245→
   246→The most common agent failure is: "I was told to create `pub struct RateLimiter` and
   247→did not." SymbolGate catches this at effectively zero cost, before any compilation or
   248→test execution.
   249→
   250→### Mismatch Taxonomy
   251→
   252→When expectations are not met, SymbolGate produces a structured error digest:
   253→
   254→```
   255→4 symbol expectation(s) unmet:
   256→  MISSING: struct RateLimiter at core::rate_limit
   257→  WRONG_VIS: fn check_rate at core::rate_limit (found: private, expected: pub)
   258→  WRONG_KIND: Limiter at core::rate_limit (found: struct, expected: trait)
   259→  WRONG_PATH: struct Clock at core::time (found at: core::clock)
   260→  AMBIGUOUS: fn foo at core::util (2 matches)
   261→```
   262→
   263→Five mismatch categories: `MISSING`, `WRONG_VIS`, `WRONG_KIND`, `WRONG_PATH`,
   264→`AMBIGUOUS`. Each tells the agent (or the human) exactly what went wrong and where.
   265→
   266→### Symbol Extraction
   267→
   268→The scanner is a lightweight single-pass line-based extractor. It handles:
   269→- Visibility modifiers: `pub`, `pub(crate)`, `pub(super)`, `pub(in path)`
   270→- Modifier keywords: `async`, `unsafe`, `extern "C"`, `const fn`
   271→- Item kinds: `struct`, `enum`, `trait`, `fn`, `type`, `const`, `static`, `mod`
   272→
   273→It deliberately does not descend into `mod foo { ... }` blocks (rare in idiomatic Rust).
   274→
   275→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/symbol_gate.rs:449–478 — `extract_symbols()`
   276→> lightweight parser.
   277→
   278→---
   279→
   280→## 7. DiffGate — Vacuous-Implementation Rejection
   281→
   282→**File**: `crates/roko-gate/src/diff_gate.rs` (358 lines)
   283→
   284→`DiffGate` solves a specific failure mode: agents that "pass" gates by producing
   285→vacuous implementations. Without this gate, an agent can replace function bodies with
   286→`todo!()` or `Ok(())` to make compile and lint gates happy while doing no actual work.
   287→
   288→### Rejection Criteria
   289→
   290→A diff is rejected when:
   291→1. **Empty diff**: Zero added lines.
   292→2. **Below threshold**: Non-whitespace added lines < `min_added_lines` (default: 1).
   293→3. **All forbidden tokens**: Every substantive added line matches a forbidden token.
   294→
   295→Default forbidden tokens:
   296→- `todo!()`, `todo!`, `unimplemented!()`, `unimplemented!`
   297→- `panic!("not implemented")`, `Ok(())`, `return Ok(())`
   298→
   299→### Analysis Output
   300→
   301→```rust
   302→pub struct DiffAnalysis {
   303→    pub added_lines: u32,
   304→    pub non_whitespace_added: u32,
   305→    pub all_added_are_forbidden: bool,
   306→}
   307→```
   308→
   309→The `analyze_diff()` function is pure: no I/O, no subprocess, just string scanning.
   310→It skips diff headers (`+++`, `---`, `@@`), counts `+` lines, filters whitespace and
   311→comments, and checks against the forbidden token list.
   312→
   313→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/diff_gate.rs:1–33 — Module doc: "Rejects changes
   314→> that 'pass' gates only because they introduced no substantive work."
   315→
   316→---
   317→
   318→## 8. Remaining Gates (Scaffold Status)
   319→
   320→### 8.1 GeneratedTestGate (Rung 4)
   321→
   322→Runs tests that were automatically generated by the agent or a dedicated test-generation
   323→agent. These are distinct from the project's existing test suite (Rung 2). Generated
   324→tests are more targeted — they specifically exercise the code the agent just wrote.
   325→
   326→### 8.2 PropertyTestGate (Rung 5)
   327→
   328→Runs property-based tests (QuickCheck / proptest style). Property tests provide stronger
   329→guarantees than example-based tests: they assert invariants over randomized inputs.
   330→
   331→### 8.3 IntegrationGate (Rung 6)
   332→
   333→Runs the full integration test suite. This is the most expensive gate — it may involve
   334→standing up services, databases, or network connections.
   335→
   336→### 8.4 LlmJudgeGate (Auxiliary)
   337→
   338→Uses an LLM to evaluate code quality, correctness, or adherence to specifications. This
   339→is the only gate that consults a model rather than a deterministic tool. It is used
   340→when properties are too nuanced for automated checking (e.g., "does this implementation
   341→match the PRD's intent?").
   342→
   343→### 8.5 VerifyChainGate (Auxiliary)
   344→
   345→Verifies chain-related artifacts. Part of the Phase 2+ architecture.
   346→
   347→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/lib.rs — All 11 gate modules declared.
   348→
   349→---
   350→
   351→## 9. Shared Patterns Across All Gates
   352→
   353→### 9.1 Builder Pattern
   354→
   355→Every gate uses a builder-style API with `with_*` methods:
   356→
   357→```rust
   358→CompileGate::cargo()
   359→    .with_extra_args(vec!["--features".into(), "ci".into()])
   360→    .with_timeout_ms(120_000)
   361→```
   362→
   363→### 9.2 GatePayload
   364→
   365→Gates that shell out to external tools read their configuration from a `GatePayload`
   366→in the signal body:
   367→
   368→```rust
   369→pub struct GatePayload {
   370→    pub working_dir: PathBuf,
   371→    pub target_dir: Option<PathBuf>,
   372→    pub extra_env: HashMap<String, String>,
   373→    // ... build system, test selector, etc.
   374→}
   375→```
   376→
   377→This decouples the gate from the orchestrator: the orchestrator constructs the signal
   378→with the right payload, and the gate reads it.
   379→
   380→### 9.3 Timeout + kill_on_drop
   381→
   382→Every subprocess-spawning gate sets `cmd.kill_on_drop(true)` and wraps execution in
   383→`tokio::time::timeout()`. This ensures:
   384→- No zombie processes from abandoned gates
   385→- Bounded execution time for every gate
   386→
   387→### 9.4 Error Summarization
   388→
   389→Each gate has a gate-specific `summarize_*` function that extracts the most relevant
   390→error lines from stderr. This keeps verdict reasons concise (3 lines) while preserving
   391→full output in `detail`.
   392→
   393→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/compile.rs:130–151 — `summarize_errors()`.
   394→> crates/roko-gate/src/test_gate.rs:244–267 — `summarize_test_failures()`.
   395→> crates/roko-gate/src/clippy_gate.rs:146–163 — `summarize_lint_issues()`.
   396→
   397→---
   398→
   399→## 10. Gate Composition
   400→
   401→Gates are designed to be composed, not used in isolation. The primary composition
   402→mechanism is the `GatePipeline` (see [03-gate-pipeline.md](./03-gate-pipeline.md)),
   403→which chains gates sequentially with optional short-circuit behavior. The `RungSelector`
   404→(see [02-6-rung-selector.md](./02-6-rung-selector.md)) determines which gates to
   405→include based on plan complexity and prior failures.
   406→
   407→The composition chain is:
   408→
   409→```
   410→RungSelector picks rungs for this plan's complexity
   411→    ↓
   412→GatePipeline composes the selected gates
   413→    ↓
   414→Each gate runs in sequence (or short-circuits on failure)
   415→    ↓
   416→Aggregated Verdict flows to ratchet, thresholds, feedback
   417→```
   418→
   419→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/gate_pipeline.rs — GatePipeline composes
   420→> `Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>` sequentially.
   421→

1→# 02 — The 7-Rung Gate Selector
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-gate` (`crates/roko-gate/src/rung_selector.rs`)
     5→> **Status**: Implemented (527 lines)
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→Not every task needs every gate. A one-line variable rename doesn't need property-based
    15→testing. A new subsystem does. The rung selector solves this: given a plan's complexity
    16→and the gates available in this environment, it produces a sorted list of rungs to
    17→execute.
    18→
    19→The system has 7 rungs, numbered 0 through 6, ordered from cheapest to most expensive.
    20→Lower-numbered rungs always run before higher-numbered ones. The selector determines
    21→*which* subset of rungs to activate based on three inputs:
    22→
    23→1. **Plan complexity** — how ambitious is this change?
    24→2. **Rung capabilities** — which gates are actually available?
    25→3. **Prior failures** — did previous attempts fail, warranting escalation?
    26→
    27→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/rung_selector.rs — Full implementation.
    28→
    29→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/tmp/mori-agents/20-verification-first-architecture.md —
    30→> 6-rung gate system design (Rungs 0–5 in the original; Roko extends to 7 with Rung 6
    31→> for integration tests).
    32→
    33→---
    34→
    35→## 2. The 7 Rungs
    36→
    37→```rust
    38→#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    39→pub enum Rung {
    40→    Compile     = 0,    // Does it compile?
    41→    Lint        = 1,    // Does it pass linting?
    42→    Test        = 2,    // Do tests pass?
    43→    Symbol      = 3,    // Are required symbols present?
    44→    GeneratedTest = 4,  // Do auto-generated tests pass?
    45→    PropertyTest  = 5,  // Do property-based tests pass?
    46→    Integration   = 6,  // Do integration tests pass?
    47→}
    48→```
    49→
    50→### 2.1 Rung 0: Compile
    51→
    52→The cheapest gate. Runs `cargo check --workspace` (or equivalent). Takes seconds.
    53→Catches syntax errors, type mismatches, missing imports. Every plan runs at least this
    54→rung.
    55→
    56→**Gate**: `CompileGate`
    57→**Cost**: Low (seconds)
    58→**False positive rate**: 0% (deterministic)
    59→
    60→### 2.2 Rung 1: Lint
    61→
    62→Runs `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` (or language equivalent). Catches code quality
    63→issues, common mistakes, and style violations. Still fast — typically under a minute.
    64→
    65→**Gate**: `ClippyGate`
    66→**Cost**: Low (seconds to one minute)
    67→**False positive rate**: 0% (deterministic)
    68→
    69→### 2.3 Rung 2: Test
    70→
    71→Runs the project's existing test suite. This is the first "medium cost" gate — tests
    72→can take minutes for large projects. Catches functional regressions.
    73→
    74→**Gate**: `TestGate`
    75→**Cost**: Medium (seconds to 15 minutes)
    76→**False positive rate**: 0% for deterministic tests; flaky tests exist but are not
    77→the gate's fault.
    78→
    79→### 2.4 Rung 3: Symbol
    80→
    81→Verifies that required symbols (structs, traits, functions) exist with correct kind,
    82→visibility, and module path. Zero cost — no subprocess, just file walking and regex
    83→matching.
    84→
    85→**Gate**: `SymbolGate`
    86→**Cost**: Near-zero (file I/O only)
    87→**False positive rate**: 0% (deterministic)
    88→
    89→Despite being "free," Symbol is ranked after Test because the information it provides
    90→(symbol existence) is a subset of what compile + test already verify. Symbol is most
    91→valuable when it catches issues *before* a failed compile-test cycle reveals them.
    92→
    93→### 2.5 Rung 4: GeneratedTest
    94→
    95→Runs tests that were automatically generated to exercise the agent's output. These are
    96→more targeted than the project's existing tests — they specifically test the new code.
    97→
    98→**Gate**: `GeneratedTestGate`
    99→**Cost**: High (test generation + execution)
   100→**False positive rate**: Moderate (generated tests may be incorrect)
   101→
   102→### 2.6 Rung 5: PropertyTest
   103→
   104→Runs property-based tests (QuickCheck / proptest). These assert invariants over
   105→randomized inputs, providing stronger guarantees than example-based tests.
   106→
   107→**Gate**: `PropertyTestGate`
   108→**Cost**: High (randomized exploration)
   109→**False positive rate**: Low (invariants are precise)
   110→
   111→### 2.7 Rung 6: Integration
   112→
   113→The most expensive gate. Runs full integration tests that may involve external services,
   114→databases, or network connections.
   115→
   116→**Gate**: `IntegrationGate`
   117→**Cost**: Highest (minutes to hours)
   118→**False positive rate**: Moderate (infrastructure flakiness)
   119→
   120→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/rung_selector.rs — `Rung` enum definition with
   121→> discriminants 0–6.
   122→
   123→---
   124→
   125→## 3. Plan Complexity
   126→
   127→The selector's primary input is `PlanComplexity`, which classifies the scope of the
   128→planned change:
   129→
   130→```rust
   131→#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   132→pub enum PlanComplexity {
   133→    Trivial,    // Rename, typo fix, config change
   134→    Simple,     // Single function change, small bug fix
   135→    Standard,   // Multi-file feature, medium scope
   136→    Complex,    // New subsystem, architectural change
   137→}
   138→```
   139→
   140→### 3.1 Complexity → Rung Mapping
   141→
   142→Each complexity level implies a baseline set of rungs:
   143→
   144→| Complexity | Baseline Rungs | Rationale |
   145→|---|---|---|
   146→| `Trivial` | Compile only (0) | No functional changes; compile is sufficient |
   147→| `Simple` | Compile + Lint (0–1) | Small changes; lint catches quality issues |
   148→| `Standard` | Compile + Lint + Test + Symbol (0–3) | Feature work needs test coverage |
   149→| `Complex` | All available (0–6) | Architectural changes need full verification |
   150→
   151→### 3.2 Escalation on Failure
   152→
   153→When a plan fails a gate, the complexity escalates:
   154→
   155→```rust
   156→impl PlanComplexity {
   157→    pub fn escalate(&self) -> Self {
   158→        match self {
   159→            Self::Trivial => Self::Simple,
   160→            Self::Simple => Self::Standard,
   161→            Self::Standard => Self::Complex,
   162→            Self::Complex => Self::Complex,  // Already maximal
   163→        }
   164→    }
   165→}
   166→```
   167→
   168→Escalation adds more rungs on retry. A `Trivial` plan that fails compile gets escalated
   169→to `Simple` (adding lint). A `Simple` plan that fails lint gets escalated to `Standard`
   170→(adding test + symbol). This captures the heuristic: if the easy checks catch a problem,
   171→the change is more complex than initially classified, and deeper verification is
   172→warranted.
   173→
   174→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/rung_selector.rs — `PlanComplexity::escalate()`
   175→> method.
   176→
   177→---
   178→
   179→## 4. Rung Capabilities
   180→
   181→Not every environment has every gate available. Some projects don't have a linter
   182→configured. Some don't have property tests. The `RungCaps` struct records which rungs
   183→can actually run:
   184→
   185→```rust
   186→pub struct RungCaps {
   187→    pub compile: bool,
   188→    pub lint: bool,
   189→    pub test: bool,
   190→    pub symbol: bool,
   191→    pub generated_test: bool,
   192→    pub property_test: bool,
   193→    pub integration: bool,
   194→}
   195→```
   196→
   197→The selector intersects the complexity-implied rungs with the available capabilities.
   198→If the complexity says "run through Rung 3" but `symbol` is `false`, the symbol rung is
   199→skipped.
   200→
   201→---
   202→
   203→## 5. The `select_rungs()` Function
   204→
   205→The public API combines all three inputs:
   206→
   207→```rust
   208→pub fn select_rungs(
   209→    complexity: PlanComplexity,
   210→    caps: &RungCaps,
   211→    prior_failures: u32,
   212→) -> Vec<Rung>
   213→```
   214→
   215→The algorithm:
   216→
   217→1. **Determine effective complexity**: If `prior_failures > 0`, escalate complexity
   218→   by `prior_failures` levels (capped at `Complex`).
   219→2. **Map complexity to maximum rung**: Trivial → 0, Simple → 1, Standard → 3,
   220→   Complex → 6.
   221→3. **Collect all rungs ≤ maximum** that are available in `caps`.
   222→4. **Sort ascending** (cheapest first).
   223→5. **Return the rung list**.
   224→
   225→### Example
   226→
   227→```
   228→Input:
   229→  complexity = Simple
   230→  caps = { compile: true, lint: true, test: true, symbol: false, ... }
   231→  prior_failures = 1
   232→
   233→Step 1: Escalate Simple by 1 → Standard
   234→Step 2: Standard → max rung 3
   235→Step 3: Available rungs ≤ 3 = [Compile(0), Lint(1), Test(2)]
   236→         (Symbol(3) excluded because caps.symbol = false)
   237→Step 4: Already sorted
   238→Result: [Compile, Lint, Test]
   239→```
   240→
   241→---
   242→
   243→## 6. Relationship to the Gate Pipeline
   244→
   245→The rung selector produces a `Vec<Rung>`. The orchestrator maps each `Rung` to a
   246→concrete gate implementation and feeds them into a `GatePipeline`:
   247→
   248→```
   249→RungSelector::select_rungs(complexity, caps, failures)
   250→    → Vec<Rung>
   251→    → map each Rung to Box<dyn Gate>
   252→    → GatePipeline::new(gates).with_short_circuit(true)
   253→    → pipeline.verify(signal, ctx)
   254→    → aggregated Verdict
   255→```
   256→
   257→This separation of concerns means the selector knows nothing about gate implementations
   258→and the pipeline knows nothing about complexity. Each can evolve independently.
   259→
   260→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/gate_pipeline.rs — GatePipeline accepts
   261→> `Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>` without knowing which rungs they represent.
   262→
   263→---
   264→
   265→## 7. The Verification-First Architecture
   266→
   267→The rung system embodies the verification-first architecture from the Mori reference
   268→system. The core insight is:
   269→
   270→**Cheap verification gates that run first prevent expensive retries.**
   271→
   272→If the code doesn't compile (Rung 0), there is no point running tests (Rung 2). If
   273→the code has lint violations (Rung 1), many of those violations will also cause test
   274→failures. Running gates in ascending cost order with short-circuit behavior means the
   275→system spends the minimum possible time and compute on verification before either
   276→passing or identifying the cheapest-to-fix failure.
   277→
   278→The cost savings compound:
   279→- A compile failure caught in 3 seconds saves a 15-minute test run.
   280→- A lint failure caught in 10 seconds saves the same 15-minute test run.
   281→- A symbol check caught in 50ms saves a 3-second compile + 10-second lint + 15-minute
   282→  test run (though symbol is ranked after test in the default ordering).
   283→
   284→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/tmp/mori-agents/20-verification-first-architecture.md —
   285→> "6-rung gate system: separate test generation from implementation, tests must fail
   286→> before implementation, immutable verification artifacts."
   287→
   288→---
   289→
   290→## 8. Escalation in Practice
   291→
   292→The escalation mechanism creates a feedback loop between gate outcomes and verification
   293→intensity:
   294→
   295→```
   296→Attempt 1: Trivial complexity → Compile only
   297→  → Compile fails
   298→  → Escalate to Simple (prior_failures = 1)
   299→
   300→Attempt 2: Simple complexity → Compile + Lint
   301→  → Both pass
   302→  → Record pass at Rung 1
   303→  → Ratchet prevents regression below Rung 1
   304→
   305→Attempt 3 (if task retry): Simple complexity → Compile + Lint
   306→  → Lint fails
   307→  → Escalate to Standard (prior_failures = 2)
   308→
   309→Attempt 4: Standard complexity → Compile + Lint + Test + Symbol
   310→  → All pass
   311→  → Record pass at Rung 3
   312→  → Task verified
   313→```
   314→
   315→This means the system starts cheap and escalates only when warranted. A plan that
   316→passes on the first attempt at Trivial complexity uses only the compile gate —
   317→sub-second verification. A plan that fails repeatedly gets the full battery.
   318→
   319→---
   320→
   321→## 9. Rung Ordering vs. Rung Cost
   322→
   323→The rung numbers (0–6) encode a rough cost ordering, but the actual cost depends on the
   324→project:
   325→
   326→| Rung | Typical Cost | Notes |
   327→|---|---|---|
   328→| 0 (Compile) | 1–10 seconds | Incremental builds are faster |
   329→| 1 (Lint) | 2–60 seconds | Clippy can be slow on large codebases |
   330→| 2 (Test) | 5 seconds – 15 minutes | Depends on test count |
   331→| 3 (Symbol) | 10–100 ms | Pure file I/O, no subprocess |
   332→| 4 (GeneratedTest) | 30 seconds – 5 minutes | Depends on generation + execution |
   333→| 5 (PropertyTest) | 10 seconds – 10 minutes | Depends on iterations |
   334→| 6 (Integration) | 1 minute – 1 hour | Depends on infrastructure |
   335→
   336→The rung numbers represent a logical ordering (compile before test before integration),
   337→not a strict cost ordering. Symbol (Rung 3) is actually cheaper than Compile (Rung 0),
   338→but logically sits after Test because its value is in catching issues that compile + test
   339→miss.
   340→
   341→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/02-five-layers.md — Layer 3 (Harness): "scoring,
   342→> pattern detection, interventions, process rewards, adaptive gating."
   343→
   344→---
   345→
   346→## 10. Configuration and Extension
   347→
   348→### 10.1 Adding a New Rung
   349→
   350→To add a Rung 7 (e.g., "SecurityAudit"):
   351→1. Add `SecurityAudit = 7` to the `Rung` enum.
   352→2. Add `security_audit: bool` to `RungCaps`.
   353→3. Update `select_rungs()` to include Rung 7 for `Complex` plans.
   354→4. Implement a `SecurityAuditGate` in a new module.
   355→5. Map `Rung::SecurityAudit` to `SecurityAuditGate` in the orchestrator.
   356→
   357→### 10.2 Configuring per-Project Capabilities
   358→
   359→The `RungCaps` struct is constructed by the orchestrator based on project detection:
   360→- If `Cargo.toml` exists → `compile: true`, `lint: true`, `test: true`
   361→- If `roko.toml` specifies `[gates.symbol]` → `symbol: true`
   362→- If property test fixtures exist → `property_test: true`
   363→
   364→This means the selector adapts to the project automatically — no configuration needed
   365→for basic verification.
   366→
   367→---
   368→
   369→## 11. Relationship to Adaptive Thresholds
   370→
   371→The adaptive threshold system (see [06-adaptive-thresholds.md](./06-adaptive-thresholds.md))
   372→tracks per-rung pass rates. If a rung consistently passes (20+ consecutive passes), the
   373→threshold system may recommend skipping it (advisory only). This provides a
   374→runtime-adaptive complement to the static complexity-based selection.
   375→
   376→The two systems compose: the rung selector determines the *baseline* set of rungs, and
   377→the adaptive thresholds *refine* it based on historical performance.
   378→
   379→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/adaptive_threshold.rs — `should_skip_rung()`
   380→> advisory based on consecutive pass streak.
   381→

1→# 03 — The Gate Pipeline
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-gate` (`crates/roko-gate/src/gate_pipeline.rs`)
     5→> **Status**: Implemented (593 lines), wired into orchestrate.rs
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→The `GatePipeline` composes multiple gates into a single verification step. It accepts a
    15→`Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>`, runs them sequentially, and produces an aggregated `Verdict`. It
    16→implements the `Gate` trait itself, so a pipeline can be nested inside another pipeline
    17→or used anywhere a single gate is expected.
    18→
    19→This is the primary mechanism for turning the rung selector's output (a list of rungs)
    20→into a concrete verification execution.
    21→
    22→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/gate_pipeline.rs — Full implementation.
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## 2. Structure
    27→
    28→```rust
    29→pub struct GatePipeline {
    30→    gates: Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>,
    31→    short_circuit: bool,
    32→    name: String,
    33→}
    34→```
    35→
    36→| Field | Purpose |
    37→|---|---|
    38→| `gates` | Ordered list of gates to execute |
    39→| `short_circuit` | If true, stop on first failure |
    40→| `name` | Display name for the pipeline's own verdict |
    41→
    42→### Construction
    43→
    44→```rust
    45→GatePipeline::new(vec![
    46→    Box::new(CompileGate::cargo()),
    47→    Box::new(ClippyGate::cargo()),
    48→    Box::new(TestGate::cargo()),
    49→])
    50→.with_short_circuit(true)
    51→.with_name("rung-pipeline")
    52→```
    53→
    54→The builder pattern follows the same convention as individual gates: `with_*` methods
    55→return `Self` for chaining.
    56→
    57→---
    58→
    59→## 3. Short-Circuit vs. Full Execution
    60→
    61→### 3.1 Short-Circuit Mode (`short_circuit: true`)
    62→
    63→The default and most common mode. The pipeline stops at the first gate that fails and
    64→returns a failure verdict immediately. This is the correct behavior for the rung
    65→pipeline: if compile fails, there is no point running lint or tests.
    66→
    67→```
    68→CompileGate → FAIL → stop → return Verdict::fail(...)
    69→              (ClippyGate and TestGate never run)
    70→```
    71→
    72→**Why this matters**: In a 7-rung pipeline where integration tests take 30 minutes, a
    73→compile failure caught in 3 seconds saves 30+ minutes of wasted compute. Short-circuit
    74→mode is the mechanism that makes the verification-first architecture efficient.
    75→
    76→### 3.2 Full Execution Mode (`short_circuit: false`)
    77→
    78→All gates run regardless of individual outcomes. The final verdict is a failure if *any*
    79→gate failed. This mode is useful when you want a comprehensive report of all issues
    80→rather than just the first.
    81→
    82→```
    83→CompileGate → FAIL → continue
    84→ClippyGate  → PASS → continue
    85→TestGate    → FAIL → continue
    86→→ return aggregated Verdict::fail(...)
    87→  (detail includes output from all three gates)
    88→```
    89→
    90→---
    91→
    92→## 4. Verdict Aggregation
    93→
    94→The pipeline's `verify()` method aggregates individual verdicts into a single
    95→pipeline-level verdict:
    96→
    97→### 4.1 Pass Condition
    98→
    99→The pipeline passes if and only if **every** gate passes. A single failure anywhere in
   100→the chain makes the whole pipeline fail.
   101→
   102→### 4.2 Detail Aggregation
   103→
   104→Individual gate outputs are concatenated into the pipeline's `detail` field, separated
   105→by headers:
   106→
   107→```
   108→--- [compile:cargo] ---
   109→Compiling foo v0.1.0
   110→Finished dev in 2.3s
   111→
   112→--- [clippy:cargo] ---
   113→warning: unused variable
   114→
   115→--- [test:cargo] ---
   116→test result: ok. 12 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored
   117→```
   118→
   119→This gives the caller (and the agent) a complete view of what happened at each
   120→verification step.
   121→
   122→### 4.3 Reason Construction
   123→
   124→On failure, the pipeline's `reason` field lists which gate(s) failed:
   125→
   126→```
   127→gate pipeline failed: compile:cargo (error: bad thing; error[E0425]: symbol not found)
   128→```
   129→
   130→In short-circuit mode, there is exactly one failed gate. In full-execution mode, there
   131→may be multiple.
   132→
   133→### 4.4 Duration
   134→
   135→The pipeline's `duration_ms` is the sum of all individual gate durations. This tracks
   136→total wall-clock time spent on verification.
   137→
   138→### 4.5 Test Count Merging
   139→
   140→If any gate in the pipeline produces `TestCount` (test gates do), the pipeline merges
   141→them by summing passed, failed, and ignored counts across all gates. This is relevant
   142→when a pipeline contains multiple test gates (e.g., unit tests + integration tests).
   143→
   144→---
   145→
   146→## 5. The Pipeline as a Gate
   147→
   148→`GatePipeline` implements `Gate`:
   149→
   150→```rust
   151→#[async_trait]
   152→impl Gate for GatePipeline {
   153→    async fn verify(&self, signal: &Signal, ctx: &Context) -> Verdict {
   154→        // ... iterate over self.gates, aggregate verdicts
   155→    }
   156→
   157→    fn name(&self) -> &str {
   158→        &self.name
   159→    }
   160→}
   161→```
   162→
   163→This composability is intentional. It means:
   164→- A pipeline can contain other pipelines (nesting)
   165→- Any code that accepts `&dyn Gate` can accept a pipeline
   166→- The adaptive threshold system, ratchet, and feedback systems all work with pipelines
   167→  without special-casing
   168→
   169→---
   170→
   171→## 6. Execution Flow
   172→
   173→```
   174→Pipeline::verify(signal, ctx)
   175→│
   176→├─ gate[0].verify(signal, ctx) → verdict_0
   177→│  ├─ if failed && short_circuit → return fail verdict
   178→│  └─ collect detail, test counts
   179→│
   180→├─ gate[1].verify(signal, ctx) → verdict_1
   181→│  ├─ if failed && short_circuit → return fail verdict
   182→│  └─ collect detail, test counts
   183→│
   184→├─ ... (for each gate)
   185→│
   186→└─ aggregate:
   187→   ├─ passed = all verdicts passed
   188→   ├─ reason = join failure reasons
   189→   ├─ detail = join all details with headers
   190→   ├─ duration = sum of durations
   191→   ├─ test_count = sum of test counts
   192→   └─ return aggregated Verdict
   193→```
   194→
   195→---
   196→
   197→## 7. How the Orchestrator Uses the Pipeline
   198→
   199→In `crates/roko-cli/src/orchestrate.rs`, the orchestrator constructs a pipeline per task:
   200→
   201→```
   202→1. Determine plan complexity (Trivial/Simple/Standard/Complex)
   203→2. Detect environment capabilities (which build tools exist)
   204→3. select_rungs(complexity, caps, prior_failures)
   205→4. Map each Rung to a concrete Box<dyn Gate>
   206→5. GatePipeline::new(gates).with_short_circuit(true)
   207→6. pipeline.verify(signal, ctx)
   208→7. Feed verdict to ratchet, thresholds, feedback
   209→```
   210→
   211→The pipeline is constructed fresh for each task execution. This means:
   212→- Different tasks can have different pipelines (based on complexity)
   213→- Escalation adds gates to the pipeline on retry
   214→- The pipeline is lightweight — no persistent state
   215→
   216→---
   217→
   218→## 8. Error Handling Within the Pipeline
   219→
   220→Because the Gate trait returns `Verdict` (not `Result<Verdict>`), the pipeline never
   221→has to handle gate errors. Every gate handles its own infrastructure failures internally.
   222→The pipeline simply collects verdicts and aggregates them.
   223→
   224→This is the practical benefit of the `-> Verdict` design decision: composition is
   225→trivial. There are no error propagation paths to worry about, no `?` operators, no
   226→`Result::map` chains. Just: run the gate, get a verdict, check if it passed.
   227→
   228→> **Citation**: 00-gate-trait.md — "Gate failure is not an error — it is a verdict."
   229→
   230→---
   231→
   232→## 9. Pipeline Lifecycle in the Universal Loop
   233→
   234→```
   235→Universal loop: query → score → route → compose → act → VERIFY → write → react
   236→                                                        ^^^^^^^^
   237→                                                   GatePipeline lives here
   238→
   239→Signal produced by agent (act step)
   240→    ↓
   241→GatePipeline.verify(signal, ctx)
   242→    ↓
   243→Verdict flows to:
   244→    ├─ write: Verdict persisted as signal in Substrate
   245→    ├─ react: GateRatchet.record_pass(plan_id, rung)
   246→    ├─ react: AdaptiveThresholds.update(rung, passed)
   247→    ├─ react: GateFeedback for agent context on retry
   248→    ├─ react: EfficiencyEvent for learning
   249→    └─ react: CascadeRouter.update_arm(model, reward)
   250→```
   251→
   252→The pipeline is the single point where all these downstream systems get their input.
   253→This centralization means there's one place to add new feedback consumers, one place
   254→to add instrumentation, and one place to add logging.
   255→
   256→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/01-synapse-architecture.md — Cybernetic feedback loops
   257→> from Gate to Scorer, Router, Composer.
   258→
   259→---
   260→
   261→## 10. Testing the Pipeline
   262→
   263→The pipeline has extensive tests in `gate_pipeline.rs`:
   264→
   265→| Test | What It Verifies |
   266→|---|---|
   267→| `pipeline_empty_passes` | Empty pipeline returns pass verdict |
   268→| `pipeline_single_pass` | Single passing gate → pass |
   269→| `pipeline_single_fail` | Single failing gate → fail |
   270→| `pipeline_short_circuits` | Stops at first failure |
   271→| `pipeline_full_execution` | Runs all gates when short_circuit=false |
   272→| `pipeline_aggregates_test_counts` | Merges test counts across gates |
   273→| `pipeline_detail_headers` | Detail output has per-gate headers |
   274→| `pipeline_duration_sums` | Total duration = sum of gate durations |
   275→
   276→These tests use mock gates that return predetermined verdicts, avoiding the need for
   277→actual subprocess spawning in unit tests.
   278→
   279→---
   280→
   281→## 11. Relationship to Other Components
   282→
   283→| Component | Relationship |
   284→|---|---|
   285→| `RungSelector` | Determines which gates go into the pipeline |
   286→| `GateRatchet` | Consumes pipeline verdicts to track regression |
   287→| `AdaptiveThresholds` | Consumes pipeline verdicts for per-rung EMA |
   288→| `GateFeedback` | Parses pipeline detail output for agent context |
   289→| `ArtifactStore` | Future: stores pipeline artifacts content-addressed |
   290→| Orchestrator | Constructs and executes the pipeline per task |
   291→
   292→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/gate_pipeline.rs — Tests demonstrating pipeline
   293→> behavior.
   294→
   295→---
   296→
   297→## 12. Design Rationale: Sequential, Not Parallel
   298→
   299→The current pipeline executes gates sequentially. This is deliberate:
   300→
   301→1. **Dependency ordering**: Rung N often depends on Rung N-1's success. Running tests
   302→   on code that doesn't compile wastes time and produces confusing errors.
   303→2. **Short-circuit value**: Sequential execution enables short-circuit, which is the
   304→   pipeline's primary optimization.
   305→3. **Simplicity**: Sequential execution has no synchronization concerns.
   306→
   307→Future: A "gate group" concept where independent gates within the same rung run in
   308→parallel. For example, if a rung has both a symbol gate and a format gate (both zero
   309→cost, no dependency between them), they could run concurrently. This would be a new
   310→composition primitive, not a change to the pipeline's sequential semantics.
   311→
   312→---
   313→
   314→## 13. Gate Composition Algebra
   315→
   316→The pipeline's sequential composition is one combinator. A complete algebra over gates
   317→enables richer verification topologies — parallel fan-outs, voting, fallback chains, and
   318→confidence-weighted verdicts. The algebra treats `Gate` as the base type and defines
   319→combinators that produce new gates from existing ones.
   320→
   321→> **Citation**: Foundational Property-Based Testing (Paraskevopoulou & Hritcu) — formal
   322→> compositional structures over verification predicates.
   323→
   324→### 13.1 Verdict Lattice
   325→
   326→Verdicts form a bounded lattice ordered by severity:
   327→
   328→```
   329→Skip < Warn < Pass < Fail
   330→
   331→identity:  Skip  (a gate that produces Skip has no effect)
   332→absorber:  Fail  (once present, dominates any merge)
   333→merge(v1, v2) = max(v1, v2)   -- most severe verdict wins
   334→```
   335→
   336→This gives a monoid over verdicts: `(Verdict, merge, Skip)`. Every combinator preserves
