   337→this structure — the composed gate always returns a single `Verdict` from the same lattice.
   338→
   339→```rust
   340→/// Extended verdict with a confidence interval, not just pass/fail.
   341→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, PartialOrd)]
   342→pub enum VerdictSeverity {
   343→    Skip = 0,
   344→    Warn = 1,
   345→    Pass = 2,
   346→    Fail = 3,
   347→}
   348→
   349→impl VerdictSeverity {
   350→    pub fn merge(self, other: Self) -> Self {
   351→        if self as u8 >= other as u8 { self } else { other }
   352→    }
   353→}
   354→```
   355→
   356→### 13.2 Combinators
   357→
   358→```rust
   359→/// Sequential composition: g1 THEN g2 (short-circuit on fail).
   360→/// This is what GatePipeline already does.
   361→pub struct Sequential(Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>);
   362→
   363→/// Parallel composition: run all gates concurrently, merge verdicts.
   364→/// Independent gates (e.g., SymbolGate + DiffGate) run simultaneously.
   365→pub struct Parallel(Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>);
   366→
   367→/// Fallback: try g1; if it fails, try g2 instead.
   368→/// Useful for degraded environments (e.g., no clippy → fall back to grep-based lint).
   369→pub struct Fallback(Box<dyn Gate>, Box<dyn Gate>);
   370→
   371→/// Voting: run N gates, pass if >= K pass (quorum).
   372→/// Useful for LLM judge panels where individual judges may disagree.
   373→pub struct Voting {
   374→    gates: Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>,
   375→    quorum: usize, // minimum passes required
   376→}
   377→
   378→/// Weighted: scale a gate's confidence by a factor.
   379→/// Low-confidence gates (LLM judge) contribute less to aggregate decisions.
   380→pub struct Weighted {
   381→    gate: Box<dyn Gate>,
   382→    weight: f64, // [0.0, 1.0]
   383→}
   384→
   385→/// Threshold: pass only if gate's score exceeds a minimum.
   386→/// Converts continuous scores into binary verdicts at a chosen cut-point.
   387→pub struct Threshold {
   388→    gate: Box<dyn Gate>,
   389→    min_score: f32, // gate.score must be >= this
   390→}
   391→```
   392→
   393→### 13.3 Parallel Gate Group
   394→
   395→The `Parallel` combinator enables concurrent execution of independent gates within
   396→a single rung:
   397→
   398→```rust
   399→#[async_trait]
   400→impl Gate for Parallel {
   401→    async fn verify(&self, signal: &Signal, ctx: &Context) -> Verdict {
   402→        let futures: Vec<_> = self.0.iter()
   403→            .map(|g| g.verify(signal, ctx))
   404→            .collect();
   405→        let verdicts = futures::future::join_all(futures).await;
   406→
   407→        let passed = verdicts.iter().all(|v| v.passed);
   408→        let duration = verdicts.iter().map(|v| v.duration_ms).max().unwrap_or(0);
   409→        let test_count = merge_test_counts(&verdicts);
   410→        let detail = verdicts.iter()
   411→            .map(|v| format!("--- [{}] ---\n{}", v.gate, v.detail.as_deref().unwrap_or("")))
   412→            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
   413→            .join("\n\n");
   414→
   415→        Verdict {
   416→            passed,
   417→            gate: "parallel-group".into(),
   418→            reason: if passed { "all gates passed".into() }
   419→                    else { format!("{} gate(s) failed",
   420→                        verdicts.iter().filter(|v| !v.passed).count()) },
   421→            detail: Some(detail),
   422→            duration_ms: duration, // wall-clock = max, not sum
   423→            test_count,
   424→            ..Default::default()
   425→        }
   426→    }
   427→}
   428→```
   429→
   430→Key difference: duration is `max(durations)` not `sum(durations)`, because gates run
   431→concurrently. A `Parallel(SymbolGate, DiffGate)` taking 50ms and 20ms respectively
   432→completes in 50ms, not 70ms.
   433→
   434→### 13.4 Voting Gate (Quorum)
   435→
   436→For subjective gates (LLM judges, heuristic checks), a single verdict is noisy. A
   437→voting gate runs multiple judges and requires a quorum:
   438→
   439→```rust
   440→#[async_trait]
   441→impl Gate for Voting {
   442→    async fn verify(&self, signal: &Signal, ctx: &Context) -> Verdict {
   443→        let futures: Vec<_> = self.gates.iter()
   444→            .map(|g| g.verify(signal, ctx))
   445→            .collect();
   446→        let verdicts = futures::future::join_all(futures).await;
   447→
   448→        let pass_count = verdicts.iter().filter(|v| v.passed).count();
   449→        let passed = pass_count >= self.quorum;
   450→        let avg_score = verdicts.iter().map(|v| v.score).sum::<f32>()
   451→            / verdicts.len() as f32;
   452→
   453→        Verdict {
   454→            passed,
   455→            score: avg_score,
   456→            gate: "voting-panel".into(),
   457→            reason: format!("{}/{} passed (quorum: {})",
   458→                pass_count, verdicts.len(), self.quorum),
   459→            ..Default::default()
   460→        }
   461→    }
   462→}
   463→```
   464→
   465→### 13.5 Composition Examples
   466→
   467→```rust
   468→// Standard 4-rung pipeline (current behavior)
   469→Sequential(vec![CompileGate, ClippyGate, TestGate, SymbolGate])
   470→
   471→// Parallel lint: clippy + format check run concurrently
   472→Sequential(vec![
   473→    CompileGate,
   474→    Parallel(vec![ClippyGate, FormatGate]),
   475→    TestGate,
   476→])
   477→
   478→// LLM judge panel: 3 cheap judges, pass if 2+ agree
   479→Voting { gates: vec![Judge1, Judge2, Judge3], quorum: 2 }
   480→
   481→// Degraded environment: try clippy, fall back to grep-based lint
   482→Fallback(ClippyGate, GrepLintGate)
   483→
   484→// Progressive: compile → (clippy ∥ diff) → test → (symbol ∥ generated) → property → integration
   485→Sequential(vec![
   486→    CompileGate,
   487→    Parallel(vec![ClippyGate, DiffGate]),
   488→    TestGate,
   489→    Parallel(vec![SymbolGate, GeneratedTestGate]),
   490→    PropertyTestGate,
   491→    IntegrationGate,
   492→])
   493→```
   494→
   495→---
   496→
   497→## 14. Probabilistic Gates
   498→
   499→Standard gates return binary pass/fail. Probabilistic gates return a confidence
   500→interval — a range `[lower, upper]` expressing how certain the gate is about its verdict.
   501→This is essential for gates that use sampling (property-based tests, fuzz tests) where
   502→the result is inherently statistical.
   503→
   504→> **Citation**: Sequential Analysis (Siegmund, Springer) — confidence-interval stopping
   505→> rules. Wilson score interval for proportion estimation with small samples.
   506→
   507→### 14.1 Confidence Interval Structure
   508→
   509→```rust
   510→/// A verdict with statistical confidence bounds.
   511→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   512→pub struct ProbabilisticVerdict {
   513→    /// The point estimate of the pass rate.
   514→    pub pass_rate: f64,
   515→    /// Lower bound of the confidence interval.
   516→    pub ci_lower: f64,
   517→    /// Upper bound of the confidence interval.
   518→    pub ci_upper: f64,
   519→    /// Confidence level (e.g., 0.95 for 95% CI).
   520→    pub confidence_level: f64,
   521→    /// Number of samples taken.
   522→    pub sample_count: u64,
   523→    /// Whether the gate passed at the chosen threshold.
   524→    pub passed: bool,
   525→    /// Standard Verdict for pipeline compatibility.
   526→    pub verdict: Verdict,
   527→}
   528→```
   529→
   530→### 14.2 Wilson Score Interval
   531→
   532→The Wilson score interval is preferred over the naive Wald interval because it is
   533→well-calibrated even at small sample sizes and near boundary proportions (p ≈ 0 or 1):
   534→
   535→```rust
   536→/// Compute Wilson score confidence interval for a proportion.
   537→///
   538→/// Parameters:
   539→///   successes: number of passing tests
   540→///   total: total number of tests run
   541→///   z: z-score for desired confidence (1.96 for 95%, 2.576 for 99%)
   542→///
   543→/// Returns: (lower_bound, upper_bound)
   544→fn wilson_interval(successes: u64, total: u64, z: f64) -> (f64, f64) {
   545→    let n = total as f64;
   546→    let p_hat = successes as f64 / n;
   547→    let z2 = z * z;
   548→
   549→    let denominator = 1.0 + z2 / n;
   550→    let center = (p_hat + z2 / (2.0 * n)) / denominator;
   551→    let margin = (z / denominator)
   552→        * ((p_hat * (1.0 - p_hat) / n) + (z2 / (4.0 * n * n))).sqrt();
   553→
   554→    ((center - margin).max(0.0), (center + margin).min(1.0))
   555→}
   556→```
   557→
   558→### 14.3 Sequential Stopping Rule
   559→
   560→Property-based tests and fuzz tests can use sequential hypothesis testing to stop
   561→early when the outcome is statistically clear, rather than running a fixed number
   562→of iterations:
   563→
   564→```rust
   565→/// Sequential probabilistic gate that stops when confidence is sufficient.
   566→pub struct SequentialPropertyGate {
   567→    /// Property to test.
   568→    pub property: Box<dyn PropertyFn>,
   569→    /// Desired confidence level.
   570→    pub confidence: f64,         // default: 0.95
   571→    /// Acceptable failure rate.
   572→    pub acceptable_error: f64,   // default: 0.01 (1%)
   573→    /// Maximum iterations before giving up.
   574→    pub max_iterations: u64,     // default: 10_000
   575→    /// z-score for the confidence level (precomputed).
   576→    pub z_score: f64,            // 1.96 for 95%
   577→}
   578→
   579→/// Pseudocode for sequential verification:
   580→///
   581→/// n = 0, pass_count = 0
   582→/// loop:
   583→///     input = generate_random()
   584→///     if property(input):
   585→///         pass_count += 1
   586→///     n += 1
   587→///
   588→///     (lower, upper) = wilson_interval(pass_count, n, z_score)
   589→///
   590→///     if lower > (1.0 - acceptable_error):
   591→///         return Pass with confidence=lower
   592→///         // CI lower bound exceeds threshold → statistically confident it passes
   593→///
   594→///     if upper < (1.0 - acceptable_error):
   595→///         return Fail with confidence=(1.0 - upper)
   596→///         // CI upper bound below threshold → statistically confident it fails
   597→///
   598→///     if n >= max_iterations:
   599→///         return Warn with confidence=pass_count/n
   600→///         // Inconclusive after max iterations
   601→```
   602→
   603→### 14.4 Fuzz Gate with Probabilistic Bounds
   604→
   605→```rust
   606→/// Coverage-guided fuzzing as a probabilistic gate.
   607→pub struct FuzzGate {
   608→    /// Fuzz target name (cargo-fuzz target).
   609→    pub target: String,
   610→    /// Maximum wall-clock duration.
   611→    pub max_duration: Duration,      // default: 30s
   612→    /// Corpus directory for seed inputs.
   613→    pub corpus_dir: Option<PathBuf>,
   614→    /// Minimum executions before declaring pass.
   615→    pub min_executions: u64,         // default: 1_000
   616→}
   617→
   618→/// FuzzGate returns a probabilistic verdict:
   619→///
   620→/// result = cargo_fuzz_run(target, max_time=duration, corpus=corpus_dir)
   621→/// if result.crashes > 0:
   622→///     return Fail(
   623→///         crashes=result.crashes,
   624→///         minimized_inputs=result.artifacts,
   625→///         confidence=1.0  // crash is definitive
   626→///     )
   627→/// confidence = 1.0 - (1.0 / result.total_runs as f64)
   628→/// // With N executions and 0 crashes, P(no bug) ≈ 1 - 1/N
   629→/// return Pass(
   630→///     executions=result.total_runs,
   631→///     coverage_delta=result.new_edges,
   632→///     confidence=confidence,
   633→/// )
   634→```
   635→
   636→### 14.5 Probabilistic Verdict → Standard Verdict
   637→
   638→For pipeline compatibility, every probabilistic verdict converts to a standard `Verdict`:
   639→
   640→```rust
   641→impl From<ProbabilisticVerdict> for Verdict {
   642→    fn from(pv: ProbabilisticVerdict) -> Self {
   643→        Verdict {
   644→            passed: pv.passed,
   645→            score: pv.ci_lower as f32, // conservative: use lower bound
   646→            gate: pv.verdict.gate,
   647→            reason: format!(
   648→                "pass_rate={:.3} CI=[{:.3}, {:.3}] ({}% confidence, n={})",
   649→                pv.pass_rate, pv.ci_lower, pv.ci_upper,
   650→                (pv.confidence_level * 100.0) as u32, pv.sample_count
   651→            ),
   652→            detail: pv.verdict.detail,
   653→            duration_ms: pv.verdict.duration_ms,
   654→            test_count: pv.verdict.test_count,
   655→            error_digest: pv.verdict.error_digest,
   656→        }
   657→    }
   658→}
   659→```
   660→
   661→The `score` field uses the lower bound of the CI — the most conservative estimate.
   662→This means the downstream adaptive threshold and process reward systems operate on
   663→worst-case estimates, not optimistic point estimates.
   664→
   665→---
   666→
   667→## 15. Progressive Delivery Pipeline
   668→
   669→Adapted from canary deployment strategies (Argo Rollouts, Flagger), a progressive
   670→delivery pipeline increases verification depth in phases, with automatic rollback
   671→on failure at any phase.
   672→
   673→> **Citation**: "Progressive Delivery in CI/CD Pipelines" (IJISAE, 2024) — canary,
   674→> blue-green, and feature-flag strategies in production CI/CD.
   675→
   676→### 15.1 Phase Structure
   677→
   678→```rust
   679→/// A progressive gate pipeline that increases verification depth in stages.
   680→pub struct ProgressivePipeline {
   681→    /// Phases ordered by increasing depth / cost.
   682→    pub phases: Vec<ProgressivePhase>,
   683→    /// Name for this pipeline.
   684→    pub name: String,
   685→}
   686→
   687→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   688→pub struct ProgressivePhase {
   689→    /// Human-readable phase label (e.g., "Smoke", "Standard", "Deep").
   690→    pub label: String,
   691→    /// The gate (or composed gate) for this phase.
   692→    /// Uses Box<dyn Gate> so it can be a single gate or a Parallel/Sequential.
   693→    pub gate: Box<dyn Gate>,
   694→    /// Blast radius fraction [0.0, 1.0] — informational, for logging.
   695→    pub blast_radius: f64,
   696→    /// Minimum duration to hold at this phase before advancing (optional).
   697→    pub hold_duration: Option<Duration>,
   698→}
   699→```
   700→
   701→### 15.2 Phase Progression for Agent Verification
   702→
   703→| Phase | Blast Radius | Gates | Cost | Purpose |
   704→|---|---|---|---|---|
   705→| Smoke | 1% | Compile only | ~3s | Does it parse? |
   706→| Lint | 5% | Compile + Clippy ∥ Diff | ~8s | Is it clean? |
   707→| Test | 25% | Full test suite | ~60s | Does it work? |
   708→| Property | 50% | Property tests (256 cases) | ~120s | Does it generalize? |
   709→| Deep | 100% | PBT (10K) + Fuzz (30s) + Integration | ~180s | Is it robust? |
   710→
   711→The cost column shows that short-circuiting at Smoke saves up to 180s per doomed
   712→attempt. For a plan with 50 tasks averaging 3 attempts each, the savings compound
   713→to hours of verification time.
   714→
   715→### 15.3 Rollback and Bake-In
   716→
   717→```
   718→Phase 1 (Smoke):  compile → PASS → hold 0s → advance
   719→Phase 2 (Lint):   clippy + diff → PASS → hold 0s → advance
   720→Phase 3 (Test):   test suite → FAIL → ROLLBACK → record failure signal
   721→                   (Phases 4-5 never run)
   722→```
   723→
   724→A failure at any phase triggers immediate rollback. The failure is recorded as a
   725→verdict Signal with the phase label as metadata, enabling the adaptive threshold
   726→system to track which phases are bottlenecks.
   727→
   728→---
   729→
   730→## 16. Pipeline Instrumentation
   731→
   732→### 16.1 Per-Gate Metrics
   733→
   734→Every gate execution produces an instrumentation event:
   735→
   736→```rust
   737→pub struct GateMetrics {
   738→    pub gate_name: String,
   739→    pub rung: u8,
   740→    pub passed: bool,
   741→    pub duration_ms: u64,
   742→    pub score: f32,
   743→    /// For probabilistic gates: confidence interval bounds.
   744→    pub ci_lower: Option<f64>,
   745→    pub ci_upper: Option<f64>,
   746→    pub sample_count: Option<u64>,
   747→    /// Memory high-water mark during gate execution (bytes).
   748→    pub peak_memory_bytes: Option<u64>,
   749→    /// Whether this gate was skipped (advisory skip from AdaptiveThresholds).
   750→    pub skipped: bool,
   751→}
   752→```
   753→
   754→### 16.2 Pipeline-Level Summary
   755→
   756→```rust
   757→pub struct PipelineMetrics {
   758→    pub name: String,
   759→    pub total_duration_ms: u64,
   760→    pub gates_run: usize,
   761→    pub gates_skipped: usize,
   762→    pub gates_passed: usize,
   763→    pub gates_failed: usize,
   764→    pub short_circuited: bool,
   765→    /// Which phase (in progressive mode) was reached.
   766→    pub phase_reached: Option<String>,
   767→    /// Per-gate breakdown.
   768→    pub gate_metrics: Vec<GateMetrics>,
   769→}
   770→```
   771→
   772→These metrics feed the adaptive threshold system, the efficiency event logger, and
   773→the dashboard's verification health display.
   774→
   775→---
   776→
   777→## 17. Test Criteria
   778→
   779→| Test | Property |
   780→|---|---|
   781→| `parallel_runs_concurrently` | Parallel(SlowGate, SlowGate) completes in ~1x, not 2x |
   782→| `parallel_both_fail` | Parallel with two failures returns aggregated failure |
   783→| `voting_quorum_pass` | 2/3 judges pass with quorum=2 → pass |
   784→| `voting_quorum_fail` | 1/3 judges pass with quorum=2 → fail |
   785→| `fallback_primary_passes` | Fallback does not run secondary if primary passes |
   786→| `fallback_primary_fails` | Fallback runs secondary and returns its verdict |
   787→| `sequential_stopping_early_pass` | Sequential property gate stops before max iterations |
   788→| `sequential_stopping_early_fail` | Counterexample found → stops and returns Fail |
   789→| `wilson_interval_small_sample` | CI width is large with n=5, small with n=10000 |
   790→| `probabilistic_to_standard_uses_lower_bound` | Conversion uses CI lower bound as score |
   791→| `progressive_short_circuits_on_phase_failure` | Fail at phase 2 → phases 3-5 never run |
   792→| `progressive_advances_all_phases` | All pass → reaches final phase |
   793→| `nested_pipeline` | Pipeline containing a Parallel containing gates works correctly |
   794→

1→# 04 — The Artifact Store
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-gate` (`crates/roko-gate/src/artifact_store.rs`)
     5→> **Status**: Implemented (172 lines)
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→The `ArtifactStore` is a content-addressed, append-only store for gate artifacts. Every
    15→artifact is identified by its BLAKE3 hash. The store deduplicates automatically: storing
    16→the same content twice returns the same hash without writing a second copy.
    17→
    18→Content addressing is a cornerstone of the verification architecture. It enables:
    19→- **Immutable artifacts**: Once stored, an artifact's content is fixed. The hash is its
    20→  identity. There is no "update" operation.
    21→- **Deduplication**: Identical outputs from different gate runs share storage.
    22→- **Reproducibility**: Given a hash, you can always retrieve the exact artifact.
    23→- **Forensic replay**: Any verdict can be traced to its exact inputs and outputs.
    24→
    25→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/artifact_store.rs — Full implementation.
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## 2. Structure
    30→
    31→```rust
    32→pub type ContentHash = [u8; 32];
    33→
    34→pub struct ArtifactStore {
    35→    items: HashMap<ContentHash, Vec<u8>>,
    36→}
    37→```
    38→
    39→The store is an in-memory `HashMap` from 32-byte BLAKE3 hashes to byte vectors. This
    40→is intentionally simple — no filesystem, no database, no network. The current
    41→implementation is an in-process store suitable for a single plan execution.
    42→
    43→### Why BLAKE3
    44→
    45→BLAKE3 is chosen over SHA-256 for three reasons:
    46→1. **Speed**: BLAKE3 is 5–15x faster than SHA-256 on modern hardware, critical when
    47→   hashing megabytes of test output.
    48→2. **Streaming**: BLAKE3 supports incremental hashing without buffering the full input.
    49→3. **Keyed mode**: BLAKE3 supports keyed hashing, enabling future per-session namespacing
    50→   without a separate HMAC construction.
    51→
    52→---
    53→
    54→## 3. Operations
    55→
    56→### 3.1 Store
    57→
    58→```rust
    59→pub fn store(&mut self, data: &[u8]) -> ContentHash {
    60→    let hash = blake3::hash(data).into();
    61→    self.items.entry(hash).or_insert_with(|| data.to_vec());
    62→    hash
    63→}
    64→```
    65→
    66→Computes the BLAKE3 hash of the input data. If the hash is not already in the store,
    67→inserts the data. Returns the hash in both cases. This is the only write operation.
    68→
    69→### 3.2 Retrieve
    70→
    71→```rust
    72→pub fn get(&self, hash: &ContentHash) -> Option<&[u8]> {
    73→    self.items.get(hash).map(Vec::as_slice)
    74→}
    75→```
    76→
    77→Returns the artifact bytes for a given hash, or `None` if the hash is not in the store.
    78→
    79→### 3.3 Contains
    80→
    81→```rust
    82→pub fn contains(&self, hash: &ContentHash) -> bool {
    83→    self.items.contains_key(hash)
    84→}
    85→```
    86→
    87→Check existence without retrieving the data.
    88→
    89→### 3.4 Count
    90→
    91→```rust
    92→pub fn len(&self) -> usize {
    93→    self.items.len()
    94→}
    95→```
    96→
    97→Number of unique artifacts stored.
    98→
    99→---
   100→
   101→## 4. Immutability and Append-Only Semantics
   102→
   103→The store has no `delete`, `update`, or `clear` operations in its public API. Once an
   104→artifact is stored, it exists for the lifetime of the store. This is a deliberate design
   105→constraint:
   106→
   107→- **No accidental loss**: A gate artifact that was used to produce a verdict cannot
   108→  disappear.
   109→- **Audit trail**: The chain from verdict → artifact hash → artifact content is always
   110→  intact.
   111→- **Concurrency safety**: Append-only structures have simpler concurrency properties
   112→  than mutable ones.
   113→
   114→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/tmp/mori-agents/20-verification-first-architecture.md —
   115→> "Immutable verification artifacts" as a key architectural decision.
   116→
   117→---
   118→
   119→## 5. Deduplication
   120→
   121→When an agent retries a task and produces identical output, the artifact store does not
   122→allocate new memory. The BLAKE3 hash matches the existing entry, and the `or_insert_with`
   123→short-circuits. This matters because:
   124→
   125→- Gate outputs can be large (megabytes of test runner output)
   126→- Retries are common (3–5 attempts per task is typical)
   127→- Many retries produce identical or near-identical output on the portions that haven't
   128→  changed
   129→
   130→Deduplication is automatic and zero-cost at the application level.
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→## 6. Relationship to Gate Verdicts
   135→
   136→The artifact store sits alongside the gate pipeline, not inside it. The current
   137→integration pattern is:
   138→
   139→```
   140→Gate produces verdict with detail (full output)
   141→    ↓
   142→Orchestrator stores detail in ArtifactStore
   143→    ↓
   144→ArtifactStore returns ContentHash
   145→    ↓
   146→ContentHash can be attached to the verdict or signal for later retrieval
   147→```
   148→
   149→This separation means gates don't need to know about the artifact store. They produce
   150→verdicts with `detail` strings, and the orchestrator decides what to persist.
   151→
   152→---
   153→
   154→## 7. Future: Persistent Artifact Store
   155→
   156→The current in-memory store is ephemeral — it lives and dies with the process. The
   157→design anticipates a persistent version:
   158→
   159→### 7.1 Filesystem Layout
   160→
   161→```
   162→.roko/artifacts/
   163→├── ab/
   164→│   ├── ab3f8c1d2e...  (BLAKE3 hash as filename)
   165→│   └── abd9e4f720...
   166→├── cd/
   167→│   └── cd1a2b3c4d...
   168→└── manifest.jsonl      (hash → metadata mapping)
   169→```
   170→
   171→Two-character prefix directories prevent any single directory from having too many
   172→entries (a common filesystem performance issue).
   173→
   174→### 7.2 Manifest
   175→
   176→A JSONL file mapping hashes to metadata:
   177→```json
   178→{"hash": "ab3f8c1d2e...", "gate": "compile:cargo", "plan": "plan-42", "rung": 0, "timestamp": "2026-04-10T12:00:00Z", "size_bytes": 4096}
   179→```
   180→
   181→### 7.3 Garbage Collection
   182→
   183→With persistence comes the need for GC. Artifacts older than a configurable threshold
   184→(e.g., 30 days) with no references from active plans can be pruned. The JSONL manifest
   185→enables efficient reference counting.
   186→
   187→---
   188→
   189→## 8. Content Addressing in the Broader Architecture
   190→
   191→Content-addressed storage appears in multiple places in Roko:
   192→
   193→| Component | What It Hashes | Hash Algorithm |
   194→|---|---|---|
   195→| `ArtifactStore` | Gate output bytes | BLAKE3 |
   196→| `Signal` | Signal content | BLAKE3 |
   197→| `FileSubstrate` | Signal bodies (JSONL) | BLAKE3 |
   198→| Episode logs | Agent turns | N/A (sequential) |
   199→
   200→The consistency of BLAKE3 across the system means any artifact or signal can be
   201→cross-referenced by hash. A verdict's detail text, stored in the artifact store, hashes
   202→to the same value whether you compute it from the store or from the verdict's detail
   203→field.
   204→
   205→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/01-synapse-architecture.md — "Content-addressed,
   206→> scored, decaying, lineage-tracked" — Engrams are content-addressed; artifacts follow
   207→> the same principle.
   208→
   209→---
   210→
   211→## 9. Relationship to Forensic AI
   212→
   213→The artifact store is a building block for Forensic AI causal replay (see
   214→[12-forensic-ai-causal-replay.md](./12-forensic-ai-causal-replay.md)). To replay an
   215→agent's verification history:
   216→
   217→1. Retrieve the signal (engram) by hash from the Substrate.
   218→2. Retrieve the gate artifacts by hash from the ArtifactStore.
   219→3. Replay the gate pipeline with the original signal and compare verdicts.
   220→
   221→Content addressing makes this replay exact: the same hash guarantees the same content,
   222→so the replayed inputs are byte-identical to the originals.
   223→
   224→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md — Innovation IX: Forensic AI Causal
   225→> Replay, "content-addressed replay of any agent action."
   226→
   227→---
   228→
   229→## 10. Testing
   230→
   231→The artifact store's tests cover:
   232→
   233→| Test | What It Verifies |
   234→|---|---|
   235→| `store_and_retrieve` | Basic store/get roundtrip |
   236→| `deduplication` | Same content → same hash, no duplicate storage |
   237→| `missing_hash` | `get()` returns `None` for unstored hashes |
   238→| `contains_check` | `contains()` matches `get().is_some()` |
   239→| `empty_data` | Empty byte slices are valid artifacts |
   240→| `large_data` | Large inputs (megabytes) work correctly |
   241→
   242→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/artifact_store.rs — Tests section.
   243→
   244→---
   245→
   246→## 11. Summary
   247→
   248→The `ArtifactStore` is deliberately minimal: store bytes, get bytes, check existence.
   249→No deletion, no mutation, no networking. This simplicity makes it correct by construction
   250→— there are no race conditions, no consistency issues, and no data loss paths.
   251→
   252→The key insight is that **verification artifacts are write-once, read-many**. A gate's
   253→output never changes after the gate runs. By giving each artifact a unique
   254→content-derived identity (BLAKE3 hash), the system can reference artifacts reliably
   255→across time, across retries, and across processes without coordination.
   256→

1→# 05 — Gate Ratcheting
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-gate` (`crates/roko-gate/src/ratchet.rs`)
     5→> **Status**: Implemented (207 lines)
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→The `GateRatchet` prevents verification regression. Once a plan has passed rung N, it
    15→should never be allowed to regress to rung N-1. The ratchet tracks the highest rung each
    16→plan has passed and provides a `can_regress()` check that the conductor uses before
    17→accepting a lower verdict.
    18→
    19→This solves a specific failure mode in multi-attempt agent loops: **convergence
    20→thrashing**. An agent fixes the compile error but breaks lint. On retry, it fixes lint
    21→but breaks compile. Without a ratchet, this cycle can repeat indefinitely, consuming
    22→compute and making no net progress.
    23→
    24→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/ratchet.rs — Full implementation.
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## 2. The Thrashing Problem
    29→
    30→Consider a 3-rung pipeline (Compile → Lint → Test):
    31→
    32→```
    33→Attempt 1: Compile PASS, Lint FAIL
    34→  Agent receives: "warning: unused variable"
    35→  Agent fixes lint issue
    36→
    37→Attempt 2: Compile FAIL, (Lint/Test never run)
    38→  Agent's lint fix introduced a type error
    39→  Agent receives: "error[E0308]: mismatched types"
    40→  Agent fixes type error
    41→
    42→Attempt 3: Compile PASS, Lint FAIL
    43→  Agent's type fix reintroduced the lint issue
    44→  Agent receives: "warning: unused variable"
    45→  ... (infinite loop)
    46→```
    47→
    48→Each attempt passes one rung and fails the next. The agent is doing work — it's
    49→modifying code each time — but it's not making *progress*. The net verification state
    50→oscillates between "compiles but doesn't lint" and "lints but doesn't compile."
    51→
    52→The ratchet breaks this cycle by making the pipeline say: "You passed Compile on
    53→Attempt 1. You are not allowed to regress below Compile on Attempt 2." If Attempt 2
    54→fails compile, the ratchet flags this as a regression, and the system can:
    55→- Reject the attempt outright
    56→- Flag it for human review
    57→- Give the agent a different prompt ("Your previous attempt passed compile. Your new
    58→  attempt broke compile. Fix the compile error without regressing.")
    59→
    60→---
    61→
    62→## 3. Data Structure
    63→
    64→```rust
    65→pub struct GateRatchet {
    66→    passes: HashMap<String, u8>,
    67→}
    68→```
    69→
    70→A map from plan identifier (string) to the highest rung number (u8) that plan has
    71→passed. The rung number corresponds to the `Rung` enum's discriminant (0–6).
    72→
    73→### Why u8?
    74→
    75→Seven rungs fit in 3 bits. Using `u8` is the natural Rust choice for a small non-negative
    76→integer. It avoids the overhead of an enum in a `HashMap` and allows simple comparison
    77→operators (`>`, `>=`).
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→## 4. Operations
    82→
    83→### 4.1 Record a Pass
    84→
    85→```rust
    86→pub fn record_pass(&mut self, plan_id: impl Into<String>, rung: u8) {
    87→    let entry = self.passes.entry(plan_id.into()).or_insert(0);
    88→    if rung > *entry {
    89→        *entry = rung;
    90→    }
    91→}
    92→```
    93→
    94→Records that `plan_id` passed `rung`. Only advances the watermark — if the plan has
    95→already passed a higher rung, this is a no-op.
    96→
    97→**Monotonic property**: The stored value for any plan ID can only increase or stay the
    98→same. It never decreases. This is the core ratchet invariant.
    99→
   100→### 4.2 Query Highest Pass
   101→
   102→```rust
   103→pub fn highest_pass(&self, plan_id: &str) -> Option<u8> {
   104→    self.passes.get(plan_id).copied()
   105→}
   106→```
   107→
   108→Returns the highest rung the plan has passed, or `None` if the plan has no recorded
   109→passes. `None` means the ratchet has no opinion — the plan is free to pass or fail any
   110→rung.
   111→
   112→### 4.3 Check for Regression
   113→
   114→```rust
   115→pub fn can_regress(&self, plan_id: &str, rung: u8) -> bool {
   116→    match self.passes.get(plan_id) {
   117→        None => true,                    // Unknown plan: no regression possible
   118→        Some(&highest) => rung >= highest, // OK if same or higher
   119→    }
   120→}
   121→```
   122→
   123→Returns `false` if accepting `rung` as the new highest would be a regression (i.e., the
   124→plan has already passed a strictly higher rung). Returns `true` if:
   125→- The plan has never been recorded (no regression possible)
   126→- The plan's highest pass is equal to or lower than `rung`
   127→
   128→**Note**: The method is named `can_regress` but returns `true` when regression is *not*
   129→happening. The semantics are: "Is it acceptable to record this rung?" — which is true
   130→when no regression would occur.
   131→
   132→### 4.4 Plan Count and Clear
   133→
   134→```rust
   135→pub fn plan_count(&self) -> usize {
   136→    self.passes.len()
   137→}
   138→
   139→pub fn clear(&mut self) {
   140→    self.passes.clear();
   141→}
   142→```
   143→
   144→`plan_count()` reports how many plans are tracked. `clear()` resets the ratchet entirely,
   145→used when starting a fresh execution session.
   146→
   147→---
   148→
   149→## 5. Usage Pattern in the Orchestrator
   150→
   151→```rust
   152→// After gate pipeline produces a verdict:
   153→let rung = verdict_rung;  // Which rung did we reach?
   154→let plan_id = &task.plan_id;
   155→
   156→if verdict.passed {
   157→    ratchet.record_pass(plan_id, rung);
   158→} else {
   159→    // Check if this failure represents a regression
   160→    if !ratchet.can_regress(plan_id, rung) {
   161→        // Regression detected! Agent passed rung N before but now fails it.
   162→        // Options:
   163→        //   1. Reject this attempt
   164→        //   2. Feed back: "You regressed from rung N to rung M"
   165→        //   3. Trigger re-planning
   166→    }
   167→}
   168→```
   169→
   170→---
   171→
   172→## 6. Ratchet + Escalation Interaction
   173→
   174→The ratchet and the escalation mechanism (see [02-6-rung-selector.md](./02-6-rung-selector.md))
   175→work together but serve different purposes:
   176→
   177→| Mechanism | Direction | Purpose |
   178→|---|---|---|
   179→| Escalation | Forward (adds rungs) | Failed → try harder |
   180→| Ratchet | Backward (blocks regression) | Passed → don't lose progress |
   181→
   182→Together they create a monotonically advancing verification frontier:
   183→
   184→```
   185→Attempt 1: Complexity=Simple, Rungs=[Compile, Lint]
   186→  → Compile PASS (ratchet records rung 0)
   187→  → Lint FAIL
   188→  → Escalate to Standard
   189→
   190→Attempt 2: Complexity=Standard, Rungs=[Compile, Lint, Test, Symbol]
   191→  → Compile must still pass (ratchet enforces)
   192→  → Lint PASS (ratchet records rung 1)
   193→  → Test FAIL
   194→  → Escalate to Complex
   195→
   196→Attempt 3: Complexity=Complex, Rungs=[all]
   197→  → Compile must still pass (ratchet enforces)
   198→  → Lint must still pass (ratchet enforces)
   199→  → Test PASS (ratchet records rung 2)
   200→  → ... and so on
   201→```
   202→
   203→Each attempt can only move the verification frontier forward. Rungs that have been
   204→passed stay passed.
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## 7. Per-Plan Isolation
   209→
   210→Each plan has its own ratchet entry. Plan A's progress has no effect on Plan B's
   211→ratchet. This is important because:
   212→
   213→- Plans are independent units of work (a plan might be "implement rate limiter" while
   214→  another is "fix auth bug")
   215→- Different plans may be at different stages of verification
   216→- One plan's compile failure doesn't prevent another plan from passing lint
   217→
   218→The `HashMap<String, u8>` keying on plan ID provides this isolation naturally.
   219→
   220→---
   221→
   222→## 8. Ratchet in the Context of Process Reward Models
   223→
   224→The ratchet is a simple form of process reward: it tracks *intermediate* verification
   225→progress, not just final outcomes. A plan that reaches Rung 3 (passed Compile + Lint +
   226→Test) has demonstrated more progress than one that only reaches Rung 1 (passed Compile).
   227→
   228→This intermediate signal feeds into the process reward model (see
   229→[07-process-reward-models.md](./07-process-reward-models.md)):
   230→
   231→- **Promise score**: How likely is this plan to eventually pass all rungs, given that
   232→  it has passed rung N so far?
   233→- **Progress score**: Is the plan advancing (reaching higher rungs on successive
   234→  attempts) or stalling?
   235→
   236→The ratchet provides the raw data — "plan X has reached rung N" — that the process
   237→reward model interprets.
   238→
   239→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/02-five-layers.md — "Process Reward Models: Promise +
   240→> Progress scoring, low Promise → early intervention, negative Progress → re-planning."
   241→
   242→---
   243→
   244→## 9. Edge Cases
   245→
   246→### 9.1 Rung 0 Ratchet
   247→
   248→If a plan passes Rung 0 (Compile), the ratchet records `highest = 0`. On the next
   249→attempt, `can_regress("plan", 0)` returns `true` (rung 0 >= 0). Only a *lower* rung
   250→would trigger regression, but there is no rung below 0. So a plan that passed Compile
   251→can fail Compile on a subsequent attempt without the ratchet blocking it.
   252→
   253→Wait — that seems wrong. If we passed Compile, shouldn't we enforce it?
   254→
   255→The answer is: `can_regress` checks if the *proposed rung* is below the highest. Rung 0
   256→is not below 0, so it's allowed. This is correct because the ratchet's `record_pass()`
   257→only fires on success. A failure at Rung 0 doesn't call `record_pass()`, so the stored
   258→value stays at 0. The regression check happens externally — the orchestrator checks
   259→`can_regress(plan, failing_rung)` and decides what to do.
   260→
   261→### 9.2 Full Pipeline Pass
   262→
   263→When a plan passes all 7 rungs, `highest_pass` = 6. Any subsequent failure at any rung
   264→is a regression (since all rungs 0–5 are below 6). Only passing rung 6 again is non-
   265→regressive.
   266→
   267→### 9.3 String Plan IDs
   268→
   269→Plan IDs are strings, supporting both owned (`String::from("plan-1")`) and borrowed
   270→(`"plan-1"`) inputs via `impl Into<String>`.
   271→
   272→---
   273→
   274→## 10. Future: Persistent Ratchet
   275→
   276→The current ratchet is in-memory and ephemeral. When the process exits, all ratchet
   277→state is lost. For long-running or resumable executions, the ratchet should persist
   278→to disk:
   279→
   280→```json
   281→// .roko/state/gate-ratchet.json
   282→{
   283→  "plan-42": 3,
   284→  "plan-43": 1,
   285→  "plan-44": 5
   286→}
   287→```
   288→
   289→This aligns with the existing executor snapshot persistence
   290→(`.roko/state/executor.json`) and would be loaded on `--resume`.
   291→
   292→---

   315→---
   316→
   317→## 12. Summary
   318→
   319→The `GateRatchet` is a one-way valve on verification progress. It answers one question:
   320→"Has this plan ever passed a higher rung than what it's being asked to accept now?"
   321→If yes, the answer is regression. If no, the answer is progress (or same level).
   322→
   323→Its simplicity — a `HashMap<String, u8>` with monotonic updates — makes it correct by
   324→construction. There are no complex algorithms, no statistical models, no heuristics.
   325→Just: the highest rung you've passed is the floor you can't drop below.
   326→

1→# 06 — Adaptive Gate Thresholds
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-gate` (`crates/roko-gate/src/adaptive_threshold.rs`)
     5→> **Status**: Implemented (215 lines), persists to `.roko/learn/gate-thresholds.json`
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→Adaptive thresholds tune verification behavior based on historical pass rates. They use
    15→exponential moving averages (EMA) per gate rung to track how often each rung passes, and
    16→from that, derive two advisory signals:
    17→
    18→1. **Retry budget**: How many retries should a rung get? High pass rate → fewer retries.
    19→   Low pass rate → more retries.
    20→2. **Skip advisory**: Should a rung be skipped? If it has passed 20+ times consecutively,
    21→   it's probably always passing and could be skipped to save time.
    22→
    23→Both signals are advisory — the orchestrator may override them. But they provide a
    24→data-driven default that adapts to the project's actual verification characteristics.
    25→
    26→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/adaptive_threshold.rs — Full implementation.
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## 2. Per-Rung Statistics
    31→
    32→```rust
    33→pub struct RungStats {
    34→    pub ema_pass_rate: f64,       // Exponential moving average of pass rate [0.0, 1.0]
    35→    pub total_observations: u64,  // Total gate runs for this rung
    36→    pub consecutive_passes: u32,  // Consecutive passes (reset on any failure)
    37→}
    38→```
    39→
    40→Each rung gets its own `RungStats`. A fresh rung starts with:
    41→- `ema_pass_rate = 0.5` (neutral prior — no assumption about pass/fail tendency)
    42→- `total_observations = 0`
    43→- `consecutive_passes = 0`
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## 3. The EMA Algorithm
    48→
    49→### 3.1 Update Rule
    50→
    51→```rust
    52→pub fn update(&mut self, rung: u32, passed: bool) {
    53→    let stats = self.rungs.entry(rung).or_default();
    54→    let value = if passed { 1.0 } else { 0.0 };
    55→
    56→    if stats.total_observations == 0 {
    57→        stats.ema_pass_rate = value;  // First observation sets the rate directly
    58→    } else {
    59→        stats.ema_pass_rate = EMA_ALPHA.mul_add(value, (1.0 - EMA_ALPHA) * stats.ema_pass_rate);
    60→    }
    61→
    62→    stats.total_observations += 1;
    63→
    64→    if passed {
    65→        stats.consecutive_passes += 1;
    66→    } else {
    67→        stats.consecutive_passes = 0;
    68→    }
    69→}
    70→```
    71→
    72→### 3.2 Why EMA?
    73→
    74→An exponential moving average with α = 0.1 means:
    75→- Recent observations weigh more than old ones
    76→- The effective memory is ~1/α ≈ 10 observations
    77→- Gradual changes in pass rate are tracked smoothly
    78→
    79→This is important because gate pass rates change over time:
    80→- A new project with many issues has low pass rates initially
    81→- As issues are fixed, pass rates climb
    82→- A major refactor temporarily drops pass rates before they recover
    83→
    84→A simple average (total passes / total observations) would be slow to respond to these
    85→shifts. The EMA adapts within ~10 observations.
    86→
    87→### 3.3 The α Parameter
    88→
    89→`EMA_ALPHA = 0.1` is the decay constant. Higher α means more responsive (recent data
    90→dominates), lower α means more stable (historical data dominates).
    91→
    92→| α | Effective window | Behavior |
    93→|---|---|---|
    94→| 0.01 | ~100 observations | Very stable, slow to adapt |
    95→| 0.1 | ~10 observations | Balanced (current default) |
    96→| 0.3 | ~3 observations | Responsive, potentially noisy |
    97→
    98→The choice of 0.1 balances responsiveness with stability. A gate that fails once
    99→shouldn't immediately triple the retry budget, but a gate that fails 5 times in a row
   100→should.
   101→
   102→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/07-fast-feedback-loops.md — Fast feedback
   103→> loops using EMA-based calibration.
   104→
   105→---
   106→
   107→## 4. Retry Budget Suggestion
   108→
   109→```rust
   110→pub fn suggested_max_retries(&self, rung: u32) -> u32 {
   111→    let Some(stats) = self.rungs.get(&rung) else {
   112→        return 3; // Default for unknown rungs
   113→    };
   114→
   115→    if stats.total_observations < 5 {
   116→        return 3; // Not enough data
   117→    }
   118→
   119→    // Map pass rate to retries: high pass → low retries, low pass → high retries
   120→    let retries = stats.ema_pass_rate.mul_add(-range, max).round() as u32;
   121→    retries.clamp(MIN_RETRIES, MAX_RETRIES)
   122→}
   123→```
   124→
   125→The mapping is linear:
   126→- Pass rate 1.0 → 1 retry (it almost always passes; one attempt is enough)
   127→- Pass rate 0.5 → 3 retries (coin flip; give it a few tries)
   128→- Pass rate 0.0 → 5 retries (it almost never passes; maximize attempts)
   129→
   130→### Constants
   131→
   132→| Constant | Value | Purpose |
   133→|---|---|---|
   134→| `MIN_RETRIES` | 1 | Floor: always try at least once |
   135→| `MAX_RETRIES` | 5 | Ceiling: don't waste resources endlessly |
   136→
   137→### Cold Start
   138→
   139→For unknown rungs or rungs with fewer than 5 observations, the default is 3 retries.
   140→This avoids extreme behavior early in a project's lifecycle.
   141→
   142→---
   143→
   144→## 5. Skip Advisory
   145→
   146→```rust
   147→pub fn should_skip_rung(&self, rung: u32) -> bool {
   148→    self.rungs
   149→        .get(&rung)
   150→        .is_some_and(|s| s.consecutive_passes >= SKIP_STREAK_THRESHOLD)
   151→}
   152→```
   153→
   154→If a rung has passed `SKIP_STREAK_THRESHOLD` (20) consecutive times, the system
   155→suggests it can be skipped. This advisory is not enforced — the orchestrator decides
   156→whether to honor it.
   157→
   158→### Why 20?
   159→
   160→Twenty consecutive passes means the gate hasn't failed in at least 20 task executions.
   161→For a gate like Compile, this suggests the project's code generation is reliable enough
   162→that compile failures are rare. Running the gate still costs time (seconds to minutes),
   163→and if the system has high confidence the gate will pass, skipping it saves that time.
   164→
   165→### Why Advisory Only?
   166→
   167→Even a gate with 100 consecutive passes can fail unexpectedly (new dependency breaks,
   168→CI environment changes, agent produces unusually complex code). Making the skip advisory
   169→rather than mandatory means:
   170→- The orchestrator can skip the gate 90% of the time for speed
   171→- Every Nth run (e.g., every 5th), it still runs the gate to check
   172→- A failure resets the consecutive pass counter, re-enabling the gate
   173→
   174→This "mostly skip, periodically verify" pattern is common in testing infrastructure
   175→(see: test quarantine systems, flaky test skip-and-retry).
   176→
   177→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/09-evaluation-map.md — 14 feedback loops
   178→> across 5 speed tiers, including machine-speed confidence calibration.
   179→
   180→---
   181→
   182→## 6. Persistence
   183→
   184→```rust
   185→pub fn save(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<(), std::io::Error> {
   186→    let json = serde_json::to_string_pretty(self)?;
   187→    let tmp = path.with_extension("json.tmp");
   188→    std::fs::write(&tmp, &json)?;
   189→    std::fs::rename(&tmp, path)?;
   190→    Ok(())
   191→}
   192→
   193→pub fn load_or_new(path: &Path) -> Self {
   194→    std::fs::read_to_string(path)
   195→        .ok()
   196→        .and_then(|s| serde_json::from_str(&s).ok())
   197→        .unwrap_or_default()
   198→}
   199→```
   200→
   201→### Atomic Write
   202→
   203→The `save()` method uses the atomic write pattern: write to a temporary file, then
   204→rename. This ensures the file is never in a half-written state. If the process crashes
   205→between `write` and `rename`, the old file remains intact.
   206→
   207→### Graceful Degradation
   208→
   209→`load_or_new()` returns a fresh `AdaptiveThresholds` if the file is missing or corrupt.
   210→This means the system always starts correctly — it just loses its historical data on
   211→corruption.
   212→
   213→### Storage Location
   214→
   215→The thresholds persist to `.roko/learn/gate-thresholds.json`. This is the learning
   216→subsystem's data directory, alongside:
   217→- `.roko/learn/cascade-router.json` (model routing state)
   218→- `.roko/learn/experiments.json` (prompt experiment state)
   219→- `.roko/learn/efficiency.jsonl` (per-turn efficiency events)
   220→
   221→> **Citation**: CLAUDE.md — "Adaptive gate thresholds: EMA per rung in
   222→> `.roko/learn/gate-thresholds.json`."
   223→
   224→---
   225→
   226→## 7. Interaction with Other Components
   227→
   228→### 7.1 Rung Selector
   229→
   230→The rung selector (see [02-6-rung-selector.md](./02-6-rung-selector.md)) determines
   231→which rungs to run based on static complexity. The adaptive thresholds refine this:
   232→
   233→```
   234→Static selection: complexity → rungs [0, 1, 2, 3]
   235→Adaptive refinement:
   236→  Rung 0: 25 consecutive passes → skip advisory
   237→  Rung 1: 3 consecutive passes → run normally
   238→  Rung 2: 12 consecutive passes → run normally
   239→  Rung 3: not tracked yet → run with default retries
   240→Final: rungs [1, 2, 3] with rung 0 skipped
   241→```
   242→
   243→### 7.2 Retry Logic
   244→
   245→The orchestrator's retry loop consults `suggested_max_retries()`:
   246→
   247→```rust
   248→let max_retries = thresholds.suggested_max_retries(current_rung);
   249→for attempt in 0..max_retries {
   250→    let verdict = pipeline.verify(signal, ctx).await;
   251→    if verdict.passed { break; }
   252→    // ... escalate, adjust prompt, retry
   253→}
   254→```
   255→
   256→### 7.3 Gate Pipeline Feedback
   257→
   258→After each pipeline execution, the orchestrator updates the thresholds:
   259→
   260→```rust
   261→for (rung, verdict) in rung_verdicts {
   262→    thresholds.update(rung as u32, verdict.passed);
   263→}
   264→thresholds.save(&thresholds_path)?;
   265→```
   266→
   267→This closes the feedback loop: gate outcomes → EMA update → retry budget adjustment →
   268→different gate behavior on next execution.
   269→

   273→
   274→```rust
   275→pub fn rung_stats(&self, rung: u32) -> Option<&RungStats> {
   276→    self.rungs.get(&rung)
   277→}
   278→
   279→pub fn all_rungs(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = (&u32, &RungStats)> {

   284→These methods enable the dashboard and status commands to display per-rung health:
   285→

   287→Gate Thresholds:
   288→  Rung 0 (Compile):  98.2% pass rate, 142 observations, 31 consecutive passes [SKIP ADVISORY]
   289→  Rung 1 (Lint):     87.5% pass rate, 130 observations, 8 consecutive passes
   290→  Rung 2 (Test):     72.1% pass rate, 118 observations, 3 consecutive passes
   291→  Rung 3 (Symbol):   95.0% pass rate, 45 observations, 15 consecutive passes
   292→```
   293→
   294→---
   295→
   296→## 9. Relationship to the GVU Framework
   297→
   298→The adaptive thresholds are a practical implementation of the GVU framework's guidance
   299→on verification investment. The framework proves that stronger verifiers yield better
   300→self-improvement. The thresholds operationalize this by:
   301→
   302→1. **Allocating more retries** to gates with low pass rates (investing more in
   303→   verification where it's most needed).
   304→2. **Reducing retries** for gates with high pass rates (not wasting resources on
   305→   verification that's already reliable).
   306→3. **Skipping gates** that are essentially always passing (redirecting verification
   307→   budget to where it matters).
   308→
   309→This is adaptive resource allocation for verification — a concrete instance of the GVU
   310→insight that verification quality matters more than generation quality.
   311→
   312→> **Citation**: Song et al. (ICLR 2025) — GVU framework, verification-first investment
   313→> strategy.
   314→

   320→|---|---|
   321→| `new_rung_starts_neutral` | Unknown rung → default 3 retries, no skip |
   322→| `high_pass_rate_reduces_retries` | ~100% pass rate → 1 retry |
   323→| `low_pass_rate_increases_retries` | ~0% pass rate → 5 retries |
   324→| `consecutive_passes_trigger_skip` | 20 consecutive → skip advisory |

   331→
   332→## 11. Statistical Process Control (SPC) Extensions
   333→
   334→The current EMA provides a smoothed estimate of pass rates. Statistical Process Control
   335→adds formal anomaly detection — distinguishing true process changes from random
   336→fluctuation. Three complementary SPC methods detect different kinds of shifts.
   337→
   338→> **Citation**: "Improved adaptive CUSUM control chart for industrial process monitoring"
   339→> (Nature Scientific Reports, 2025).
   340→
   341→### 11.1 CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) for Sustained Shifts
   342→
   343→CUSUM detects small, sustained changes in gate pass rates that the EMA might smooth
   344→over. A gate whose pass rate drifts from 90% to 80% over 20 runs may not trigger an
   345→alert in EMA but will accumulate signal in CUSUM.
   346→
   347→```rust
   348→/// CUSUM detector for sustained shifts in gate pass rates.
   349→///
   350→/// Tracks cumulative departures from target in both directions.
   351→/// Signals when accumulated drift exceeds the decision interval.
   352→pub struct CusumDetector {
   353→    /// Reference value (slack parameter). Typically 0.5 * delta where
   354→    /// delta is the shift size to detect in standard deviation units.
   355→    /// Default: 0.25 (detects ~0.5σ shifts in pass rate).
   356→    pub k: f64,
   357→    /// Decision interval. Higher = fewer false alarms, slower detection.
   358→    /// Default: 4.0 (ARL₀ ≈ 168 observations before false alarm).
   359→    pub h: f64,
   360→    /// Target pass rate (process mean under normal operation).
   361→    /// Updated from historical data or set from EMA baseline.
   362→    pub mu_0: f64,
   363→    /// Process standard deviation estimate.
   364→    /// For binary pass/fail: σ = sqrt(p * (1-p)).
   365→    pub sigma: f64,
   366→    /// Upper CUSUM accumulator (detects upward shift — improving).
   367→    pub c_plus: f64,
   368→    /// Lower CUSUM accumulator (detects downward shift — degrading).
   369→    pub c_minus: f64,
   370→    /// Whether a shift has been detected.
   371→    pub shift_detected: bool,
   372→    /// Direction of detected shift.
   373→    pub shift_direction: Option<ShiftDirection>,
   374→}
   375→
   376→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
   377→pub enum ShiftDirection {
   378→    /// Pass rate is improving (higher than target).
   379→    Improving,
   380→    /// Pass rate is degrading (lower than target).
   381→    Degrading,
   382→}
   383→
   384→impl CusumDetector {
   385→    /// Update with a new observation.
   386→    ///
   387→    /// value: 1.0 for pass, 0.0 for fail.
   388→    pub fn update(&mut self, value: f64) {
   389→        let z = (value - self.mu_0) / self.sigma; // standardize
   390→
   391→        // Detect upward shift (improving)
   392→        self.c_plus = (self.c_plus + z - self.k).max(0.0);
   393→        // Detect downward shift (degrading)
   394→        self.c_minus = (self.c_minus - z - self.k).max(0.0);
   395→
   396→        if self.c_plus > self.h {
   397→            self.shift_detected = true;
   398→            self.shift_direction = Some(ShiftDirection::Improving);
   399→            self.c_plus = self.h / 2.0; // Fast Initial Response (FIR) reset
   400→        } else if self.c_minus > self.h {
   401→            self.shift_detected = true;
   402→            self.shift_direction = Some(ShiftDirection::Degrading);
   403→            self.c_minus = self.h / 2.0; // FIR reset
   404→        } else {
   405→            self.shift_detected = false;
   406→            self.shift_direction = None;
   407→        }
   408→    }
   409→}
   410→```
   411→
   412→**Parameters**:
   413→
   414→| Parameter | Default | Range | Effect |
   415→|---|---|---|---|
   416→| `k` (reference value) | 0.25 | 0.1–1.0 | Lower = more sensitive to small shifts |
   417→| `h` (decision interval) | 4.0 | 2.0–8.0 | Lower = faster detection, more false alarms |
   418→| FIR reset | `h/2` | — | Halves accumulator on signal, enabling re-detection |
   419→
   420→### 11.2 EWMA Control Chart
   421→
   422→Extends the existing EMA with formal control limits. The current implementation tracks
   423→`ema_pass_rate` but has no formal bounds for when that rate is "out of control."
   424→
   425→```rust
   426→/// EWMA control chart with time-varying control limits.
   427→///
   428→/// Adds formal upper/lower control limits (UCL/LCL) to the existing
   429→/// EMA pass rate tracking. When the EMA crosses a limit, the gate
   430→/// is flagged as out-of-control.
   431→pub struct EwmaControlChart {
   432→    /// Smoothing factor (same as EMA_ALPHA). Range: [0.05, 0.25].
   433→    /// Lower = more memory, detects smaller shifts.
   434→    pub lambda: f64,      // default: 0.10
   435→    /// Control limit width in sigma units. Range: [2.5, 3.5].
   436→    /// Wider = fewer false alarms.
   437→    pub l_factor: f64,    // default: 2.814
   438→    /// Target mean (established from historical data).
   439→    pub mu_0: f64,
   440→    /// Process standard deviation.
   441→    pub sigma: f64,
   442→    /// Current EWMA value (= ema_pass_rate from RungStats).
   443→    pub z: f64,
   444→    /// Number of observations (for time-varying limit computation).
   445→    pub n: u64,
   446→}
   447→
   448→impl EwmaControlChart {
   449→    /// Compute the current upper and lower control limits.
   450→    ///
   451→    /// Time-varying: limits are wider early (few observations) and
   452→    /// converge to steady-state as n grows.
   453→    pub fn control_limits(&self) -> (f64, f64) {
   454→        let asymptotic_var = self.lambda / (2.0 - self.lambda);
   455→        let time_factor = 1.0 - (1.0 - self.lambda).powi(2 * self.n as i32);
   456→        let sigma_z = self.sigma * (asymptotic_var * time_factor).sqrt();
   457→
   458→        let ucl = self.mu_0 + self.l_factor * sigma_z;
   459→        let lcl = self.mu_0 - self.l_factor * sigma_z;
   460→        (lcl.max(0.0), ucl.min(1.0)) // clamp to [0, 1] for pass rates
   461→    }
   462→
   463→    /// Check if the current EWMA is within control limits.
   464→    pub fn is_in_control(&self) -> bool {
   465→        let (lcl, ucl) = self.control_limits();
   466→        self.z >= lcl && self.z <= ucl
   467→    }
   468→}
   469→```
   470→
   471→**ARL (Average Run Length) Tuning**:
   472→
   473→| λ | L | ARL₀ (in-control) | ARL₁ (1σ shift) | Best for |
   474→|---|---|---|---|---|
   475→| 0.05 | 2.625 | ~500 | ~26 | Small persistent drifts |
   476→| 0.10 | 2.814 | ~500 | ~31 | **Balanced (default)** |
   477→| 0.20 | 2.962 | ~500 | ~41 | Larger sudden shifts |
   478→
   479→ARL₀ ~ 500 means one false alarm per ~500 observations. ARL₁ ~ 31 means a true
   480→1σ shift is detected in ~31 observations on average.
   481→
   482→### 11.3 BOCPD (Bayesian Online Change Point Detection)
   483→
   484→When EMA and CUSUM detect a shift, BOCPD provides a probabilistic answer to "did the
   485→gate's fundamental behavior change?" This is critical after major refactors, dependency
   486→updates, or model switches, where the baseline itself shifts.
   487→
   488→> **Citation**: Adams & MacKay, "Bayesian Online Changepoint Detection" (arXiv:0710.3742,
   489→> 2007).
   490→
   491→```rust
   492→/// Bayesian Online Change Point Detection.
   493→///
   494→/// Maintains a posterior distribution over run lengths (time since last
   495→/// change point). When P(run_length = 0) spikes, a change point has
   496→/// occurred and the gate baseline should be recalibrated.
   497→pub struct BocpdDetector {
   498→    /// Prior probability of a change point at each step.
   499→    /// Lower = fewer expected change points (more stable process).
   500→    /// Default: 1/200 (expect one change point per 200 observations).
   501→    pub hazard_rate: f64,
   502→    /// Maximum run length to track (truncation for O(R_max) per step).
   503→    pub max_run_length: usize,  // default: 300
   504→    /// Run-length posterior probabilities.
   505→    pub run_length_probs: Vec<f64>,
   506→    /// Sufficient statistics for the underlying model (Normal-Gamma
   507→    /// conjugate for Gaussian observations).
   508→    pub sufficient_stats: Vec<NormalGammaStats>,
   509→    /// Threshold for declaring a change point.
   510→    /// When P(r=0) > threshold, a change point is declared.
   511→    pub changepoint_threshold: f64,  // default: 0.5
   512→}
   513→
   514→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   515→pub struct NormalGammaStats {
   516→    pub mu: f64,     // posterior mean
   517→    pub kappa: f64,  // pseudo-observations for mean
   518→    pub alpha: f64,  // shape for variance
   519→    pub beta: f64,   // rate for variance
   520→}
   521→
   522→impl BocpdDetector {
   523→    /// Update with a new observation and return whether a change point was detected.
   524→    pub fn update(&mut self, value: f64) -> bool {
   525→        // 1. Compute predictive probability for each run length
   526→        let predictive: Vec<f64> = self.sufficient_stats.iter()
   527→            .map(|s| s.predictive_probability(value))
   528→            .collect();
   529→
   530→        // 2. Growth probabilities (no change point)
   531→        let growth: Vec<f64> = self.run_length_probs.iter()
   532→            .zip(predictive.iter())
   533→            .map(|(p, pi)| p * pi * (1.0 - self.hazard_rate))
   534→            .collect();
   535→
   536→        // 3. Change-point probability (run length resets to 0)
   537→        let changepoint_mass: f64 = self.run_length_probs.iter()
   538→            .zip(predictive.iter())
   539→            .map(|(p, pi)| p * pi * self.hazard_rate)
   540→            .sum();
   541→
   542→        // 4. Build new posterior
   543→        let mut new_probs = vec![changepoint_mass];
   544→        new_probs.extend(growth.iter().take(self.max_run_length - 1));
   545→
   546→        // 5. Normalize
   547→        let total: f64 = new_probs.iter().sum();
   548→        if total > 0.0 {
   549→            for p in &mut new_probs {
   550→                *p /= total;
   551→            }
   552→        }
   553→
   554→        // 6. Update sufficient statistics for each run length
   555→        // (extend by one entry for r=0, update existing entries)
   556→        self.update_sufficient_stats(value);
   557→
   558→        self.run_length_probs = new_probs;
   559→
   560→        // 7. Detect change point
   561→        self.run_length_probs[0] > self.changepoint_threshold
   562→    }
   563→}
   564→```
   565→
   566→**Parameters**:
   567→
   568→| Parameter | Default | Range | Effect |
   569→|---|---|---|---|
   570→| `hazard_rate` | 1/200 | 1/50 – 1/1000 | Prior on change frequency |
   571→| `max_run_length` | 300 | 50–1000 | Truncation depth (memory vs accuracy) |
   572→| `changepoint_threshold` | 0.5 | 0.3–0.8 | Sensitivity to regime changes |
   573→
   574→**When to recalibrate**: When BOCPD detects a change point, the system should:
   575→1. Reset the CUSUM accumulators to zero
   576→2. Update the EWMA target mean (μ₀) to the post-changepoint EMA
   577→3. Log a regime-change event to `.roko/learn/efficiency.jsonl`
   578→4. Optionally notify the dashboard
   579→
   580→---
   581→
   582→## 12. Multi-Gate Threshold Coordination
   583→
   584→When one gate's behavior changes, should other gates adjust? The answer is yes —
   585→gates are not independent. A compile time increase often precedes test flakiness.
   586→A coverage drop in lint gates correlates with more test failures.
   587→
   588→### 12.1 The Coordination Problem
   589→
   590→Consider: the test gate's pass rate drops from 90% to 60%. The adaptive threshold
   591→increases test retries from 2 to 4. But the *reason* tests are failing is that the
   592→compile gate is letting through code with subtle type errors that the linter would
   593→catch. The correct response isn't more test retries — it's tighter lint enforcement.
   594→
   595→Independent threshold adjustment misses these cross-gate correlations.
   596→
   597→### 12.2 Hotelling's T² for Multi-Gate Anomaly Detection
   598→
   599→```rust
   600→/// Multi-gate anomaly detector using Hotelling's T-squared statistic.
   601→///
   602→/// Monitors the joint distribution of gate metrics (pass rates, durations,
   603→/// scores) across all rungs simultaneously. Detects correlated anomalies
   604→/// that per-gate monitors miss.
   605→pub struct MultiGateDetector {
   606→    /// Number of metrics being tracked (one per gate).
   607→    pub p: usize,
   608→    /// Historical mean vector (one entry per gate's pass rate).
   609→    pub mu: Vec<f64>,
   610→    /// Inverse covariance matrix (p × p).
   611→    /// Captures inter-gate correlations.
   612→    pub sigma_inv: Vec<Vec<f64>>,
   613→    /// Chi-squared critical value for the chosen alpha.
   614→    /// Default alpha=0.01, p=7 gates → threshold ≈ 18.48.
   615→    pub threshold: f64,
   616→    /// Minimum observations before monitoring begins.
   617→    pub warmup_period: u64,   // default: 30
   618→    /// Current observation count.
   619→    pub observation_count: u64,
   620→}
   621→
   622→impl MultiGateDetector {
   623→    /// Feed a new observation vector (one pass rate per gate) and check
   624→    /// for multi-gate anomaly.
   625→    pub fn observe(&mut self, x: &[f64]) -> Option<MultiGateAnomaly> {
   626→        self.observation_count += 1;
   627→        if self.observation_count < self.warmup_period {
   628→            self.update_statistics(x);
   629→            return None;
   630→        }
   631→
   632→        // Compute T² = (x - μ)ᵀ Σ⁻¹ (x - μ)
   633→        let diff: Vec<f64> = x.iter().zip(self.mu.iter())
   634→            .map(|(xi, mi)| xi - mi)
   635→            .collect();
   636→        let t_squared = self.quadratic_form(&diff, &self.sigma_inv);
   637→
   638→        if t_squared > self.threshold {
   639→            // Identify which gate(s) are contributing most to the anomaly
   640→            let contributions = self.per_gate_contributions(&diff);
   641→            Some(MultiGateAnomaly {
   642→                t_squared,
   643→                threshold: self.threshold,
   644→                primary_gates: contributions,
   645→            })
   646→        } else {
   647→            self.update_statistics(x);
   648→            None
   649→        }
   650→    }
   651→}
   652→
   653→pub struct MultiGateAnomaly {
   654→    /// The T² statistic value.
   655→    pub t_squared: f64,
   656→    /// The threshold that was exceeded.
   657→    pub threshold: f64,
   658→    /// Gates ranked by their contribution to the anomaly,
   659→    /// with attribution scores.
   660→    pub primary_gates: Vec<(usize, f64)>,
   661→}
   662→```
   663→
   664→### 12.3 Coordination Policies
   665→
   666→When a multi-gate anomaly is detected, a coordination policy determines the response:
   667→
   668→```rust
   669→pub enum CoordinationPolicy {
   670→    /// Independent: each gate adjusts thresholds independently.
   671→    /// This is the current behavior and the default.
   672→    Independent,
   673→
   674→    /// Sympathetic: when a downstream gate degrades, upstream gates tighten.
   675→    /// Example: test failures → tighten compile/lint gates.
   676→    Sympathetic {
   677→        /// How much to tighten upstream gates when downstream fails.
   678→        /// 0.0 = no tightening, 1.0 = maximum tightening.
   679→        tightening_factor: f64, // default: 0.3
   680→    },
   681→
   682→    /// Compensatory: when one gate relaxes (high pass rate), neighboring
   683→    /// gates tighten to maintain overall verification strength.
   684→    /// Conserves total verification investment.
   685→    Compensatory {
   686→        /// Target aggregate verification score across all gates.
   687→        target_aggregate: f64, // default: 0.85
   688→    },
   689→
   690→    /// Diagnostic: on anomaly, run additional diagnostic gates that are
   691→    /// normally skipped, to identify root cause.
   692→    Diagnostic {
   693→        /// Additional gates to activate on anomaly.
   694→        diagnostic_gates: Vec<Box<dyn Gate>>,
   695→    },
   696→}
   697→```
   698→
   699→### 12.4 Sympathetic Tightening Example
   700→
   701→```
   702→Gate pass rates at T=100:
   703→  Compile: 98%  →  retry budget: 1
   704→  Lint:    90%  →  retry budget: 2
   705→  Test:    85%  →  retry budget: 2
   706→
   707→Gate pass rates at T=120 (test degrades):
   708→  Compile: 97%  →  retry budget: 1
   709→  Lint:    88%  →  retry budget: 2
   710→  Test:    60%  →  retry budget: 4
   711→
   712→Multi-gate anomaly detected (T² = 22.1 > 18.48 threshold).
   713→Primary contributor: Test gate.
   714→
   715→Sympathetic response (tightening_factor=0.3):
   716→  Compile: tighten → enable --all-targets flag, add 1 retry
   717→  Lint:    tighten → enable -D warnings (deny all warnings)
   718→  Test:    increase retries as normal
   719→
   720→Rationale: if tests are failing more, tighter upstream gates catch problems
   721→earlier and cheaper, reducing the load on the expensive test gate.
   722→```
   723→
   724→---
   725→
   726→## 13. Domain-Specific Threshold Profiles
   727→
   728→Different agent roles (code writer, test writer, documentation, infra) have different
   729→gate characteristics. A compile gate that passes 99% of the time for a test-writer
   730→agent might pass only 80% of the time for a complex refactoring agent.
   731→
   732→### 13.1 Profile Structure
   733→
   734→```rust
   735→/// Pre-configured threshold profile for a specific agent role or domain.
   736→pub struct ThresholdProfile {
   737→    /// Profile identifier.
   738→    pub id: String,
   739→    /// Human-readable name.
   740→    pub name: String,
   741→    /// Initial pass rate priors per rung.
   742→    /// Used instead of the neutral 0.5 prior for cold-start.
   743→    pub initial_priors: HashMap<u32, f64>,
   744→    /// Per-rung EMA alpha overrides.
   745→    /// Some domains need faster adaptation (higher α).
   746→    pub alpha_overrides: HashMap<u32, f64>,
   747→    /// Per-rung retry budget overrides [min, max].
   748→    pub retry_bounds: HashMap<u32, (u32, u32)>,
   749→    /// Per-rung skip streak threshold overrides.
   750→    pub skip_thresholds: HashMap<u32, u32>,
   751→    /// CUSUM parameters per rung.
   752→    pub cusum_params: HashMap<u32, CusumParams>,
   753→}
   754→
   755→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   756→pub struct CusumParams {
   757→    pub k: f64,
   758→    pub h: f64,
   759→}
   760→```
   761→
   762→### 13.2 Built-In Profiles
   763→
   764→```rust
   765→/// Profiles for common agent roles.
   766→pub fn profile_for_role(role: &str) -> ThresholdProfile {
   767→    match role {
   768→        "code-writer" => ThresholdProfile {
   769→            name: "Code Writer".into(),
   770→            initial_priors: hashmap! {
   771→                0 => 0.85, // compile: most generated code compiles
   772→                1 => 0.75, // lint: often has warnings
   773→                2 => 0.60, // test: tests frequently break
   774→                3 => 0.90, // symbol: symbol checks are reliable
   775→            },
   776→            alpha_overrides: hashmap! {
   777→                2 => 0.15, // test gate: adapt faster (more volatile)
   778→            },
   779→            retry_bounds: hashmap! {
   780→                2 => (2, 6), // tests: more retries allowed
   781→            },
   782→            ..Default::default()
   783→        },
   784→        "test-writer" => ThresholdProfile {
   785→            name: "Test Writer".into(),
   786→            initial_priors: hashmap! {
   787→                0 => 0.95, // compile: test code almost always compiles
   788→                1 => 0.90, // lint: test code is cleaner
   789→                2 => 0.50, // test: new tests often need iteration
   790→            },
   791→            retry_bounds: hashmap! {
   792→                2 => (3, 8), // tests: expect more iteration
   793→            },
   794→            ..Default::default()
   795→        },
   796→        "refactoring" => ThresholdProfile {
   797→            name: "Refactoring".into(),
   798→            initial_priors: hashmap! {
   799→                0 => 0.70, // compile: refactors break things
   800→                1 => 0.65, // lint: type changes cascade
   801→                2 => 0.55, // test: existing tests may break
   802→                3 => 0.80, // symbol: symbols shift during refactors
   803→            },
   804→            alpha_overrides: hashmap! {
   805→                0 => 0.20, // compile: adapt very fast during refactors
   806→                1 => 0.20,
   807→            },
   808→            cusum_params: hashmap! {
   809→                0 => CusumParams { k: 0.15, h: 3.0 }, // more sensitive
   810→            },
   811→            ..Default::default()
   812→        },
   813→        _ => ThresholdProfile::default(),
   814→    }
   815→}
   816→```
   817→
   818→### 13.3 Profile Selection
   819→
   820→The orchestrator selects a profile based on the task description and plan metadata:
   821→
   822→```
   823→Task: "Refactor auth module to use trait objects"
   824→  → Keywords: "refactor" → profile: "refactoring"
   825→  → Initial compile prior: 0.70 (instead of neutral 0.50)
   826→  → CUSUM sensitivity increased
   827→
   828→Task: "Add unit tests for the parser"
   829→  → Keywords: "test" → profile: "test-writer"
   830→  → Initial test prior: 0.50 (expects iteration)
   831→  → More retries for test gate
   832→```
   833→
