   835→
   836→## 14. Change-Point Detection Integration
   837→
   838→CUSUM, EWMA control charts, and BOCPD work together in a hierarchy:
   839→
   840→```
   841→Per gate observation (pass/fail)
   842→    │
   843→    ├── EMA update (existing) ─── smoothed pass rate
   844→    │
   845→    ├── CUSUM update ─── sustained shift detection
   846→    │    └── shift detected? → adjust retry budget more aggressively
   847→    │
   848→    ├── EWMA control chart ─── formal anomaly detection
   849→    │    └── out of control? → flag gate in dashboard, notify conductor
   850→    │
   851→    └── BOCPD update ─── regime change detection
   852→         └── change point? → recalibrate baselines for all detectors
   853→```
   854→
   855→### 14.1 Offline Batch Analysis with PELT
   856→
   857→For retrospective analysis (e.g., "when did our test reliability degrade?"), the
   858→PELT algorithm finds optimal change points in historical gate data:
   859→
   860→> **Citation**: Killick et al., "Optimal Detection of Changepoints with a Linear
   861→> Computational Cost" (arXiv:1101.1438, 2012).
   862→
   863→```rust
   864→/// Offline change-point detection using PELT (Pruned Exact Linear Time).
   865→///
   866→/// Finds all points where the gate's statistical properties changed.
   867→/// Used for retrospective analysis, not online monitoring.
   868→pub struct PeltDetector {
   869→    /// Cost function for a segment of observations.
   870→    /// Default: negative log-likelihood for Gaussian data.
   871→    pub cost: CostFunction,
   872→    /// Penalty term controlling number of change points.
   873→    /// BIC: p * ln(n), where p = parameters, n = observations.
   874→    /// Higher penalty = fewer change points detected.
   875→    pub penalty: f64,
   876→    /// Minimum segment length between change points.
   877→    pub min_segment: usize,  // default: 5
   878→}
   879→
   880→pub enum CostFunction {
   881→    /// Gaussian negative log-likelihood (for continuous scores).
   882→    Gaussian,
   883→    /// Bernoulli negative log-likelihood (for binary pass/fail).
   884→    Bernoulli,
   885→}
   886→
   887→impl PeltDetector {
   888→    /// Find all change points in a historical sequence.
   889→    ///
   890→    /// Returns indices where the process changed.
   891→    /// Complexity: O(n) expected with pruning.
   892→    pub fn detect(&self, data: &[f64]) -> Vec<usize> {
   893→        let n = data.len();
   894→        let mut f = vec![0.0_f64; n + 1];
   895→        f[0] = -self.penalty;
   896→        let mut cp = vec![Vec::new(); n + 1];
   897→        let mut candidates: Vec<usize> = vec![0];
   898→
   899→        for t in 1..=n {
   900→            let mut best_cost = f64::MAX;
   901→            let mut best_tau = 0;
   902→
   903→            for &tau in &candidates {
   904→                if t - tau < self.min_segment { continue; }
   905→                let segment_cost = self.cost.compute(&data[tau..t]);
   906→                let total = f[tau] + segment_cost + self.penalty;
   907→                if total < best_cost {
   908→                    best_cost = total;
   909→                    best_tau = tau;
   910→                }
   911→            }
   912→
   913→            f[t] = best_cost;
   914→            cp[t] = cp[best_tau].clone();
   915→            cp[t].push(best_tau);
   916→
   917→            // Pruning: remove candidates that can never be optimal
   918→            candidates.retain(|&tau| {
   919→                f[tau] + self.cost.compute(&data[tau..t]) <= f[t]
   920→            });
   921→            candidates.push(t);
   922→        }
   923→
   924→        cp[n].clone()
   925→    }
   926→}
   927→```
   928→
   929→### 14.2 Retrospective Report
   930→
   931→```
   932→PELT analysis of Test gate pass rates (last 500 observations):
   933→
   934→Change points detected at observations: [47, 183, 312]
   935→
   936→Segment 1 (obs 0-47):   mean pass rate 0.92 ± 0.04  [stable, healthy]
   937→Segment 2 (obs 48-183):  mean pass rate 0.71 ± 0.08  [regression, likely caused by auth refactor]
   938→Segment 3 (obs 184-312): mean pass rate 0.88 ± 0.05  [recovery after fix batch]
   939→Segment 4 (obs 313-500): mean pass rate 0.82 ± 0.06  [current regime, moderate]
   940→
   941→Recommendation: current regime is below segment 1 baseline. Consider targeted
   942→testing improvements for the code patterns introduced since observation 312.
   943→```
   944→
   945→---
   946→
   947→## 15. Enhanced RungStats Structure
   948→
   949→The SPC extensions require additional per-rung state:
   950→
   951→```rust
   952→/// Extended per-rung statistics with SPC monitoring.
   953→pub struct RungStatsExtended {
   954→    // --- Existing fields ---
   955→    pub ema_pass_rate: f64,
   956→    pub total_observations: u64,
   957→    pub consecutive_passes: u32,
   958→
   959→    // --- CUSUM ---
   960→    pub cusum: CusumDetector,
   961→
   962→    // --- EWMA control chart ---
   963→    pub ewma_chart: EwmaControlChart,
   964→
   965→    // --- BOCPD ---
   966→    pub bocpd: BocpdDetector,
   967→
   968→    // --- Metadata ---
   969→    /// Timestamp of last observation (for decay calculations).
   970→    pub last_observation_ms: u64,
   971→    /// Number of regime changes detected (lifetime).
   972→    pub regime_changes: u32,
   973→    /// Current regime start observation index.
   974→    pub current_regime_start: u64,
   975→}
   976→
   977→impl RungStatsExtended {
   978→    /// Update all detectors with a new observation.
   979→    pub fn observe(&mut self, passed: bool, timestamp_ms: u64) {
   980→        let value = if passed { 1.0 } else { 0.0 };
   981→
   982→        // Existing EMA update
   983→        self.update_ema(value);
   984→        self.update_consecutive(passed);
   985→
   986→        // SPC updates
   987→        self.cusum.update(value);
   988→        self.ewma_chart.update(value);
   989→        let changepoint = self.bocpd.update(value);
   990→
   991→        // On regime change, recalibrate everything
   992→        if changepoint {
   993→            self.regime_changes += 1;
   994→            self.current_regime_start = self.total_observations;
   995→            self.cusum.reset();
   996→            self.ewma_chart.recalibrate(self.ema_pass_rate);
   997→        }
   998→
   999→        self.last_observation_ms = timestamp_ms;
  1000→        self.total_observations += 1;
  1001→    }
  1002→
  1003→    /// Comprehensive health assessment for this rung.
  1004→    pub fn health(&self) -> RungHealth {
  1005→        RungHealth {
  1006→            pass_rate: self.ema_pass_rate,
  1007→            in_control: self.ewma_chart.is_in_control(),
  1008→            shift_detected: self.cusum.shift_detected,
  1009→            shift_direction: self.cusum.shift_direction,
  1010→            recent_changepoint: self.bocpd.run_length_probs[0]
  1011→                > self.bocpd.changepoint_threshold * 0.5,
  1012→            suggested_retries: self.suggested_max_retries(),
  1013→            should_skip: self.should_skip(),
  1014→        }
  1015→    }
  1016→}
  1017→
  1018→pub struct RungHealth {
  1019→    pub pass_rate: f64,
  1020→    pub in_control: bool,
  1021→    pub shift_detected: bool,
  1022→    pub shift_direction: Option<ShiftDirection>,
  1023→    pub recent_changepoint: bool,
  1024→    pub suggested_retries: u32,
  1025→    pub should_skip: bool,
  1026→}
  1027→```
  1028→
  1029→---
  1030→
  1031→## 16. Test Criteria for SPC Extensions
  1032→
  1033→| Test | Property |
  1034→|---|---|
  1035→| `cusum_detects_sustained_drop` | 20 observations at 90%, then 20 at 70% → shift detected |
  1036→| `cusum_ignores_noise` | Random fluctuations around 80% → no shift signal |
  1037→| `cusum_fir_reset` | After detection, accumulator resets to h/2, can re-detect |
  1038→| `ewma_control_limits_converge` | UCL/LCL stabilize after ~50 observations |
  1039→| `ewma_flags_out_of_control` | Pass rate 90% → suddenly 50% → flagged within 10 obs |
  1040→| `bocpd_detects_regime_change` | 100 obs at 90%, then 100 at 60% → change point at ~100 |
  1041→| `bocpd_no_false_alarm` | Stable 85% pass rate for 500 obs → no change point |
  1042→| `pelt_finds_known_changepoints` | Synthetic data with breaks at [50, 150] → detected ±3 |
  1043→| `multi_gate_detects_correlated_drop` | Two gates degrade together → T² exceeds threshold |
  1044→| `sympathetic_tightening_triggers` | Downstream degradation → upstream retry budget decreases |
  1045→| `profile_cold_start` | New gate with profile prior starts at profile rate, not 0.5 |
  1046→| `regime_change_recalibrates` | BOCPD changepoint → CUSUM reset + EWMA target update |
  1047→

1→# 07 — Process Reward Models
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-learn` (planned integration), `roko-gate` (data source)
     5→> **Status**: Design, informed by active gate infrastructure
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→Process reward models (PRMs) score intermediate reasoning steps rather than only the
    15→final output. In the context of agent-driven development, this means evaluating the
    16→agent's *process* — each tool call, each file edit, each reasoning turn — not just
    17→whether the final code passes gates.
    18→
    19→Standard verification is binary: the code either compiles or it doesn't, tests either
    20→pass or they don't. Process rewards add granularity: *how much progress did the agent
    21→make toward a working solution?* This matters because:
    22→
    23→- An agent that gets 90% of the way to a working solution before failing is more
    24→  promising than one that makes no progress
    25→- Intermediate progress signals enable early intervention (abandon a failing approach
    26→  before it consumes the full retry budget)
    27→- Process rewards provide 10x richer training signal than final pass/fail outcomes
    28→
    29→> **Citation**: Lightman et al. "Let's Verify Step by Step" (2023) — PRM800K dataset,
    30→> process reward models for mathematical reasoning.
    31→
    32→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/02-five-layers.md — "Process Reward Models: Promise +
    33→> Progress scoring, low Promise → early intervention, negative Progress → re-planning."
    34→
    35→---
    36→
    37→## 2. The Two Dimensions: Promise and Progress
    38→
    39→Roko's process reward model tracks two orthogonal signals per agent execution:
    40→
    41→### 2.1 Promise
    42→
    43→**Promise** estimates how likely the current execution is to eventually succeed, given
    44→the work done so far. It answers: "Is this approach heading somewhere good?"
    45→
    46→Indicators of high Promise:
    47→- The agent is making compile-passing edits (Rung 0 passes)
    48→- The agent's tool calls are targeting the right files
    49→- The edit pattern matches successful historical executions for similar tasks
    50→
    51→Indicators of low Promise:
    52→- The agent is making the same edit repeatedly (loop detection)
    53→- The agent's tool calls are targeting unrelated files
    54→- The compile error count is increasing, not decreasing
    55→
    56→**Intervention**: Low Promise → early termination of the current attempt, before the
    57→full retry budget is consumed. Better to start fresh with a different prompt or model
    58→than to continue down a failing path.
    59→
    60→### 2.2 Progress
    61→
    62→**Progress** measures whether the agent is advancing toward the goal or stalling. It
    63→answers: "Is this attempt doing better than the previous one?"
    64→
    65→Indicators of positive Progress:
    66→- Higher rung reached than the previous attempt (ratchet advancing)
    67→- Fewer compile errors than the previous attempt
    68→- More tests passing than the previous attempt
    69→
    70→Indicators of negative Progress:
    71→- Same or lower rung than the previous attempt
    72→- Same or more compile errors
    73→- Same or fewer tests passing
    74→
    75→**Intervention**: Negative Progress across multiple attempts → re-planning. The current
    76→plan may be fundamentally flawed. Rather than retrying with the same approach, generate
    77→a new plan and start over.
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→## 3. Data Sources for Process Rewards
    82→
    83→The gate infrastructure provides rich data for computing process rewards:
    84→
    85→### 3.1 Per-Turn Tool Call Metadata
    86→
    87→Each agent turn produces a `ToolCallMeta`:
    88→```rust
    89→pub struct ToolCallMeta {
    90→    pub tool_name: String,
    91→    pub duration_ms: u64,
    92→    pub result_tokens: u64,
    93→    pub succeeded: bool,
    94→    pub advanced_task: bool,      // Did this call advance the task?
    95→    pub was_redundant: bool,      // Was this call unnecessary?
    96→    pub error_category: Option<String>,
    97→}
    98→```
    99→
   100→The `advanced_task` flag can be computed post-hoc: if a tool call's output was referenced
   101→in the final solution (the agent used the information), it advanced the task. If not,
   102→it was wasted work.
   103→
   104→> **Citation**: tmp/implementation-plans/modelrouting/12-advanced-patterns.md §K
   105→> (Task 2J.16) — "Per-step rewards provide 10x richer signal than final pass/fail."
   106→
   107→### 3.2 Gate Verdicts with Rung Information
   108→
   109→Each gate pipeline execution produces verdicts with:
   110→- Which rung was reached (highest passing rung)
   111→- Test counts (passed/failed/ignored) — a proxy for "how close to passing"
   112→- Error digest — what went wrong, machine-parseable
   113→
   114→### 3.3 Diff Analysis
   115→
   116→The `DiffGate`'s analysis provides:
   117→- How many substantive lines were added
   118→- Whether the additions are vacuous (todo!/unimplemented!)
   119→- Whether the code is getting more or less complete
   120→
   121→### 3.4 Ratchet State
   122→
   123→The `GateRatchet` tracks the highest rung passed per plan. Changes in the ratchet state
   124→across attempts reveal whether the agent is making progress:
   125→- Ratchet advances from rung 1 to rung 2: positive Progress
   126→- Ratchet stays at rung 1 after 3 attempts: stalling
   127→- Ratchet would regress from rung 2 to rung 1: negative Progress (blocked by ratchet)
   128→
   129→---
   130→
   131→## 4. The Promise Score Function
   132→
   133→Promise is computed as a weighted combination of signals:
   134→
   135→```
   136→Promise(attempt) = w₁ × rung_fraction
   137→                 + w₂ × test_pass_rate
   138→                 + w₃ × error_trend
   139→                 + w₄ × tool_efficiency
   140→```
   141→
   142→Where:
   143→- `rung_fraction` = highest_rung_passed / total_rungs (0.0 to 1.0)
   144→- `test_pass_rate` = tests_passed / total_tests (0.0 to 1.0, or 0.5 if no test gate)
   145→- `error_trend` = 1.0 if errors decreasing, 0.5 if stable, 0.0 if increasing
   146→- `tool_efficiency` = useful_tool_calls / total_tool_calls
   147→
   148→Default weights: w₁=0.4, w₂=0.3, w₃=0.2, w₄=0.1.
   149→
   150→### Promise Thresholds
   151→
   152→| Promise | Interpretation | Action |
   153→|---|---|---|
   154→| > 0.8 | High — likely to succeed | Continue, possibly reduce retries |
   155→| 0.4–0.8 | Moderate — uncertain | Continue with standard retries |
   156→| 0.2–0.4 | Low — probably failing | Consider early termination |
   157→| < 0.2 | Very low — almost certainly failing | Terminate, try different approach |
   158→
   159→---
   160→
   161→## 5. The Progress Score Function
   162→
   163→Progress compares the current attempt to the previous one:
   164→
   165→```
   166→Progress(attempt_n) = Δrung + Δtest_rate + Δerror_count
   167→```
   168→
   169→Where:
   170→- `Δrung` = (current_rung - previous_rung) / total_rungs
   171→- `Δtest_rate` = current_pass_rate - previous_pass_rate
   172→- `Δerror_count` = (previous_errors - current_errors) / max(previous_errors, 1)
   173→  (positive = improvement)
   174→
   175→### Progress Thresholds
   176→
   177→| Progress | Interpretation | Action |
   178→|---|---|---|
   179→| > 0.1 | Advancing | Continue, approach is working |
   180→| -0.1 to 0.1 | Stalling | Escalate complexity, adjust prompt |
   181→| < -0.1 | Regressing | Stop retrying, re-plan |
   182→
   183→---
   184→
   185→## 6. Integration with the Feedback Loop
   186→
   187→Process rewards create a fast feedback loop within the agent execution cycle:
   188→
   189→```
   190→Agent turn N
   191→    ↓
   192→Gate pipeline
   193→    ↓
   194→Compute Promise(N) and Progress(N)
   195→    ↓
   196→Decision:
   197→  Promise > 0.4 and Progress > -0.1 → continue to turn N+1
   198→  Promise < 0.2 → early termination
   199→  Progress < -0.1 for 3 turns → re-plan
   200→```
   201→
   202→This is a *within-attempt* optimization. It happens faster than the retry loop
   203→(which operates across attempts) and much faster than the escalation mechanism
   204→(which operates across failed attempts).
   205→
   206→The three feedback timescales:
   207→1. **Process reward** (per-turn): Promise/Progress → continue/terminate
   208→2. **Retry loop** (per-attempt): Gate verdict → retry with adjusted prompt
   209→3. **Escalation** (across attempts): Repeated failure → add rungs, re-plan
   210→
   211→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/07-fast-feedback-loops.md — Machine-speed
   212→> evaluation loops: confidence calibration, context attribution, cost-effectiveness.
   213→
   214→---
   215→
   216→## 7. Academic Foundations
   217→
   218→### 7.1 Lightman et al. (2023) — PRM800K
   219→
   220→Showed that process supervision (scoring each step) outperforms outcome supervision
   221→(scoring only the final answer) for mathematical reasoning. Best-of-N selection with
   222→process rewards outperformed majority voting by 8%.
   223→
   224→### 7.2 AgentPRM (arXiv:2502.10325)
   225→
   226→Extended process rewards to agent tool-use settings. Per-step rewards provide 10x richer
   227→signal than final pass/fail. The key insight: not all tool calls contribute equally to
   228→the outcome. Scoring each call identifies which calls are productive vs. wasteful.
   229→
   230→### 7.3 Self-Refine (Madaan et al. 2023)
   231→
   232→Showed that LLMs can improve their own outputs through iterative refinement with
   233→feedback. Process rewards formalize the feedback signal that drives refinement: rather
   234→than generic "try again," the agent gets "your Promise score dropped because error count
   235→increased — focus on reducing errors."
   236→
   237→### 7.4 Reflexion (Shinn et al. 2023)
   238→
   239→Introduced the concept of verbal reinforcement for agents: converting numeric feedback
   240→into natural language that the agent can learn from. Process rewards can be converted
   241→to reflexion-style feedback: "Your last 3 attempts reached Rung 1 but failed at Rung 2.
   242→Test failures are all in the auth module. Focus on auth module tests."
   243→
   244→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/tmp/mori-refactor/06-harness.md — Academic foundations
   245→> section: "Process Reward Models (Lightman et al. 2023 PRM800K), Self-Refine (Madaan
   246→> et al. 2023), Reflexion (Shinn et al. 2023)."
   247→
   248→---
   249→
   250→## 8. Promise + Progress as Cybernetic Signals
   251→
   252→In the Synapse Architecture's cybernetic feedback model, gate verdicts flow back to
   253→Scorer, Router, Composer, and the agent. Process rewards add two new feedback channels:
   254→
   255→| Signal | From | To | Effect |
   256→|---|---|---|---|
   257→| Promise | Gate + ToolCallMeta | Conductor | Early termination on low Promise |
   258→| Progress | Gate (across attempts) | Router | Model re-selection on stalling |
   259→| Progress | Gate (across attempts) | Composer | Prompt adjustment on stalling |
   260→| Promise × Progress | Combined | Policy | Re-plan on persistent low signals |
   261→
   262→This creates a multi-timescale control system:
   263→- **Fast**: Promise per-turn → terminate unproductive attempts
   264→- **Medium**: Progress per-attempt → adjust routing and prompts
   265→- **Slow**: Trend across plans → update model preferences and prompt templates
   266→
   267→> **Citation**: refactoring-prd/01-synapse-architecture.md — Cybernetic feedback loops
   268→> from Gate to Scorer, Router, Composer.
   269→
   270→---
   271→
   272→## 9. Relationship to Predictive Foraging
   273→
   274→Process rewards connect to the predictive foraging system (from the agent-chain
   275→architecture). Before a task starts, the router predicts success probability. After the
   276→task runs, the actual outcome produces a residual (prediction - reality). Process
   277→rewards refine these predictions:
   278→
   279→- If Promise is high but the task fails → the predictor was overconfident
   280→- If Promise is low but the task succeeds → the predictor was underconfident
   281→- Residuals from process rewards calibrate the predictor faster than binary outcomes
   282→
   283→> **Citation**: tmp/implementation-plans/modelrouting/12-advanced-patterns.md §B
   284→> (Tasks 2J.03–2J.04) — Predictive Foraging: prediction → residual → bias correction.
   285→
   286→---
   287→
   288→## 10. Summary
   289→
   290→Process reward models transform binary gate verdicts into a continuous signal of agent
   291→quality. By tracking Promise (is this attempt heading somewhere good?) and Progress (is
   292→the agent improving across attempts?), the system can make finer-grained decisions about
   293→when to continue, when to terminate early, and when to re-plan entirely.
   294→
   295→The data is already there — gate verdicts, tool call metadata, ratchet state, diff
   296→analysis. Process rewards are a *lens* on this data, not a new data collection system.
   297→They turn "passed/failed" into "making progress / stalling / regressing" and enable
   298→interventions that save compute and improve outcomes.
   299→
   300→---
   301→
   302→## 11. Self-Supervised PRM Training from Gate Verdicts
   303→
   304→Roko has a unique advantage over academic PRM systems: it generates its own step-level
   305→training labels. The gate pipeline is a deterministic oracle. Every intermediate artifact
   306→can be verified, producing automated labels without human annotation.
   307→
   308→> **Citation**: Lightman et al. "Let's Verify Step by Step" (arXiv:2305.20050, 2023) —
   309→> PRM800K required 800K human labels. Self-supervised approaches eliminate this cost.
   310→
   311→> **Citation**: "Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation"
   312→> (arXiv:2502.01715, 2025) — compiler-driven step-level rewards for code RL.
   313→
   314→### 11.1 The Self-Supervision Loop
   315→
   316→```
   317→Agent execution trace:
   318→  step_1: read file → artifact_1 (no code change)
   319→  step_2: edit file → artifact_2 (code changed)
   320→  step_3: edit file → artifact_3 (code changed)
   321→  step_4: run tests → artifact_4 (no code change)
   322→  step_5: fix test → artifact_5 (code changed)
   323→
   324→Gate verification of each intermediate artifact:
   325→  artifact_1: N/A (no code change — label inherited from previous)
   326→  artifact_2: compile PASS, test FAIL → partial credit
   327→  artifact_3: compile PASS, test PASS 8/10 → more credit
   328→  artifact_5: compile PASS, test PASS 10/10 → full credit
   329→
   330→Step-level labels:
   331→  step_1: 0.3 (read → neutral, but necessary)
   332→  step_2: 0.5 (compiles but tests fail)
   333→  step_3: 0.7 (more tests pass)
   334→  step_4: 0.3 (information-gathering, no code progress)
   335→  step_5: 1.0 (all tests pass)
   336→```
   337→
   338→### 11.2 Monte Carlo Step-Level Q-Values
   339→
   340→For richer labels than binary gate outcomes, use Monte Carlo rollouts to estimate the
   341→probability that a step leads to eventual success:
   342→
   343→```rust
   344→/// Monte Carlo estimator for step-level quality values.
   345→///
   346→/// For each intermediate step, estimate the probability that continuing
   347→/// from that state leads to eventual gate passage. This is the Q-value
   348→/// of the step under the current policy.
   349→pub struct MonteCarloStepLabeler {
   350→    /// Number of rollouts per step (more = better estimate, higher cost).
   351→    pub num_rollouts: usize,    // default: 8
   352→    /// Maximum additional turns per rollout.
   353→    pub max_rollout_turns: usize, // default: 10
   354→    /// Gate pipeline to evaluate rollout outcomes.
   355→    pub gate_pipeline: GatePipeline,
   356→    /// Agent backend for generating rollout continuations.
   357→    pub agent: Box<dyn AgentBackend>,
   358→}
   359→
   360→impl MonteCarloStepLabeler {
   361→    /// Estimate the Q-value of reaching this intermediate state.
   362→    ///
   363→    /// Pseudocode:
   364→    ///   successes = 0
   365→    ///   for k in 0..num_rollouts:
   366→    ///       continuation = agent.continue_from(state, max_turns=max_rollout_turns)
   367→    ///       verdict = gate_pipeline.verify(continuation.artifact)
   368→    ///       if verdict.passed:
   369→    ///           successes += 1
   370→    ///   q_value = successes / num_rollouts
   371→    ///   label = "correct" if q_value > 0.5 else "incorrect"
   372→    pub async fn estimate_q_value(&self, state: &IntermediateState) -> StepLabel {
   373→        let mut successes = 0;
   374→        for _ in 0..self.num_rollouts {
   375→            let continuation = self.agent
   376→                .continue_from(state, self.max_rollout_turns).await;
   377→            let verdict = self.gate_pipeline
   378→                .verify(&continuation.as_signal(), &state.context).await;
   379→            if verdict.passed {
   380→                successes += 1;
   381→            }
   382→        }
   383→        let q_value = successes as f64 / self.num_rollouts as f64;
   384→        StepLabel {
   385→            q_value,
   386→            label: if q_value > 0.5 { StepQuality::Correct } else { StepQuality::Incorrect },
   387→            confidence: wilson_confidence(successes as u64, self.num_rollouts as u64),
   388→        }
   389→    }
   390→}
   391→
   392→pub struct StepLabel {
   393→    pub q_value: f64,
   394→    pub label: StepQuality,
   395→    pub confidence: f64,
   396→}
   397→
   398→pub enum StepQuality {
   399→    Correct,
   400→    Incorrect,
   401→    Ambiguous, // q_value near 0.5
   402→}
   403→```
   404→
   405→**Cost analysis**: With 8 rollouts per step and ~5 code-modifying steps per task, this
   406→requires 40 additional agent turns plus 40 gate evaluations. At Haiku-tier costs (~$0.001
   407→per turn), the total labeling cost is ~$0.04 per task. For 100 tasks, $4 produces
   408→~500 step-level labels — orders of magnitude cheaper than human annotation.
   409→
   410→### 11.3 FoVer: Formally Verified Labels
   411→
   412→For code changes that can be expressed as logical assertions, formal verification
   413→provides perfect step-level labels:
   414→
   415→> **Citation**: Kamoi et al., "FoVer: Generalizable Process Reward Models via Formally
   416→> Verified Training Data" (arXiv:2505.15960, 2025) — Z3 and Isabelle for automatic labels.
   417→
   418→```rust
   419→/// Formally verified step labeling for code changes.
   420→///
   421→/// For steps that modify contracts, invariants, or type-level properties,
   422→/// attempt formal verification of the intermediate state.
   423→pub struct FormalStepLabeler {
   424→    /// Verification backend (Z3, Isabelle, or Prusti for Rust).
   425→    pub verifier: Box<dyn FormalVerifier>,
   426→    /// Maximum verification time per step.
   427→    pub timeout: Duration,  // default: 30s
   428→}
   429→
   430→pub trait FormalVerifier: Send + Sync {
   431→    /// Attempt to verify that the code change preserves stated invariants.
   432→    ///
   433→    /// Returns Verified (label=correct), Counterexample (label=incorrect),
   434→    /// or Timeout/Unknown (no label).
   435→    fn verify(&self, pre_state: &Code, post_state: &Code,
   436→              invariants: &[Invariant]) -> VerificationResult;
   437→}
   438→
   439→pub enum VerificationResult {
   440→    /// Invariants hold — step is correct.
   441→    Verified,
   442→    /// Counterexample found — step introduced a bug.
   443→    Counterexample(String),
   444→    /// Verification timed out or was inconclusive.
   445→    Unknown,
   446→}
   447→```
   448→
   449→FoVer labels are high-confidence but limited to steps where formal specs exist.
   450→In practice, ~10-20% of code changes in a typed language like Rust can be formally
   451→checked (type constraints, trait bounds, lifetime invariants). The rest use Monte Carlo.
   452→
   453→---
   454→
   455→## 12. RLHF Alternatives for Agent Improvement
   456→
   457→Gate verdicts provide a natural reward signal. The question is how to convert
   458→these rewards into improved agent behavior across future tasks.
   459→
   460→### 12.1 DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)
   461→
   462→DPO avoids explicit reward model training by directly optimizing the policy from
   463→preference pairs. For agent verification, preference pairs come from gate outcomes:
   464→
   465→> **Citation**: Rafailov et al., "Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model
   466→> Is Secretly a Reward Model" (NeurIPS 2023, arXiv:2305.18290).
   467→
   468→```rust
   469→/// DPO training pair from gate verdicts.
   470→///
   471→/// When two agents attempt the same task and one passes gates while
   472→/// the other fails, this creates a natural preference pair.
   473→pub struct DpoTrainingPair {
   474→    /// The task specification (shared context).
   475→    pub task_spec: String,
   476→    /// The preferred response (passed all gates).
   477→    pub preferred: AgentTrace,
   478→    /// The dispreferred response (failed gates).
   479→    pub dispreferred: AgentTrace,
   480→    /// Margin: how much better the preferred response was.
   481→    /// Higher margin = stronger training signal.
   482→    pub margin: f64,
   483→}
   484→
   485→/// DPO loss function (for reference — training happens offline):
   486→///
   487→/// L_DPO(θ) = -E[ log σ( β × (
   488→///     log π_θ(y_w|x) / π_ref(y_w|x)
   489→///   - log π_θ(y_l|x) / π_ref(y_l|x)
   490→/// ))]
   491→///
   492→/// where:
   493→///   x = task_spec
   494→///   y_w = preferred trace
   495→///   y_l = dispreferred trace
   496→///   β = temperature (sharpness of reward model)
   497→///   π_ref = reference policy (the untuned model)
   498→
   499→/// Parameters for DPO-derived preference collection.
   500→pub struct DpoConfig {
   501→    /// Temperature parameter controlling reward sharpness.
   502→    /// Lower = more decisive preferences. Default: 0.1.
   503→    pub beta: f64,
   504→    /// Minimum margin between preferred/dispreferred.
   505→    /// Pairs with margin < this are too similar to be useful.
   506→    pub min_margin: f64,          // default: 0.3
   507→    /// Maximum pairs to collect before triggering training.
   508→    pub batch_size: usize,        // default: 128
   509→    /// Storage path for collected pairs.
   510→    pub pairs_path: PathBuf,
   511→}
   512→```
   513→
   514→**Implicit reward extraction**: DPO implicitly defines a reward model:
   515→`r(x, y) = β × log(π_θ(y|x) / π_ref(y|x))`. This can be extracted and used
   516→for the process reward scoring without separate PRM training.
   517→
   518→> **Citation**: "Bootstrapping Language Models with DPO Implicit Rewards" (ICLR 2025)
   519→> — using DPO's implicit reward for self-improvement.
   520→
   521→### 12.2 RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback)
   522→
   523→Instead of human preferences, use a judge model to generate preferences from gate
   524→verdicts and code quality analysis:
   525→
   526→```rust
   527→/// RLAIF configuration for generating AI feedback from gate signals.
   528→pub struct RlaifConfig {
   529→    /// The judge model (e.g., Opus) that evaluates traces.
   530→    pub judge_model: String,
   531→    /// Aspects to evaluate (code quality, efficiency, correctness).
   532→    pub evaluation_criteria: Vec<EvaluationCriterion>,
   533→    /// Whether to include gate verdicts as context for the judge.
   534→    pub include_gate_context: bool,  // default: true
   535→}
   536→
   537→pub struct EvaluationCriterion {
   538→    pub name: String,
   539→    pub weight: f64,
   540→    pub prompt_template: String,
   541→}
   542→
   543→/// RLAIF feedback generation:
   544→///
   545→/// 1. Collect two traces for the same task (different models or attempts)
   546→/// 2. Show both traces + gate verdicts to the judge model
   547→/// 3. Judge produces: {preferred: "A"|"B", reasoning: "...", confidence: 0.9}
   548→/// 4. Store as DPO training pair with judge's confidence as margin
   549→///
   550→/// This is cheaper than DPO because the judge model doesn't need to
   551→/// generate rollouts — it only evaluates existing traces.
   552→```
   553→
   554→### 12.3 Constitutional AI for Safety Gates
   555→
   556→Safety-related gate failures (e.g., code that introduces security vulnerabilities,
   557→race conditions, or resource leaks) can be addressed with a Constitutional AI approach:
   558→
   559→```rust
   560→/// Constitutional principles for the safety gate.
   561→pub const SAFETY_CONSTITUTION: &[&str] = &[
   562→    "Generated code must not introduce SQL injection, XSS, or command injection.",
   563→    "Generated code must not disable authentication or authorization checks.",
   564→    "Generated code must not expose secrets in logs, error messages, or comments.",
   565→    "Generated code must not introduce race conditions or deadlocks.",
   566→    "Generated code must not create unbounded resource allocation.",
   567→];
   568→
   569→/// Self-critique loop using constitutional principles:
   570→///
   571→/// 1. Agent produces code change
   572→/// 2. Critic (same or different model) evaluates against constitution
   573→/// 3. If violation found: generate revised version that fixes the violation
   574→/// 4. Repeat until clean or max iterations
   575→/// 5. Run gate pipeline on the final version
   576→///
   577→/// The critique step happens BEFORE the gate pipeline, catching safety
   578→/// issues that static gates might miss (gates check syntax/tests, not
   579→/// security semantics).
   580→```
   581→
   582→---
   583→
   584→## 13. Reward Shaping for Intermediate Steps
   585→
   586→Raw gate verdicts are sparse: most intermediate steps produce no verdict (no code
   587→change = nothing to verify). Reward shaping fills the gaps with dense signals that
   588→guide the agent without changing the optimal policy.
   589→
   590→> **Citation**: Ng et al., "Policy Invariance Under Reward Transformations" (ICML 1999)
   591→> — potential-based reward shaping preserves optimal policy.
   592→
   593→### 13.1 Potential-Based Reward Shaping
   594→
   595→```rust
   596→/// Potential function over agent states.
   597→///
   598→/// Maps each intermediate state to a scalar that estimates "how close
   599→/// to passing gates" the agent is. The shaped reward is:
   600→///
   601→///   R'(s, a, s') = R(s, a, s') + γ × Φ(s') - Φ(s)
   602→///
   603→/// where γ = discount factor and Φ = potential function.
   604→/// This preserves the optimal policy (Ng et al. 1999 theorem).
   605→pub struct GatePotential {
   606→    /// Weight for each component of the potential.
   607→    pub weights: PotentialWeights,
   608→}
   609→
   610→pub struct PotentialWeights {
   611→    /// Weight for compilation status (0/1).
   612→    pub compile_weight: f64,      // default: 0.4
   613→    /// Weight for test pass rate [0, 1].
   614→    pub test_rate_weight: f64,    // default: 0.3
   615→    /// Weight for lint cleanliness [0, 1].
   616→    pub lint_weight: f64,         // default: 0.1
   617→    /// Weight for code completeness (1 - stub_fraction) [0, 1].
   618→    pub completeness_weight: f64, // default: 0.2
   619→}
   620→
   621→impl GatePotential {
   622→    /// Compute the potential of an intermediate state.
   623→    ///
   624→    /// Higher potential = closer to passing all gates.
   625→    pub fn phi(&self, state: &IntermediateState) -> f64 {
   626→        let compile = if state.compiles { 1.0 } else { 0.0 };
   627→        let test_rate = state.tests_passed as f64
   628→            / state.tests_total.max(1) as f64;
   629→        let lint = if state.lint_clean { 1.0 } else { 0.5 };
   630→        let completeness = 1.0 - state.stub_fraction();
   631→
   632→        self.weights.compile_weight * compile
   633→            + self.weights.test_rate_weight * test_rate
   634→            + self.weights.lint_weight * lint
   635→            + self.weights.completeness_weight * completeness
   636→    }
   637→
   638→    /// Compute the shaped reward for a state transition.
   639→    ///
   640→    /// Positive when the agent moves toward passing gates.
   641→    /// Negative when the agent moves away.
   642→    /// Zero when no progress (encourages efficiency).
   643→    pub fn shaped_reward(&self, prev: &IntermediateState,
   644→                         next: &IntermediateState,
   645→                         discount: f64) -> f64 {
   646→        discount * self.phi(next) - self.phi(prev)
   647→    }
   648→}
   649→```
   650→
   651→### 13.2 Shaping Signal Interpretation
   652→
   653→```
   654→Step: agent adds a use statement (fixes compile error)
   655→  Φ(prev) = 0.0 (doesn't compile)
   656→  Φ(next) = 0.4 (compiles, no tests pass yet)
   657→  Shaped reward: 0.99 × 0.4 - 0.0 = +0.396 (positive: progress)
   658→
   659→Step: agent deletes a test (makes test suite pass vacuously)
   660→  Φ(prev) = 0.7 (compiles, 7/10 tests pass)
   661→  Φ(next) = 0.6 (compiles, 7/7 tests pass but completeness drops)
   662→  Shaped reward: 0.99 × 0.6 - 0.7 = -0.106 (negative: regression)
   663→
   664→Step: agent reads a file (no code change)
   665→  Φ(prev) = 0.5
   666→  Φ(next) = 0.5 (unchanged)
   667→  Shaped reward: 0.99 × 0.5 - 0.5 = -0.005 (near zero: encourages efficiency)
   668→```
   669→
   670→The potential-based shaping naturally penalizes vacuous changes (deleting tests to
   671→pass) and rewards genuine progress (fixing errors to pass), without any hand-coded
   672→rules for these behaviors.
   673→
   674→### 13.3 Dense Reward Schedule
   675→
   676→Combining sparse gate rewards with shaped potential rewards:
   677→
   678→```rust
   679→/// Complete per-step reward computation.
   680→pub struct StepRewardComputer {
   681→    pub gate_potential: GatePotential,
   682→    pub discount: f64,               // default: 0.99
   683→    pub gate_weight: f64,            // default: 1.0 (sparse gate reward weight)
   684→    pub shaping_weight: f64,         // default: 0.5 (shaped reward weight)
   685→}
   686→
   687→impl StepRewardComputer {
   688→    pub fn compute(&self, prev: &IntermediateState,
   689→                   next: &IntermediateState,
   690→                   gate_verdict: Option<&Verdict>) -> f64 {
   691→        // Sparse gate reward (only present on code-modifying steps)
   692→        let gate_reward = gate_verdict
   693→            .map(|v| if v.passed { 1.0 } else { -0.5 })
   694→            .unwrap_or(0.0);
   695→
   696→        // Dense shaped reward (every step)
   697→        let shaped = self.gate_potential.shaped_reward(prev, next, self.discount);
   698→
   699→        self.gate_weight * gate_reward + self.shaping_weight * shaped
   700→    }
   701→}
   702→```
   703→
   704→---
   705→
   706→## 14. ThinkPRM: Generative Process Verification
   707→
   708→Rather than training a discriminative PRM (which requires labeled data), use a
   709→generative approach: ask a reasoning model to verify each step by thinking through it.
   710→
   711→> **Citation**: Mukhal et al., "Process Reward Models That Think" (arXiv:2504.16828,
   712→> 2025) — ThinkPRM fine-tuned on 1K synthetic CoTs outperforms discriminative PRMs
   713→> using 1% of typical annotation cost.
   714→
   715→```rust
   716→/// ThinkPRM: a generative process verifier that reasons about step correctness.
   717→///
   718→/// Instead of learning a classifier, this uses chain-of-thought reasoning
   719→/// to verify each step. The model thinks through "does this step make
   720→/// sense given the task and previous steps?" and outputs a verdict.
   721→pub struct ThinkPrm {
   722→    /// The reasoning model used for verification.
   723→    /// Prefer a capable model (Sonnet+) for reliable reasoning.
   724→    pub model: String,
   725→    /// Maximum reasoning tokens per step verification.
   726→    pub max_reasoning_tokens: usize,  // default: 1024
   727→    /// Score threshold for marking a step as incorrect.
   728→    pub threshold: f64,               // default: 0.5
   729→}
   730→
   731→impl ThinkPrm {
   732→    /// Verify a single step in context.
   733→    ///
   734→    /// Prompt structure:
   735→    ///   "You are verifying step {i} of an agent's execution.
   736→    ///    Task: {task_description}
   737→    ///    Previous steps: {step_1..step_{i-1}}
   738→    ///    Current step: {step_i}
   739→    ///    Gate results so far: {gate_verdicts}
   740→    ///
   741→    ///    Think step by step about whether this step is:
   742→    ///    1. Moving toward solving the task
   743→    ///    2. Consistent with previous steps
   744→    ///    3. Likely to lead to gate passage
   745→    ///
   746→    ///    Score the step from 0.0 (definitely wrong) to 1.0 (definitely correct)."
   747→    ///
   748→    /// Returns the model's step score and reasoning chain.
   749→    pub async fn verify_step(&self, step: &Step,
   750→                              context: &StepContext) -> StepVerification {
   751→        // ... prompt construction and model call
   752→        todo!()
   753→    }
   754→}
   755→
   756→pub struct StepVerification {
   757→    pub step_index: usize,
   758→    pub score: f64,
   759→    pub reasoning: String,
   760→    pub is_correct: bool,
   761→    pub verification_tokens: usize,
   762→}
   763→```
   764→
   765→**Cost-effectiveness**: ThinkPRM requires no training data — it uses the model's
   766→reasoning ability directly. At ~1K tokens per step verification and ~5 steps per task,
   767→the cost is ~5K tokens per task (~$0.075 at Sonnet-tier). This is 10x cheaper than
   768→Monte Carlo rollouts and provides natural-language explanations of why a step is good
   769→or bad.
   770→
   771→---
   772→
   773→## 15. Integration Architecture
   774→
   775→All PRM components connect into a unified step-level scoring pipeline:
   776→
   777→```
   778→Agent step N
   779→    │
   780→    ├── Gate verdict (sparse, binary) ──────────────────────┐
   781→    │                                                        │
   782→    ├── Potential-based shaping (dense, continuous) ─────────┤
   783→    │                                                        │
   784→    ├── ThinkPRM verification (generative, reasoning) ──────┤
   785→    │                                                        ▼
   786→    │                                              StepRewardComputer
   787→    │                                                        │
   788→    │                                              Combined step score
   789→    │                                                        │
   790→    ├── If score > 0.4 AND Progress > -0.1 ──► Continue
   791→    ├── If score < 0.2 ────────────────────► Early termination
   792→    ├── If negative Progress × 3 turns ────► Re-plan
   793→    └── Accumulate for DPO pair collection
   794→```
   795→
   796→### 15.1 Persistence
   797→
   798→Step-level rewards are persisted alongside episodes:
   799→
   800→```
   801→.roko/learn/
   802→├── episodes.jsonl              # raw agent traces
   803→├── step-rewards.jsonl          # per-step reward annotations
   804→│   {"episode_id": "...", "step": 3, "gate_reward": 0.0,
   805→│    "shaped_reward": 0.15, "think_score": 0.72, "combined": 0.44}
   806→├── dpo-pairs.jsonl             # collected preference pairs
   807→│   {"task": "...", "preferred": "ep_123", "dispreferred": "ep_124",
   808→│    "margin": 0.6}
   809→└── prm-metrics.json            # PRM calibration metrics
   810→    {"accuracy": 0.83, "calibration_error": 0.04, "samples": 1200}
   811→```
   812→
   813→---
   814→
   815→## 16. Test Criteria
   816→
   817→| Test | Property |
   818→|---|---|
   819→| `monte_carlo_q_value_correct` | All-passing rollouts → q_value ≈ 1.0 |
   820→| `monte_carlo_q_value_failing` | All-failing rollouts → q_value ≈ 0.0 |
   821→| `potential_compile_fix_positive` | Fixing compile error → positive shaped reward |
   822→| `potential_delete_test_negative` | Deleting tests to pass → negative shaped reward |
   823→| `potential_no_change_near_zero` | Read-only step → shaped reward ≈ 0 |
   824→| `dpo_pair_minimum_margin` | Pairs with margin < 0.3 are filtered out |
   825→| `shaped_reward_preserves_policy` | Total shaped reward over optimal path = 0 (Ng theorem) |
   826→| `step_reward_combines_sources` | gate_weight × gate + shaping_weight × shaped = correct |
   827→| `think_prm_score_bounds` | Score always in [0.0, 1.0] |
   828→| `early_termination_on_low_promise` | Promise < 0.2 for 2 turns → termination signal |
   829→| `replan_on_negative_progress` | Progress < -0.1 for 3 turns → replan signal |
   830→

Search the web for cutting-edge 2024-2025 research papers on testing strategies for:

1. Self-improving AI agent systems - how to test agents that modify themselves
2. Property-based testing for cognitive architectures
3. Metamorphic testing for ML/AI systems
4. Adversarial testing and red-teaming for autonomous agents
5. Regression testing strategies for evolving AI systems
6. Performance benchmarking for agent orchestration systems
7. Safety testing frameworks for autonomous software agents

For each topic, find specific paper titles, authors, years, and key techniques. Focus on papers from 2024-2025. Return paper citations and key testing techniques described.

Do NOT write any code or edit any files. This is research only.

content.content:
1→# 08 — Agent Feedback from Gates
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification → L2 Engine (feedback channel)
     4→> **Crate**: `roko-gate` (`crates/roko-gate/src/feedback.rs`)
     5→> **Status**: Implemented (375 lines)
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→Raw gate output — compiler stderr, test logs, linter JSON — is verbose and full of
    15→noise. Progress bars, download messages, repeated blank lines, and Cargo metadata all
    16→waste agent context tokens without contributing actionable information. The feedback
    17→module solves this: it parses raw output into structured `GateFeedback` containing only
    18→actionable items classified by severity.
    19→
    20→This is the bridge between the Harness layer (L3, where gates run) and the agent's
    21→context window. It ensures that when an agent retries after a gate failure, it sees
    22→exactly the errors and warnings it needs to fix — and nothing else.
    23→
    24→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/feedback.rs — Full implementation.
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## 2. The GateFeedback Type
    29→
    30→```rust
    31→pub struct GateFeedback {
    32→    pub rung: u8,                  // Which rung produced this feedback
    33→    pub passed: bool,              // Whether the gate passed
    34→    pub errors: Vec<String>,       // Error-level items (must fix)
    35→    pub warnings: Vec<String>,     // Warning-level items (should fix)
    36→    pub suggestions: Vec<String>,  // Informational/help items
    37→}
    38→```
    39→
    40→Three severity buckets, ordered from most to least critical:
    41→- **errors**: Compilation errors, test failures, panics. The agent *must* fix these.
    42→- **warnings**: Unused variables, deprecated usage, style issues. The agent *should*
    43→  fix these.
    44→- **suggestions**: Compiler help messages, notes, file location pointers. These *inform*
    45→  the agent about what to do.
    46→
    47→### Helper Methods
    48→
    49→```rust
    50→impl GateFeedback {
    51→    pub fn item_count(&self) -> usize;   // Total items across all categories
    52→    pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool;       // True if no actionable items
    53→    pub fn items(&self) -> Vec<FeedbackItem>;  // All items, errors first
    54→}
    55→```
    56→
    57→The `items()` method returns all feedback as `FeedbackItem` structs (severity + message),
    58→ordered: errors first, then warnings, then suggestions. This ordering ensures the most
    59→critical information appears first in the agent's context window.
    60→
    61→---
    62→
    63→## 3. The Classification Pipeline
    64→
    65→### 3.1 Per-Line Classification
    66→
    67→```rust
    68→fn classify_line(line: &str) -> Option<(Severity, &str)>
    69→```
    70→
    71→Each line of raw output is classified into a severity or `None` (noise). The classifier
    72→is a priority chain:
    73→
    74→1. **Empty/whitespace**: → `None`
    75→2. **Noise patterns**: → `None`
    76→3. **Error patterns**: → `Some(Severity::Error, line)`
    77→4. **Warning patterns**: → `Some(Severity::Warning, line)`
    78→5. **Suggestion patterns**: → `Some(Severity::Info, line)`
    79→6. **Anything else**: → `None` (dropped as context)
    80→
    81→### 3.2 Noise Detection
    82→
    83→```rust
    84→fn is_noise(line: &str) -> bool
    85→```
    86→
    87→Lines that are pure noise — no actionable information:
    88→
    89→| Pattern | Example |
    90→|---|---|
    91→| Cargo progress | `Downloading`, `Downloaded`, `Compiling`, `Checking`, `Finished`, `Running`, `Documenting`, `Fresh`, `Packaging` |
    92→| npm deprecation | `npm WARN deprecated stable@0.1.0: deprecated package` |
    93→| Progress bars | Lines containing `━`, `▓`, `░` |
    94→
    95→These lines are common in build output and contribute nothing to error diagnosis.
    96→
    97→### 3.3 Error Detection
    98→
    99→```rust
   100→fn is_error_line(line: &str) -> bool
   101→```
   102→
   103→Lines that indicate errors:
   104→
   105→| Pattern | What It Catches |
   106→|---|---|
   107→| `error` (starts with) | Rust `error:` messages |
   108→| `Error:` (starts with) | Generic error format |
   109→| `ERROR:` (starts with) | Uppercase error format |
   110→| `FAILED` (starts with) | Test failure markers |
   111→| `FAIL ` (starts with) | Go test failure |
   112→| Contains `error[E` | Rustc error codes like `error[E0425]` |
   113→| Contains `panicked at` | Panic messages |
   114→| `thread '...' panicked` | Thread panic with test name |
   115→
   116→### 3.4 Warning Detection
   117→
   118→```rust
   119→fn is_warning_line(line: &str) -> bool
   120→```
   121→
   122→| Pattern | What It Catches |
   123→|---|---|
   124→| `warning` (starts with) | Rust warnings |
   125→| `Warning:` (starts with) | Generic warning format |
   126→| `WARNING:` (starts with) | Uppercase warning format |
   127→| `warn[` (starts with) | Clippy warning codes |
   128→
   129→### 3.5 Suggestion Detection
   130→
   131→```rust
   132→fn is_suggestion_line(line: &str) -> bool
   133→```
   134→
   135→| Pattern | What It Catches |
   136→|---|---|
   137→| `help:` (starts with) | Compiler help messages |
   138→| Contains `= help:` | Inline help annotations |
   139→| `note:` (starts with) | Compiler notes |
   140→| Contains `= note:` | Inline note annotations |
   141→| `suggestion:` (starts with) | Explicit suggestions |
   142→| `hint:` (starts with) | Hint messages |
   143→| `-->` (starts with or contains) | Source location pointers |
   144→
   145→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/feedback.rs:99–192 — Classification functions.
   146→
   147→---
   148→
   149→## 4. The Public API
   150→
   151→```rust
   152→pub fn feedback_for_agent(gate_output: &str, rung: u8) -> GateFeedback
   153→```
   154→
   155→The main entry point. Takes raw gate output (typically stdout + stderr concatenated) and
   156→a rung number, returns structured feedback.
   157→
   158→### Algorithm
   159→
   160→```
   161→for each line in gate_output:
   162→    classify_line(line)
   163→    match severity:
   164→        Error   → push to errors, set has_errors = true
   165→        Warning → push to warnings
   166→        Info    → push to suggestions
   167→        None    → skip (noise)
   168→
   169→passed = !has_errors
   170→return GateFeedback { rung, passed, errors, warnings, suggestions }
   171→```
   172→
   173→### Pass Detection
   174→
   175→The feedback's `passed` field is based purely on whether any error-level items were
   176→found. If there are warnings but no errors, the feedback says `passed = true`. This
   177→aligns with the convention that warnings don't block compilation or test execution.
   178→
   179→---
   180→
   181→## 5. How the Orchestrator Uses Feedback
   182→
   183→After a gate failure, the orchestrator generates feedback and injects it into the
   184→agent's retry prompt:
   185→
   186→```rust
   187→let verdict = pipeline.verify(signal, ctx).await;
   188→
   189→if !verdict.passed {
   190→    let feedback = feedback_for_agent(
   191→        verdict.detail.as_deref().unwrap_or(""),
   192→        current_rung,
   193→    );
   194→
   195→    // Inject into retry prompt
   196→    let retry_context = format!(
   197→        "Your previous attempt failed at rung {}.\n\
   198→         Errors ({}):\n{}\n\
   199→         Warnings ({}):\n{}\n\
   200→         Suggestions ({}):\n{}",
   201→        feedback.rung,
   202→        feedback.errors.len(),
   203→        feedback.errors.join("\n"),
   204→        feedback.warnings.len(),
   205→        feedback.warnings.join("\n"),
   206→        feedback.suggestions.len(),
   207→        feedback.suggestions.join("\n"),
   208→    );
   209→    // ... feed retry_context to the agent
   210→}
   211→```
   212→
   213→This pattern:
   214→1. Extracts only actionable information from potentially thousands of lines of output
   215→2. Categorizes by severity so the agent knows what to prioritize
   216→3. Preserves source location pointers (the `-->` lines) so the agent knows *where*
   217→   to fix
   218→
   219→---
   220→
   221→## 6. Token Economy
   222→
   223→The feedback module is a critical part of Roko's token economy. Consider a typical
   224→`cargo check` failure:
   225→
   226→| Raw output | ~2,000 lines |
   227→|---|---|
   228→| Noise (Downloading, Compiling, etc.) | ~1,500 lines |
   229→| Errors | ~10 lines |
   230→| Warnings | ~20 lines |
   231→| Suggestions | ~15 lines |
   232→| **Filtered feedback** | **~45 lines** |
   233→
   234→That's a 97.75% reduction. At ~4 tokens per line, the feedback saves ~7,800 tokens per
   235→gate failure. Over a 5-attempt retry loop with 3 gate failures, that's ~23,400 tokens
   236→saved — a significant fraction of the agent's context window.
   237→
   238→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/tmp/mori-refactor/06-harness.md — "Raw gate output
   239→> (compiler stderr, test logs, linter JSON) is verbose and full of noise that wastes
   240→> agent context tokens."
   241→
   242→---
   243→
   244→## 7. Gate-to-Scaffold Feedback Loop
   245→
   246→The feedback module is one half of a closed loop. The other half is the section
   247→effectiveness tracker (see the Scaffold layer documentation and implementation plan
   248→task 2J.05–2J.06):
   249→
   250→```
   251→Agent receives prompt (with sections)
   252→    ↓
   253→Agent produces code
   254→    ↓
   255→Gate verifies code → Verdict
   256→    ↓
   257→feedback_for_agent() → GateFeedback
   258→    ↓
   259→Two consumers:
   260→    1. Agent retry prompt (immediate)
   261→    2. SectionEffectivenessRegistry (learning)
   262→       → Which prompt sections correlated with gate success?
   263→       → Adjust section priorities for future prompts
   264→```
   265→
   266→The immediate feedback (inject errors into retry prompt) operates at machine speed.
   267→The section effectiveness learning operates at consolidation speed — it needs 50+
   268→observations to make statistical claims about which prompt sections help.
   269→
   270→> **Citation**: tmp/implementation-plans/modelrouting/12-advanced-patterns.md §C
   271→> (Tasks 2J.05–2J.06) — Gate-to-scaffold feedback loop, section effectiveness tracking
   272→> with lift > 0.05.
   273→
   274→---
   275→
   276→## 8. Severity Ordering
   277→
   278→```rust
   279→pub enum Severity {
   280→    Info,       // 0
   281→    Warning,    // 1
   282→    Error,      // 2
   283→}
   284→```
   285→
   286→`Severity` derives `PartialOrd` and `Ord`, with `Info < Warning < Error`. This enables:
   287→- Sorting feedback items by severity (`items()` returns errors first)
   288→- Filtering by minimum severity (e.g., "only show errors and warnings")
   289→- Threshold-based policies (e.g., "fail if any Error, warn if any Warning")
   290→
   291→---
   292→
   293→## 9. Serde Support
   294→
   295→Both `GateFeedback` and `FeedbackItem` derive `Serialize` and `Deserialize`. This
   296→enables:
   297→- Persisting feedback to the episode log (`.roko/episodes.jsonl`)
   298→- Transmitting feedback as JSON to agents that expect structured input
   299→- Aggregating feedback across executions for learning
   300→
   301→The serde roundtrip test in the module verifies that serialization and deserialization
   302→preserve all fields exactly.
   303→
   304→---
   305→
   306→## 10. Limitations and Future Work
   307→
   308→### 10.1 Language-Specific Heuristics
   309→
   310→The current classifier is biased toward Rust/Cargo output. The patterns for npm, Go,
   311→and other build systems are minimal. Future work should add per-language classifiers:
   312→
   313→```rust
   314→fn classify_line_for_build_system(line: &str, build: BuildSystem) -> Option<(Severity, &str)>
   315→```
   316→
   317→### 10.2 Multi-Line Error Messages
   318→
   319→Rustc error messages span multiple lines (the error, the source snippet, the help
   320→message). The current classifier treats each line independently, which means:
   321→- The error line is classified as Error
   322→- The source snippet line is classified as noise (dropped)
   323→- The help line is classified as Info
   324→
   325→This loses the visual structure of the error. A future improvement would group
   326→consecutive lines belonging to the same diagnostic into a single `FeedbackItem`.
   327→
   328→### 10.3 Structured Error Formats
   329→
   330→Some tools emit structured output (JSON, SARIF). The current classifier works on
   331→line-by-line text. Future work should detect and parse structured formats:
   332→
   333→```
   334→if gate_output starts with "[" or "{":
   335→    parse as JSON diagnostic array
   336→else:
   337→    use line-by-line classifier
   338→```
   339→
   340→---
   341→
   342→## 11. Testing
   343→
   344→The feedback module has 14 tests covering:
   345→
   346→| Test | What It Verifies |
   347→|---|---|
   348→| `feedback_empty_output_passes` | Empty input → passed, no items |
   349→| `feedback_extracts_errors` | Error lines extracted correctly |
   350→| `feedback_extracts_warnings` | Warning lines extracted correctly |
   351→| `feedback_extracts_suggestions` | Help/note lines extracted correctly |
   352→| `feedback_filters_noise` | Cargo progress lines dropped |
   353→| `feedback_mixed_output` | Mixed output classified correctly |
   354→| `feedback_item_count` | Total count across categories |
   355→| `feedback_items_ordering` | Errors first, then warnings, then suggestions |
   356→| `feedback_rung_preserved` | Rung number roundtrips correctly |
   357→| `feedback_test_failure_detected` | FAILED/panicked lines are errors |
   358→| `feedback_severity_ordering` | Info < Warning < Error |
   359→| `feedback_npm_deprecation_is_noise` | npm-specific noise detected |
   360→| `feedback_progress_bars_are_noise` | Unicode progress bars detected |
   361→| `feedback_serde_roundtrip` | JSON serialization preserves all fields |
   362→
   363→> **Citation**: crates/roko-gate/src/feedback.rs:241–374 — Tests section.

1→# 09 — Evaluation Lifecycle
     2→
     3→> **Layer**: L3 Harness — Verification
     4→> **Crates**: `roko-gate`, `roko-learn`, `roko-conductor`
     5→> **Status**: Partial (fast loops wired, slow loops designed)
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Shipping
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Overview
    13→
    14→Evaluation in Roko is not a single step — it is a lifecycle that spans five speed tiers,
    15→from sub-second machine checks to multi-day retrospective analysis. The gate pipeline is
    16→the fastest tier. The evaluation lifecycle describes how gate verdicts compound, combine
    17→with other signals, and drive progressive improvement across time.
    18→
    19→The lifecycle has 14 feedback loops organized across 5 speed tiers. Each loop composes
    20→with the others — the output of fast loops feeds into slower loops, and the insights
    21→from slow loops adjust the parameters of fast loops.
    22→
    23→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/09-evaluation-map.md — "14 feedback loops
    24→> across 5 speed tiers, complete composition diagram."
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## 2. The Five Speed Tiers
    29→
    30→| Tier | Speed | Loops | What Runs Here |
    31→|---|---|---|---|
    32→| Machine speed | Sub-second to seconds | 5 | Confidence calibration, context attribution, cost-effectiveness, tool selection, adversarial awareness |
    33→| Cognitive speed | Seconds to minutes | 3 | Gate pipeline, error diagnosis, retry logic |
    34→| Consolidation speed | Minutes to hours | 3 | Skill extraction, pattern discovery, model calibration |
    35→| Retrospective speed | Hours to days | 2 | Shadow testing, reasoning quality review |
    36→| Meta speed | Days to weeks | 1 | Meta-learning evaluation |
    37→
    38→### 2.1 Machine Speed (5 Loops)
    39→
    40→These run within or immediately after a single agent turn:
    41→
    42→**Loop 1: Confidence Calibration**
    43→The agent (or router) predicts success probability before the gate runs. After the gate
    44→runs, the prediction is compared to the outcome. Residuals accumulate, enabling
    45→calibration correction.
    46→
    47→Metric: Expected Calibration Error (ECE) — the average gap between predicted and actual
    48→pass rates across confidence bins.
    49→
    50→**Loop 2: Context Attribution**
    51→Which parts of the prompt contributed to gate success? The section effectiveness tracker
    52→correlates prompt sections with outcomes.
    53→
    54→**Loop 3: Cost-Effectiveness**
    55→Did the agent's token spend produce proportionate verification results? A 50,000-token
    56→turn that fails all gates is less cost-effective than a 10,000-token turn that passes
    57→all gates.
    58→
    59→**Loop 4: Tool Selection**
    60→Are the agent's tool call patterns efficient? Redundant file reads, unnecessary edits,
    61→and tool calls that don't advance the task are identified.
    62→
    63→**Loop 5: Adversarial Awareness**
    64→Does the agent detect adversarial inputs (prompt injections, malicious test fixtures)?
    65→This loop monitors the agent's defensive behavior.
    66→
    67→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/07-fast-feedback-loops.md — "5 machine-speed
    68→> evaluation loops: confidence calibration, context attribution, cost-effectiveness, tool
    69→> selection, adversarial awareness."
    70→
    71→### 2.2 Cognitive Speed (3 Loops)
    72→
    73→These run during a single task execution (across multiple turns):
    74→
    75→**Loop 6: Gate Pipeline**
    76→The rung selector → gate pipeline → verdict cycle. This is the core verification loop,
    77→documented in [03-gate-pipeline.md](./03-gate-pipeline.md).
    78→
    79→**Loop 7: Error Diagnosis**
    80→Gate error output is parsed into structured feedback (see
    81→[08-agent-feedback-from-gates.md](./08-agent-feedback-from-gates.md)) and enriched
    82→with cheap-model diagnosis.
    83→
    84→**Loop 8: Retry Logic**
    85→The orchestrator decides whether to retry, escalate, or re-plan based on the verdict
    86→and process reward signals.
    87→
    88→### 2.3 Consolidation Speed (3 Loops)
    89→
    90→These run after a batch of tasks (e.g., after a full plan execution):
    91→
    92→**Loop 9: Skill Extraction**
    93→Successful episodes are analyzed to extract reusable tool-use patterns (see
    94→[11-evoskills.md](./11-evoskills.md)).
    95→
    96→**Loop 10: Pattern Discovery**
    97→Cross-task analysis identifies recurring success/failure patterns. E.g., "tasks that
    98→modify auth modules fail 3x more often than average."
    99→
   100→**Loop 11: Model Calibration**
   101→Aggregate per-model performance data to calibrate the router's bandit arms. Thompson
   102→Sampling parameters are updated based on gate outcomes.
   103→
   104→### 2.4 Retrospective Speed (2 Loops)
   105→
   106→These run on a longer cadence (nightly, weekly):
   107→
   108→**Loop 12: Shadow Testing**
   109→Run the same tasks with different models/prompts in shadow mode and compare outcomes.
   110→This discovers whether the current routing is optimal.
   111→
   112→**Loop 13: Reasoning Quality Review**
   113→Evaluate agent reasoning quality across a batch of completed tasks. Three signals:
   114→alignment (did the agent follow the plan?), consistency (did reasoning stay coherent
   115→across turns?), and annotations (did the agent leave useful comments?).
   116→
   117→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/08-slow-feedback-loops.md — "3 slow loops:
   118→> shadow strategy testing, reasoning quality review, meta-learning evaluation."
   119→
   120→### 2.5 Meta Speed (1 Loop)
   121→
   122→**Loop 14: Meta-Learning Evaluation**
   123→Evaluate the evaluation system itself: are the 13 other loops improving outcomes over
   124→time? This is the system's self-assessment, tracking whether its learning is net
   125→positive.
   126→
   127→---
   128→
   129→## 3. Composition Diagram
   130→
   131→The 14 loops compose hierarchically — faster loops feed data to slower loops:
   132→
   133→```
   134→Machine Speed                 Cognitive Speed
   135→┌─────────────┐              ┌─────────────┐
   136→│ Confidence  │──residuals──→│ Gate         │
   137→│ Calibration │              │ Pipeline     │
   138→└─────────────┘              └──────┬───────┘
   139→┌─────────────┐                     │
   140→│ Context     │──lift────────┐      │ verdicts
   141→│ Attribution │              │      ↓
   142→└─────────────┘              │ ┌────────────┐
   143→┌─────────────┐              │ │ Retry      │
   144→│ Cost-       │──efficiency──│ │ Logic      │
   145→│ Effectiveness│             │ └────────────┘
   146→└─────────────┘              │
   147→┌─────────────┐              │  Consolidation Speed
   148→│ Tool        │──patterns────│  ┌──────────────┐
   149→│ Selection   │              ├─→│ Skill         │
   150→└─────────────┘              │  │ Extraction    │
   151→┌─────────────┐              │  └───────────────┘
   152→│ Adversarial │──alerts──────┤  ┌──────────────┐
   153→│ Awareness   │              ├─→│ Pattern       │
   154→└─────────────┘              │  │ Discovery     │
   155→                             │  └───────────────┘
   156→                             │  ┌──────────────┐
   157→                             └─→│ Model         │
   158→                                │ Calibration   │
   159→                                └───────┬───────┘
   160→                                        │
   161→                Retrospective Speed     │
   162→                ┌──────────────┐        │
   163→                │ Shadow       │←───────┘
   164→                │ Testing      │
   165→                └──────────────┘
   166→                ┌──────────────┐
   167→                │ Reasoning    │
   168→                │ Quality      │
   169→                └──────┬───────┘
   170→                       │
   171→           Meta Speed  │
   172→           ┌───────────┴──┐
   173→           │ Meta-Learning │
   174→           │ Evaluation    │
   175→           └──────────────┘
   176→```
   177→
   178→---
   179→
   180→## 4. The Karpathy Property
   181→
   182→Every loop in the evaluation lifecycle satisfies the Karpathy Property: **if the
   183→evaluation metric improves, the system's end-to-end performance improves**. This is a
   184→design constraint, not an observation. It means:
   185→
   186→- No metric that is uncorrelated with actual task success
   187→- No metric that can be gamed without improving outcomes
   188→- No metric that improves at the expense of another metric
   189→
   190→For gate-based loops, the Karpathy Property is straightforward: if the compile gate pass
   191→rate improves, the system is producing better code. For slower loops (reasoning quality,
   192→shadow testing), the property requires careful metric design.
   193→
   194→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/07-fast-feedback-loops.md — "Karpathy
   195→> autoresearch loop" and Karpathy Property across all loops.
   196→
   197→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/09-evaluation-map.md — "Karpathy property
   198→> across all loops."
   199→
   200→---
   201→
   202→## 5. The Four-Phase Lifecycle
   203→
   204→Beyond the speed tiers, evaluation goes through four phases:
   205→
   206→### Phase 1: Trace Inspection
   207→
   208→Examine individual agent turns: what did the agent do, what tools did it call, what
   209→was the result? This is the raw data layer.
   210→
   211→**Data sources**: Episode log (`.roko/episodes.jsonl`), efficiency events
   212→(`.roko/learn/efficiency.jsonl`).
   213→
   214→### Phase 2: Backtesting
   215→
   216→Replay past executions with different parameters: would a different model have
   217→succeeded? Would a different prompt have helped? Would more/fewer retries have been
   218→optimal?
   219→
   220→**Data sources**: Artifact store (replay exact inputs), gate threshold history.
   221→
   222→### Phase 3: Paper Trading
   223→
   224→Run new configurations in shadow mode alongside production. Compare outcomes without
   225→affecting real task execution.
   226→
   227→**Data sources**: Shadow execution results vs. production results.
   228→
   229→### Phase 4: Canary Deployment
   230→
   231→Gradually roll out proven improvements to a fraction of tasks, monitoring for regressions
   232→before full deployment.
   233→
   234→**Data sources**: A/B experiment results (`.roko/learn/experiments.json`).
   235→
   236→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/05-evaluation-lifecycle.md — "4-phase
   237→> evaluation lifecycle: Trace Inspection → Backtesting → Paper Trading → Canary."
   238→
   239→---
   240→
   241→## 6. The Gauntlet
   242→
   243→The Gauntlet is the benchmark suite that validates the evaluation lifecycle itself:
   244→
   245→| Speed | Duration | Scope |
   246→|---|---|---|
   247→| Smoke | 5 minutes | Core gate pipeline on known test cases |
   248→| Nightly | 2–4 hours | Full rung ladder on real project tasks |
   249→| Full | 24–48 hours | All 14 loops, cross-model comparison |
   250→
   251→The Gauntlet provides confidence that changes to the evaluation system don't regress
   252→evaluation quality. It is the "gate for the gates."
   253→
   254→> **Citation**: bardo-backup/prd/16-testing/01-gauntlet.md — Gauntlet benchmark suite,
   255→> 3 speeds.
   256→
   257→---
   258→
   259→## 7. Gate Verdicts as the Foundation
   260→
   261→Every loop in the evaluation lifecycle is ultimately grounded in gate verdicts. Even the
   262→slowest loop (meta-learning) depends on the aggregate of gate outcomes to measure
   263→whether the system is improving.
   264→
   265→This is why the gate architecture is so important:
   266→- Verdicts are the atomic unit of verification truth
   267→- All 14 loops consume or aggregate verdicts
   268→- The quality of the evaluation lifecycle is bounded by the quality of the gates
   269→
   270→Improving gate fidelity (adding rungs, reducing false negatives, increasing coverage)
   271→has multiplicative effects across all 14 loops. This is another manifestation of the
   272→GVU framework's insight: invest in verification quality.
   273→
   274→> **Citation**: Song et al. (ICLR 2025) — "Self-improvement succeeds when the verifier
   275→> is strong, not when the generator is strong."
   276→
